crumbs: 12  Session 4: The OU Process for Liquidity (Intuition Only)

title: 12  Session 4: The OU Process for Liquidity (Intuition Only)
text:
12.4 Slide Content Briefs (New Slides Needed)

12.4.1 Slide 4.1 — Title

Title: “The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Process for Liquidity”
Subtitle: “Intuition only — the math itself is in Session 25 (Track 2)”
Note: “If today feels easy and you want more depth, consider Track 2”



12.4.2 Slide 4.2 — Why We Need a Process Model

Title: “What we have, what we need”
What we have (from Session 3):

Time series of secondary discounts 2003–2024
Implied liquidity state \(L_t\) at quarterly frequency
Four regimes with distinct statistical character

What we need:

A model of how \(L_t\) evolves
Closed-form properties (mean, variance, distribution)
Ability to forecast and price

Speaker notes: Without a process model, we can describe the past but cannot value future cash flows.



12.4.3 Slide 4.3 — Three Properties Our Model Must Have

Title: “What properties does \(L_t\) have?”
Empirical properties (from data):

Mean reversion — discounts widen in crises but return toward a long-run average
Stochastic shocks — period-to-period changes are partly random
Bounded variance — \(L_t\) doesn’t drift indefinitely; the unconditional distribution has finite variance

Speaker notes: These three properties together rule out Brownian motion (which has unbounded variance) and rule in mean-reverting processes.



12.4.4 Slide 4.4 — Introducing the OU Process

Title: “The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process”
SDE form (large, centered): \(dL_t = \kappa(\bar{L} - L_t)\,dt + \sigma\,dW_t\)
Verbal translation: “\(L_t\) tends to revert toward \(\bar{L}\) at speed \(\kappa\), with random shocks of magnitude \(\sigma\)”
History: “Originally derived by Ornstein and Uhlenbeck in 1930 to model the velocity of a particle undergoing Brownian motion in a viscous fluid. Same math applies to mean-reverting financial variables.”
Speaker notes: Don’t over-explain notation. Students will get used to it through repetition. The verbal translation is more important than the symbols.



12.4.5 Slide 4.5 — What Each Parameter Does (\(\kappa\))

Title: “\(\kappa\) controls mean reversion speed”
Visual: Three OU paths overlaid:

Low \(\kappa\) (= 0.1): slow return to mean, looks almost like a random walk
Calibrated \(\kappa\) (= 0.45): moderate return, typical of liquidity data
High \(\kappa\) (= 2.0): fast return, looks like noise around mean

Half-life formula: “Half-life = \(\ln(2)/\kappa\)”

\(\kappa = 0.1\) → half-life 6.9 years
\(\kappa = 0.45\) → half-life 1.5 years (matches data)
\(\kappa = 2.0\) → half-life 0.35 years

Speaker notes: Connect to a familiar concept: radioactive decay. Same math. Half-life is the time to revert halfway to mean.



12.4.6 Slide 4.6 — What Each Parameter Does (\(\bar{L}\), \(\sigma\))

Title: “\(\bar{L}\) and \(\sigma\)”
\(\bar{L}\) (long-run mean):

The value \(L_t\) reverts toward
In the GE-LAV calibration, \(\bar{L} = 1.0\) by normalization
Economic meaning: the “normal” liquidity state

\(\sigma\) (volatility / diffusion coefficient):

Controls the size of random shocks
Calibrated \(\sigma = 0.32\)
Higher \(\sigma\) → more dispersion around mean

Visual: Two paths overlaid with different \(\sigma\) values
Speaker notes: Students sometimes confuse \(\sigma\) (diffusion) with stationary std (which is different). Set that up for the next slide.



12.4.7 Slide 4.7 — Stationary Distribution

Title: “Where does \(L_t\) end up in the long run?”
Result (boxed): \(L_\infty \sim \mathcal{N}\left(\bar{L}, \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2\kappa}\right)\)
Calibrated values:

Mean: \(\bar{L} = 1.0\)
Variance: \(\sigma^2/(2\kappa) = 0.32^2 / 0.90 = 0.1138\)
Standard deviation: \(\sqrt{0.1138} = 0.337\)

Visual: Histogram of OU path values over a long simulation, overlaid with Gaussian density
Speaker notes: This is the only result this lecture proves (informally). Track 2 students will see the full derivation via Fokker-Planck in Session 27.



12.4.8 Slide 4.8 — What “Stationary” Means

Title: “Stationary = unconditional distribution”
Bullets:

If you pick a random year far in the future, the distribution of \(L_t\) in that year is \(\mathcal{N}(1.0, 0.1138)\)
The transition density (where will I be next year given where I am now) is different — it’s a function of current state
As \(t \to \infty\), transition density → stationary density

Connection to data: “The empirical histogram of \(L_t\) across 2003–2024 is approximately consistent with this stationary density — modulo regime changes.”
Speaker notes: Avoid the word “ergodic” unless asked. Just say “in the long run, OU forgets where it started.”



12.4.9 Slide 4.9 — Path Simulation (Discrete Recursion)

Title: “How to simulate an OU path”
Discretization: Using small time step \(\Delta t\) (e.g., 1 month = 1/12 year):

\(L_{t+\Delta t} = L_t + \kappa(\bar{L} - L_t)\Delta t + \sigma \sqrt{\Delta t} \cdot Z\)
where \(Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) is a random draw

Live demo: In Excel or Python, simulate 100 paths and show their distribution
Speaker notes: This is genuinely useful for students even at intuition level. They should be able to do this in Excel. Track 1 students will need it for PS1.



12.4.10 Slide 4.10 — Connection to Vasicek (For Students Who Know Rate Models)

Title: “If you’ve seen Vasicek, you’ve seen OU”
Vasicek interest rate model: \(dr_t = a(b - r_t)\,dt + \sigma\,dW_t\)
GE-LAV liquidity model: \(dL_t = \kappa(\bar{L} - L_t)\,dt + \sigma\,dW_t\)
Same equation. Only the variable interpretation differs:

Vasicek: \(r_t\) is the short interest rate
GE-LAV: \(L_t\) is the liquidity state

Other examples: Hull-White (interest rates), Heston (volatility), commodity convenience yield models
Speaker notes: This slide helps students with prior fixed income or derivatives knowledge plug into the framework quickly.



12.4.11 Slide 4.11 — What OU Cannot Do

Title: “Limitations of pure OU”
Bullets:

No jumps: OU paths are continuous; real liquidity has discrete shocks (March 2020, GFC)
No fat tails: Stationary distribution is Gaussian; real data has more extreme observations
No regime switching: Single \(\kappa\), \(\bar{L}\), \(\sigma\) across all conditions

Resolution in GE-LAV: “Chapter 13 of the book extends OU to jump-diffusion. We adopt that for advanced work in Session 28+.”
Speaker notes: Honesty about model limitations. Students will appreciate that you’re not selling them perfection.



12.4.12 Slide 4.12 — Track Self-Diagnostic

Title: “How did today feel?”
Three columns:

“Felt easy, I wanted more”: Likely Track 2 candidate. Read Karatzas-Shreve Ch. 5 by Session 5
“Felt right, comfortable level”: Likely Track 1 — focus on application
“Felt hard but I got through”: Stay on Track 1. Session 25 math depth would be a stretch

Speaker notes: Use the last 10 minutes for this. Have students fill out a brief course form indicating their preliminary track choice and any questions they have about the math.

Slide showing connection to Vasicek (keep)

crumbs: 12  Session 4: The OU Process for Liquidity (Intuition Only)

title: 12  Session 4: The OU Process for Liquidity (Intuition Only)
text:
12.6 Discussion Questions

The OU process is Gaussian (stationary distribution is normal). The empirical secondary market discount data has fat tails (more extreme observations than Gaussian predicts). How much does this limitation matter for valuation?
The half-life of liquidity shocks is ~18 months at \(\kappa = 0.45\). What’s the implication for an LP who plans to exit in 12 months vs. 36 months?
Vasicek (1977) introduced the OU process to model interest rates. Why did it take ~50 years for the same framework to be applied to liquidity in private markets?
crumbs: 12  Session 4: The OU Process for Liquidity (Intuition Only)

title: 12  Session 4: The OU Process for Liquidity (Intuition Only)
section: 12.7 Worked Example: OU Path Simulation in Plain English
text:
12.7 Worked Example: OU Path Simulation in Plain English
Setup: Start at \(L_0 = 0.85\) (slightly stressed, like Q3 2024). Use \(\kappa = 0.45\), \(\bar{L} = 1.0\), \(\sigma = 0.32\). Simulate quarterly (\(\Delta t = 0.25\)).
Step 1 (next quarter): Mean reversion pulls toward 1.0: - Drift contribution: \(0.45 \times (1.0 - 0.85) \times 0.25 = 0.017\) - Random shock: \(\sigma \sqrt{\Delta t} \cdot Z = 0.32 \times 0.5 \times Z = 0.16 Z\) - If \(Z = +1\) (one std above): \(L_{0.25} = 0.85 + 0.017 + 0.16 = 1.03\) - If \(Z = -1\): \(L_{0.25} = 0.85 + 0.017 - 0.16 = 0.71\) - If \(Z = 0\): \(L_{0.25} = 0.867\)
Step 2 (second quarter): Repeat. Now starting from a new value.
Step 3 (long-run): After many quarters, the distribution of \(L_t\) converges to \(\mathcal{N}(1.0, 0.1138)\) regardless of starting point.
Teaching note: Have students do one step by hand. This grounds the abstract math in arithmetic they can do in 90 seconds.
crumbs: 12  Session 4: The OU Process for Liquidity (Intuition Only)

title: 12  Session 4: The OU Process for Liquidity (Intuition Only)
text:
12.8 Common Student Confusions (Instructor Notes)

“Why is the stationary variance \(\sigma^2/(2\kappa)\) and not just \(\sigma^2\)?” Because mean reversion fights the random shocks. Faster reversion (larger \(\kappa\)) means smaller stationary variance. This is the most-asked question of the session.
“How do you actually calibrate \(\kappa\), \(\bar{L}\), \(\sigma\) from data?” Tease the answer: it’s maximum likelihood estimation on the time series. Track 1 sees the recipe in Session 17; Track 2 sees the math in Session 25.
“Is the Gaussian assumption realistic?” No — empirical data has fatter tails. Jump-diffusion extension (Ch 13) handles this. For valuation purposes, the bias from Gaussianity is small except in extreme regimes.
“Does \(L_t\) stay positive?” No — under OU, \(L_t\) can go negative. In the model, \(L_t = 0\) represents the long-run mean liquidity (by normalization), and negative values represent stressed regimes. This is just notational; it’s not a constraint violation.
crumbs: 12  Session 4: The OU Process for Liquidity (Intuition Only)

title: 12  Session 4: The OU Process for Liquidity (Intuition Only)
text:
12.9 What to Expect Next Session
Session 5 covers the term structure of private capital returns — how the OU dynamics interact with investment horizon to produce horizon-dependent valuation effects. Key topics:

The empirical term structure of PE returns by vintage and asset class
How Jensen bias grows with \(T\)
Why long-horizon assets (infrastructure, core real estate) are most affected by the liquidity illusion
DLOM (Discount for Lack of Marketability) vs. GE-LAV — comparing methodologies

Reading: Book Chapter 3, all sections (~25 pages).
Track declaration: End of Session 5 is the track selection deadline. If you’re undecided, schedule a 15-min meeting with me this week.

crumbs: 13  Session 5: Term Structure of Private Capital Returns

title: 13  Session 5: Term Structure of Private Capital Returns
text:
13.4 Slide Content Briefs (New Slides Needed)

13.4.1 Slide 5.1 — Title

Title: “The Term Structure of Private Capital Returns”
Tag line: “Why long-horizon assets are most affected by the liquidity illusion”
Reminder banner: “Track declaration deadline today”



13.4.2 Slide 5.2 — What Is a Term Structure?

Title: “Term structures everyone knows”
Examples:

Bond yield curve: yield as a function of maturity (1Y, 2Y, 5Y, 10Y, 30Y)
Volatility term structure: implied vol as a function of option maturity
Credit spread term structure: spread by tenor

New idea: “What does the risk of private capital look like as a function of holding period?”
Speaker notes: Connect to fixed income students already know. Then pivot to the novel object.



13.4.3 Slide 5.3 — Empirical Term Structure: PE Returns by Vintage

Title: “PE returns by vintage and time-to-liquidation”
Visual: Heatmap or 3D surface of pooled IRR by vintage year × years to liquidation
Key observations:

2006–2008 vintages show large vintage-year effects (returns depend on entry conditions)
Mid-vintage years (2010–2015) cluster near long-run averages
2018–2020 vintages still uncertain (recent)

Speaker notes: This is the empirical term structure. It’s noisy. The theoretical question is: what does GE-LAV predict?



13.4.4 Slide 5.4 — The Liquidity Risk Term Structure

Title: “Risk grows with horizon — but not the way DCF says”
DCF prediction: Risk scales as \(\sigma \sqrt{T}\) (standard random walk scaling)
GE-LAV prediction (OU + Jensen): Risk has two components:

Diffusion component: scales as \(\sqrt{T}\) for short \(T\), plateaus for long \(T\) (mean reversion)
Jensen bias component: grows linearly with \(T\)

Visual: Two curves overlaid:

DCF risk: straight \(\sqrt{T}\) line
GE-LAV risk: rises, plateaus, then rises again (Jensen kicking in)

Speaker notes: This is the key conceptual contribution of Chapter 3. The shape is qualitatively different from DCF.



13.4.5 Slide 5.5 — The Jensen Bias Formula

Title: “The Jensen bias as a function of horizon”
Formula (boxed): \(B(T) = A \cdot T + C\) (affine in \(T\), to leading order)
Where:

\(A\) = annual Jensen bias accrual rate (calibrated 0.1–0.4% per year by asset class)
\(C\) = small constant offset

Calibrated values by asset class:




Asset Class
A (per year)
C




PE Buyout
0.16%
0.0%


Growth Equity
0.17%
0.1%


Real Estate
0.18%
0.0%


Infrastructure
0.18%
0.0%


Private Credit
0.10%
0.0%




Speaker notes: This is a closed-form result. Track 2 students will derive it in Session 29.



13.4.6 Slide 5.6 — Worked Example: Jensen Bias by Horizon

Title: “Computing \(B(T)\) for typical horizons”
Table:




Asset
Hold T
A
\(B(T)\)




5-yr Buyout
5
0.16%
0.8%


10-yr Growth
10
0.17%
1.7%


15-yr Secondary
15
0.17%
2.5%


20-yr Infrastructure
20
0.18%

25
0.18%
4.5%


30-yr PPP Asset
30
0.18%
5.4%




Bottom-line: “30-year PPP assets carry Jensen bias 7× larger than 5-year buyouts.”
Speaker notes: This is just the Jensen bias channel. The GE-LAV equilibrium correction can be much larger.



13.4.7 Slide 5.7 — Why Long-Horizon Assets Are Most Affected

Title: “Why infrastructure is more affected than buyout”
Three reasons:

Jensen bias scales with \(T\) — longer hold means more cumulative convexity effect
More time for liquidity state to drift — variance grows with \(T\) (up to stationary limit)
Discount factor is more sensitive to rate changes at long \(T\) — modified duration effect

Visual: Three small charts showing each effect
Speaker notes: This is the implication for infrastructure and long-hold strategies. Pension funds and insurers — the biggest holders of long-duration private assets — face the largest valuation errors.



13.4.8 Slide 5.8 — The Lévy Extension

Title: “GE-LAV term structure with jumps”
Setup: “Pure OU is Gaussian. Real data has jumps (March 2020, GFC). The Lévy extension adds compound Poisson jumps to OU.”
Effect on term structure:

Short \(T\): jumps add fat tails to short-horizon variance
Long \(T\): jump effects average out, term structure resembles pure-OU result

Calibrated jump intensity: ~0.3 jumps per year, mean jump size −0.4σ
Speaker notes: Brief mention. Full Lévy treatment is in Track 2 advanced topics.



13.4.9 Slide 5.9 — DLOM: The Industry Alternative

Title: “DLOM: Discount for Lack of Marketability”
What it is:

Industry-standard approach to adjusting valuations for illiquidity
Typical magnitudes: 15–40% reduction in computed DCF value
Methods: Restricted stock studies, pre-IPO studies, option-based models (Finnerty, Longstaff)

How it works: Apply a flat percentage discount to the DCF mark
Common assumption: “DLOM is constant for an asset class” (e.g., 25% for PE, 30% for VC)
Speaker notes: Most students will have encountered DLOM in valuation classes or CFA. Establish it as the current best industry alternative, then critique.



13.4.10 Slide 5.10 — DLOM by Asset Class (Industry Convention)

Title: “Typical DLOM ranges”
Table:




Asset Class
Typical DLOM




Late-stage private equity
15–25%


Early-stage venture
30–45%


Real estate private equity
20–30%


Infrastructure
10–20%


Private credit
5–15%




Speaker notes: Source: AICPA Valuation Guidelines, various restricted stock studies. Vary across appraisers and contexts.



13.4.11 Slide 5.11 — DLOM vs. GE-LAV: Comparison

Title: “Same problem, two answers”
Side-by-side table:









Feature
DLOM
GE-LAV




Treatment of premium
Flat percentage discount
Stochastic OU dynamics


Regime dependence
None
Endogenous to liquidity state


Horizon dependence
Flat
Affine in T


Theoretical foundation
Empirical (restricted stock studies)
First-principles asset pricing


Crisis behavior
Static
Amplifies via McKean-Vlasov


Calibration
Subjective / appraiser judgment
OLS / MLE on secondary data




Bottom-line: “DLOM was the best available method when secondary market data didn’t exist. GE-LAV is the first method that can use the secondary market data correctly.”
Speaker notes: Be respectful of DLOM. It was a reasonable approach. Now we have better tools.



13.4.12 Slide 5.12 — DLOM vs. GE-LAV: Numerical Comparison

Title: “A 20-year infrastructure asset, three methods”
Three columns:

DCF: $100M (constant 7.5%, premium 3.5%)
DCF + DLOM (15%): $85M (just shave 15% off)
GE-LAV (normal markets): $95M (Jensen-corrected + small GE adjustment)
GE-LAV (Regime 4 multi-shock): $80M
GE-LAV (Regime 2 GFC depth): $30M

Punchline: “DLOM is a constant adjustment. GE-LAV is a regime-dependent calculation. In normal markets, they’re close. In crises, they’re completely different.”



13.4.13 Slide 5.13 — Performance Measurement Implications (Tease)

Title: “What does this mean for performance measurement?”
Setup: “If valuations are wrong systematically, returns are wrong systematically”
Implications:

IRR is computed against wrong valuations
PME comparisons are biased
Vintage-year analysis is contaminated

Speaker notes: This is the bridge to Session 6 (IRR) and Session 7 (PME).



13.4.14 Slide 5.14 — Track Declaration

Title: “Today is decision day”
Reminders:

Default is Track 1 if you don’t choose
Track 2 requires stochastic calculus background or self-taught equivalent
Cross-track audit is allowed and encouraged
Track 2 PSets are mathematical; Track 1 PSets are applied

Self-test: “Did Session 4’s OU material feel intuitive? If yes, Track 2 is feasible.”
Logistics: Submit track choice via course form by end of class



13.4.15 Slide 5.15 — Where We Go From Here

Title: “Unit 1 wraps. Unit 2 begins.”
Session 6: IRR — what it is, what it isn’t, where it fails
Session 7: PME and the measurement problem
Session 8: What a correct theory must deliver (Ch 5 of book)
Session 9: Midterm review
Session 10: Midterm exam
Speaker notes: Frame Unit 1 as the “diagnostic” — what’s wrong with current practice. Unit 2 finishes the diagnosis with measurement issues. Then Unit 3 builds the alternative.
crumbs: 13  Session 5: Term Structure of Private Capital Returns

title: 13  Session 5: Term Structure of Private Capital Returns
text:
13.5 Discussion Questions

The Jensen bias for a 30-year PPP asset is ~5.4%. The DLOM convention for infrastructure is 10–20%. Are these incompatible? How should they be reconciled?
Pension funds and insurers hold most of the long-duration private assets where Jensen bias is largest. If GE-LAV becomes adopted methodology, what happens to reported PE returns at large pension funds?
Some private credit assets have short effective duration (1–3 years for direct lending). Jensen bias is small there. Does that mean private credit is “GE-LAV-immune”? What other GE-LAV channels might still matter?
crumbs: 13  Session 5: Term Structure of Private Capital Returns

title: 13  Session 5: Term Structure of Private Capital Returns
section: 13.6 Worked Numerical Example: Choosing Between DLOM and GE-LAV
text:
13.6 Worked Numerical Example: Choosing Between DLOM and GE-LAV
Setup: Insurance company holds a 15-year core infrastructure asset (regulated water utility). DCF mark = $500M.
Method 1: DLOM at 15% - Adjusted value: $500M × 0.85 = $425M - Applied uniformly across all market conditions - “Always 15% off DCF”
Method 2: GE-LAV (current state, normalization regime) - Jensen bias for T = 15: \(A \cdot 15 + C = 0.18\% \cdot 15 = 2.7\%\) - LAV value (just Jensen): $500M × (1 + 0.027) = \(513.5M\) - GE-LAV (normal regime, small equilibrium correction): ~$495M - “About flat with DCF in normal markets”
Method 3: GE-LAV (current state, stressed regime, L = −0.8) - Equilibrium rate: \(r_\text{GE-LAV}(L = -0.8) \approx 12\%\) (vs. DCF 7.5%) - GE-LAV value: $500M × e^{-(12% - 7.5%) } $250M - “50% off DCF in stress”
Method 4: GE-LAV (GFC depth, L = −1.5) - Equilibrium rate: ~32% - GE-LAV value: ~$150M - “70% off DCF at GFC depth”
Comparison: DLOM gives one answer ($425M). GE-LAV gives a menu of answers conditional on regime. The insurance regulator should care about the GFC-depth number ($150M) for solvency purposes.
crumbs: 13  Session 5: Term Structure of Private Capital Returns

title: 13  Session 5: Term Structure of Private Capital Returns
text:
13.7 Common Student Confusions (Instructor Notes)

“If Jensen bias is small (1–4%), why is it a big deal?” It compounds across the $13T market = $130-520B in systematic underreporting. Also it’s the only always-positive correction — the GE-LAV equilibrium correction can be positive or negative depending on regime.
“DLOM at 15-25% is much bigger than Jensen bias at 2-4%. Doesn’t DLOM dominate?” DLOM and Jensen are not the same thing. DLOM is trying to capture the entire illiquidity adjustment in one number. Jensen is just one channel. GE-LAV’s full correction (Jensen + GE equilibrium) can match or exceed DLOM in stressed regimes.
“How do we know the calibration is right?” Maximum likelihood on 22 years of secondary market data. Reasonably robust. Confidence intervals are tight enough that the qualitative conclusions don’t change under reasonable parameter variation.
“What about private credit? Spread compensation already exists.” Private credit has its own GE-LAV variant (Ch 23). Spread is partly compensation for credit risk, partly for illiquidity. Decomposing these requires GE-LAV.
crumbs: 13  Session 5: Term Structure of Private Capital Returns

title: 13  Session 5: Term Structure of Private Capital Returns
text:
13.8 What to Expect Next Session
Session 6 begins Unit 2 (Measurement and Theory) with a deep look at IRR. Why IRR is the dominant performance metric in private markets, what its two structural biases are, and why a constant-rate metric cannot correctly measure performance when rates are stochastic.
Reading: Book Chapter 4, sections 4.2–4.4 (~10 pages).
For Track 2 students who declared today: Begin reading Karatzas-Shreve Ch. 5 (Brownian motion and stochastic calculus). You’ll need this background by Session 25.

Identify the two structural biases of IRR (reinvestment assumption + sign-pattern dependence).
Distinguish money-weighted from time-weighted returns and explain when each is appropriate.

Explain why a constant-rate metric cannot correctly summarize performance when discount rates are stochastic.
Prepare for Session 7’s PME discussion by understanding why IRR’s failures motivated PME’s development.

title: 14  Session 6: IRR · The Internal Rate of Return Problem
text:
14.4 Slide Content Briefs (New Slides Needed)

14.4.1 Slide 6.1 — Title

Title: “The IRR Problem”
Tag line: “Why the most-used metric in private markets is structurally broken”
Speaker notes: Frame Unit 2 as the measurement counterpart to Unit 1’s valuation critique. Bad valuations cause bad returns calculations.



14.4.2 Slide 6.2 — IRR: Definition

Title: “IRR formally”
Definition: “IRR is the discount rate \(r\) that makes NPV = 0”
Equation: \(0 = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}\)
Solving: Implicit equation; typically solved numerically (Newton-Raphson or Excel’s IRR function)
Standard interpretation: “Annualized return on capital invested”
Speaker notes: Set up the math cleanly. Students will know IRR intuitively; the goal is to make them careful about it.



14.4.3 Slide 6.3 — IRR: Numerical Example

Title: “A clean IRR computation”
Cash flows:

\(t = 0\): \(-\$100M\) (initial commitment, drawn over 3 years)
\(t = 4\): \(+\$25M\)
\(t = 6\): \(+\$50M\)
\(t = 8\): \(+\$75M\)
\(t = 10\): \(+\$30M\)

IRR: Solve \(-100 + 25(1+r)^{-4} + 50(1+r)^{-6} + 75(1+r)^{-8} + 30(1+r)^{-10} = 0\)
Solution: \(r \approx 14.2\%\)
Speaker notes: Walk through one Newton iteration on the board if time. Students should see how IRR is solved, not just trust Excel.



14.4.4 Slide 6.4 — Money-Weighted vs. Time-Weighted

Title: “Two different questions, two different metrics”
Money-weighted return (= IRR):

“What return did the capital earn?”
Heavily influenced by timing of large cash flows
Standard in private markets

Time-weighted return:

“What return did the manager generate?”
Independent of investor cash flow timing
Standard in public markets (mutual funds, hedge funds)

Why PE uses MWR (= IRR):

GP controls capital call timing
LP cares about return on dollars actually deployed

Speaker notes: This distinction matters for comparing PE returns to public benchmarks. PME (Session 7) is designed to bridge MWR-based PE returns with TWR-based public benchmarks.



14.4.5 Slide 6.5 — Worked: MWR vs. TWR Difference

Title: “When MWR and TWR diverge”
Setup: Two funds with identical underlying portfolio performance but different cash flow timing
Fund A: Calls capital early in fund life, distributes late
Fund B: Calls capital late, distributes early
Result: Same TWR (both managed the portfolio the same way), but Fund B has higher IRR (capital was deployed for less time on the larger amounts)
Speaker notes: This is a classic teaching example. Make sure students understand: the same investment skill can produce different IRRs depending on cash flow timing alone.



14.4.6 Slide 6.6 — IRR Bias #1: The Reinvestment Assumption

Title: “Bias 1: IRR assumes intermediate cash flows are reinvested at IRR itself”
Setup: “When IRR = 18%, the math implicitly assumes every distribution is reinvested at 18% for the remainder of the fund life”
Reality: “LPs typically receive distributions and either spend them or invest at much lower public market rates (~7-10% for diversified equities)”
Implication: “IRR systematically overstates the realized return when distributions are large and early”
Magnitude: “Can overstate by 2-5 percentage points for typical PE fund cash flow patterns”
Speaker notes: This is well-known but under-appreciated. The Modified IRR (MIRR) is a partial fix that uses a separate reinvestment rate, but isn’t standard in PE reporting.



14.4.7 Slide 6.7 — IRR Bias #2: Sign-Pattern Dependence

Title: “Bias 2: IRR is sensitive to the sign pattern of cash flows”
Standard case (-, 0, …, 0, +): Single IRR, well-defined
Mixed signs (-, +, -, +): Multiple IRRs can exist (Descartes’ rule of signs)
PE example: Continuation fund recapitalizations create intermediate negative cash flows (LP rolls over into new vehicle, takes some cash out) → multiple IRRs possible
Speaker notes: This is very common in modern PE with continuation funds and GP-led secondaries. Students will encounter this in practice.



14.4.8 Slide 6.8 — Multiple IRRs: A Concrete Example

Title: “When IRR isn’t unique”
Cash flows:

\(t = 0\): \(-\$100\)
\(t = 1\): \(+\$230\)
\(t = 2\): \(-\$132\)

NPV equation: \(-100 + 230/(1+r) - 132/(1+r)^2 = 0\)
Two solutions: \(r = 10\%\) and \(r = 20\%\)
Which is “the IRR”? Both, mathematically. Neither, economically.
Recommended approach: “Use NPV at a fixed discount rate; report IRR only with caveats about sign pattern”
Speaker notes: This blows students’ minds the first time. It’s textbook material but rarely emphasized.



14.4.9 Slide 6.9 — IRR Under Stochastic Discount Rates

Title: “Where IRR fundamentally breaks: when rates are stochastic”
Setup: “IRR by construction is a single number — one constant rate”
GE-LAV reality: Discount rate \(r(L_t)\) varies with liquidity state
The mismatch:

IRR averages out the rate variation
But the correct present value uses the full path of \(L_t\)
These give different answers (Jensen’s inequality again)

Speaker notes: Connect to Session 2’s Jensen discussion. IRR’s constant-rate assumption embeds the same convexity error as DCF’s constant-premium assumption.



14.4.10 Slide 6.10 — The Constant-Rate Fallacy

Title: “The Constant-Rate Fallacy”
Boxed statement: “Any metric that compresses time-varying discount rates into a single constant rate inherits the systematic bias of DCF.”
Implications:

IRR is biased
Money-weighted return is biased (same as IRR)
Hurdle rate comparisons are biased
Carried interest computations are biased

The cure: Liquidity-adjusted metrics (LA-IRR, LA-PME) — covered Session 7
Speaker notes: This is the bridge to Session 7. IRR’s problem is the same as DCF’s problem, restated.



14.4.11 Slide 6.11 — Empirical Magnitudes: How Wrong Is IRR?

Title: “How wrong is IRR in practice?”
Data: Pooled IRR of US PE buyout funds 1990–2024 (Preqin, Kaplan-Schoar)
Reported pooled IRR: ~12.5% per year
Phalippou (2009) adjustment for reinvestment assumption: ~9.5%
Stafford (2022) adjustment for valuation smoothing: ~7.5%
GE-LAV-adjusted estimate (LA-IRR): ~8.5% (intermediate)
Speaker notes: This is the number for students to remember. Reported PE returns are 3-5 percentage points overstated by methodology choices.



14.4.12 Slide 6.12 — IRR and Vintage-Year Effects

Title: “Why vintage matters more than skill”
Empirical finding: “Vintage year (when the fund started investing) explains 40-60% of variation in fund-level IRR across managers”
Implication: “Top-quartile vintage 2009 outperforms top-quartile vintage 2007 — not because managers were better, but because market entry conditions were”
Connection to GE-LAV: “Vintage effect is partly a liquidity state effect — funds starting in low-L states (high implied premium) benefit when L mean-reverts back to \(\bar{L}\)”
Speaker notes: Students often think of PE returns as manager-driven. Vintage effects dominate. GE-LAV explains why mathematically.



14.4.13 Slide 6.13 — IRR and Carry: Why Practitioners Defend IRR

Title: “Why IRR survives despite known flaws”
The institutional reasons:

Carried interest is computed on IRR — moving away from IRR moves the carry calculation
GP compensation is anchored to IRR hurdles — typically 8% preferred return
LP allocators benchmark on IRR — switching benchmarks is painful
No regulatory mandate to change — IRR is in IPEV, ILPA, and Solvency II guidance

Speaker notes: This is the political economy of IRR persistence. Students entering the industry should understand this.



14.4.14 Slide 6.14 — Proposed Solutions Overview (Tease for Session 7)

Title: “Three responses to the IRR problem”
Three columns:

Reformed IRR: MIRR with explicit reinvestment rate; sign-pattern caveats; still constant-rate
PME family: Compare to public benchmark — Kaplan-Schoar, Long-Nickels, Direct Alpha
LA-IRR / LA-PME: Liquidity-adjusted version that handles stochastic rates correctly

Speaker notes: Session 7 covers all three families in detail.



14.4.15 Slide 6.15 — Summary

Title: “IRR: where it works, where it fails”
Works:

Simple sign pattern (single negative followed by positives)
Stable market conditions
Comparing across short hold periods

Fails:

Mixed sign patterns (continuation funds, recapitalizations)
Stochastic discount rates (always, in private markets)
Cross-vintage comparisons
Long-horizon assets where Jensen bias accumulates

Speaker notes: Honest summary. IRR isn’t useless; it’s incomplete.
crumbs: 14  Session 6: IRR · The Internal Rate of Return Problem

title: 14  Session 6: IRR · The Internal Rate of Return Problem
text:
14.5 Discussion Questions

The GP-LP relationship has IRR baked into the carried interest formula. If GE-LAV becomes adopted methodology, does the carry formula need to change? How?
Public mutual funds use TWR. Why has PE never moved to TWR despite known IRR flaws?
Two PE funds have identical IRRs of 18% but very different cash flow timing patterns. How would you distinguish them from each other in selecting which to commit capital to?
crumbs: 14  Session 6: IRR · The Internal Rate of Return Problem

title: 14  Session 6: IRR · The Internal Rate of Return Problem
section: 14.6 Worked Numerical Example: When IRR Misleads
text:
14.6 Worked Numerical Example: When IRR Misleads
Setup: Two PE funds, both reporting IRR = 18%.
Fund A (front-loaded distributions): - \(t=0\): \(-\$100M\) - \(t=2\): \(+\$80M\) - \(t=4\): \(+\$40M\) - \(t=6\): \(+\$20M\) - IRR: 18% (capital out quickly)
Fund B (back-loaded distributions): - \(t=0\): \(-\$100M\) - \(t=2\): \(+\$0M\) - \(t=4\): \(+\$0M\) - \(t=8\): \(+\$200M\) - IRR: 18% (capital out only at end)
The reinvestment problem:
If LP can reinvest at 7% public market rate:

Fund A realized return: $80M from year 2 grows at 7% to \((80 \cdot 1.07^6) + (40 \cdot 1.07^4) + 20 = \$120M + \$52M + \$20M = \$192M\) at year 8 — total return 92% over 8 years = 8.4% annualized
Fund B realized return: $200M at year 8 — total return 100% = 9.05% annualized

Interpretation: Same reported IRR (18%), but Fund B’s realized return to the LP is higher because the IRR overstatement is larger for Fund A.
Lesson: Reported IRR can rank funds in the wrong order when reinvestment assumption matters.
crumbs: 14  Session 6: IRR · The Internal Rate of Return Problem

title: 14  Session 6: IRR · The Internal Rate of Return Problem
text:
14.7 Common Student Confusions (Instructor Notes)

“Isn’t MIRR the standard fix?” MIRR exists but isn’t standard in PE reporting. ILPA and IPEV use plain IRR. Industry inertia.
“Can’t I just use the Modified Dietz return?” That’s a TWR computation. It tells you a different thing (manager skill) than IRR (return on dollars deployed). Both have a role.
“If IRR is so flawed, why do all PE funds report it?” Because LPs demand it, regulators sanction it, and carry computation depends on it. Network effects.
“What is Direct Alpha vs. Long-Nickels PME?” Tease — covered Session 7.
crumbs: 14  Session 6: IRR · The Internal Rate of Return Problem

title: 14  Session 6: IRR · The Internal Rate of Return Problem
text:
14.8 What to Expect Next Session
Session 7 dives into PME (Public Market Equivalent) and its variants. We’ll cover: - The Kaplan-Schoar PME and what it computes - Long-Nickels PME (with implicit reinvestment in the benchmark) - Direct Alpha (Gredil-Griffiths-Stucke) - LA-IRR and LA-PME — the GE-LAV-corrected versions - Which method to use in which context
Reading: Book Chapter 4, sections 4.5–4.10 (~12 pages).

title: 15  Session 7: PME · LA-IRR · LA-PME
text:
15.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, students will be able to:

title: 15  Session 7: PME · LA-IRR · LA-PME
text:
Compute the Kaplan-Schoar PME for a given fund’s cash flows against a chosen public benchmark.
Distinguish Kaplan-Schoar PME, Long-Nickels PME, and Direct Alpha — and state when each is appropriate.
Articulate why all standard PME variants share the same constant-rate weakness as DCF and IRR.
State the LA-IRR and LA-PME formulas and explain what corrections they apply.
Apply LA-PME to a worked example and interpret the result against a standard PME.

title: 15  Session 7: PME · LA-IRR · LA-PME
text:
15.4 Slide Content Briefs (New Slides Needed)

15.4.1 Slide 7.1 — Title

Title: “PME, LA-IRR, LA-PME”
Tag line: “Comparing private to public, correctly”



15.4.2 Slide 7.2 — The PME Question

Title: “What does PME try to answer?”
The question: “If I had invested the same cash flows in the S&P 500 instead of in this PE fund, how would I have done?”
Why this matters:

Allocators need benchmarks
Carry hurdles are absolute (8% pref); PME is relative (vs. market)
Academic and regulatory work needs cross-asset-class comparison

Speaker notes: Frame PME as the response to IRR’s weaknesses. PME doesn’t fix IRR; it gives a complementary lens.



15.4.3 Slide 7.3 — Kaplan-Schoar PME (Definition)

Title: “Kaplan-Schoar (KS) PME”
Formula: \(PME_{KS} = \dfrac{\sum_t D_t / B_t}{\sum_t C_t / B_t}\)
Where:

\(D_t\) = distributions in period \(t\)
\(C_t\) = capital calls in period \(t\)
\(B_t\) = public benchmark cumulative return from inception to period \(t\)

Interpretation:

\(PME_{KS} > 1\): PE fund outperformed the benchmark
\(PME_{KS} = 1\): PE fund matched the benchmark
\(PME_{KS} < 1\): PE fund underperformed

Speaker notes: Most-used PME in practice. Simple, intuitive, easy to compute.



15.4.4 Slide 7.4 — Kaplan-Schoar: Worked Example

Title: “KS PME: a worked example”
Setup: 10-year PE fund vs. S&P 500
Cash flows and benchmark levels:




Year
Call (\(M) | Distribution (\)M)
S&P Index Level





0
30
0
100


1
40
0
108


2
30
0
118


5
0
80
145


8
0
90
180


10
0
60
200




Numerator (sum of distributions discounted by benchmark): \(80/(145/100) + 90/(180/100) + 60/(200/100) = 55.2 + 50.0 + 30.0 = 135.2\)
Denominator (sum of calls discounted by benchmark): \(30/1.00 + 40/(108/100) + 30/(118/100) = 30 + 37.0 + 25.4 = 92.4\)
KS PME: \(135.2 / 92.4 = 1.46\) → PE fund outperformed S&P 500 by 46%



15.4.5 Slide 7.5 — Long-Nickels PME (Definition)

Title: “Long-Nickels (LN) PME”
Idea: “Build a hypothetical portfolio that mirrors the PE fund’s cash flow timing in the public benchmark, then compute its IRR”
Mechanism:

When PE calls \(X\) from LP, hypothetical portfolio buys \(X\) of benchmark
When PE distributes \(Y\) to LP, hypothetical portfolio sells \(Y\) of benchmark
Track the implied “synthetic public fund” NAV over time
Compute its IRR; compare to PE fund’s IRR

Output: Two IRRs (PE fund vs. synthetic public); difference is the alpha
Speaker notes: More sophisticated than KS PME. Handles the “what would the public benchmark have done with my exact cash flow pattern” question directly.



15.4.6 Slide 7.6 — KS vs. LN: When They Diverge

Title: “KS and LN: same idea, different formulations”
Both compare PE returns to public benchmark with same cash flow timing
KS gives a multiple (1.46×); LN gives an IRR-style annualized rate
They can disagree when:

Cash flow patterns are unusual (continuation funds, recap distributions)
Long fund lives where reinvestment matters
Multiple IRRs in the synthetic fund (rare but possible)

Speaker notes: For most simple cases, KS and LN agree. For corner cases, LN is more nuanced.



15.4.7 Slide 7.7 — Direct Alpha (Gredil-Griffiths-Stucke 2014)

Title: “Direct Alpha: a cleaner formulation”
Idea: “Compute the constant excess discount rate \(\alpha\) such that adjusted PE cash flows have zero NPV against the benchmark”
Formula: Find \(\alpha\) solving \(\sum_t (D_t - C_t) \cdot e^{-\alpha t} / B_t = 0\)
Output: A single number \(\alpha\) interpreted as the “alpha vs. the benchmark”
Advantages over KS / LN:

Returns an interpretable annualized rate
Handles complex cash flow patterns cleanly
More robust to vintage-year effects

Speaker notes: Direct Alpha is the academic favorite. Practitioners still mostly use KS.



15.4.8 Slide 7.8 — The Constant-Rate Weakness (PME’s Achilles Heel)

Title: “Why all standard PMEs share IRR’s weakness”
The common assumption:

KS, LN, and Direct Alpha all treat the public benchmark as the risk-adjusted reference
They embed the implicit assumption that any extra return beyond the benchmark is fair compensation for liquidity, vintage, and selection
This is a constant-rate assumption about the illiquidity premium

The mismatch:

GE-LAV says the illiquidity premium is stochastic and regime-dependent
PMEs cannot see this — they compress everything into a single number

Speaker notes: This is the conceptual handoff to LA-PME.



15.4.9 Slide 7.9 — LA-IRR: Liquidity-Adjusted IRR (Definition)

Title: “LA-IRR: GE-LAV’s correction to IRR”
The idea: “Compute IRR using a stochastic discount rate path \(r(L_t)\) instead of a constant rate”
Mechanics:

Calibrate \(L_t\) path from secondary market data (or simulate from OU)
Compute period-by-period discount factors \(D_t = \exp(-\int_0^t r(L_s) ds)\)
Find the effective single-rate equivalent that matches the cash flow PV

Output: A liquidity-adjusted single-rate summary, comparable to standard IRR but corrected
Magnitude: “For typical PE fund cash flow patterns, LA-IRR is typically 2–4 percentage points below reported IRR”
Speaker notes: This is the measurement counterpart to LAV’s valuation correction. Same underlying logic.



15.4.10 Slide 7.10 — LA-PME: Liquidity-Adjusted PME

Title: “LA-PME: GE-LAV’s correction to PME”
The idea: “Use the public benchmark adjusted for the contemporaneous liquidity state as the comparison”
Mechanics:

At each period, compute the benchmark return adjusted for the implied illiquidity premium gap
\(B_t^{LA} = B_t \cdot \exp(\int_0^t \pi(L_s) ds)\)
Apply KS / LN / Direct Alpha methodology to \(B_t^{LA}\) instead of raw \(B_t\)

Output: PME that compares PE to a “public benchmark plus correctly-priced liquidity premium”
Magnitude: “Typical PE fund LA-PME is 5-15% below reported KS PME”
Speaker notes: This is a meaningful magnitude. Allocators caring about absolute outperformance should use LA-PME.



15.4.11 Slide 7.11 — Comparing LA-PME to Standard PME: Worked Example

Title: “Worked: 2010-vintage buyout fund”
Setup:

Reported KS PME (vs. S&P 500): 1.32 — “32% outperformance”
Adjusted for L_t path 2010-2024 (mostly Regimes 3, 4): LA-PME = 1.18 — “18% outperformance”

Decomposition:

Of the 32 percentage points of reported KS outperformance, ~14 points are compensation for time-varying illiquidity premium (not alpha)
True alpha vs. liquidity-adjusted benchmark: ~18 percentage points

Speaker notes: Even with adjustment, this fund outperformed. But the magnitude of “true alpha” is materially smaller than reported.



15.4.12 Slide 7.12 — Vintage-Year Liquidity Effects

Title: “Why vintage matters: liquidity state at entry”
Setup:

Vintage 2009: entered at \(L_0 \approx -1.5\) (crisis depth); mean reversion drove L back to \(\bar{L}\)
Vintage 2007: entered at \(L_0 \approx +0.5\) (boom); mean reversion drove L down toward \(\bar{L}\)

Result:

2009 vintage benefited from positive mean reversion in L
2007 vintage suffered from negative mean reversion in L

GE-LAV decomposition: “Vintage-year IRR differences are partly a liquidity state effect, which GE-LAV captures explicitly”
Speaker notes: This is the explanation for the strong vintage-year pattern in PE returns. Standard models can’t decompose it; GE-LAV can.



15.4.13 Slide 7.13 — Practical: Which Metric for Which Purpose

Title: “When to use which metric”
Decision table:




Purpose
Best Metric




Carried interest computation
Reported IRR (constrained by LPA)


Cross-fund manager skill comparison
KS PME or Direct Alpha


LP allocation decisions
LA-IRR + LA-PME


Regulatory reporting (Solvency II)
LA-PME (under GE-LAV adoption)


Academic research on PE returns
Direct Alpha + LA-PME


Vintage-year analysis
LA-IRR (controls for liquidity state)




Speaker notes: Different metrics for different uses. The point isn’t “use LA-PME for everything” but “know which metric you need.”



15.4.14 Slide 7.14 — Implementation: Where to Get the Data

Title: “Implementing LA-IRR and LA-PME”
Inputs needed:

PE fund cash flows (LP statements, Preqin, PitchBook)
Public benchmark returns (S&P 500, MSCI, asset-class-specific)
\(L_t\) path: derived from secondary market discount data (Preqin, Setter, Lazard)
OU calibration parameters: from book Ch. 12 or GE-LAV platform

Implementation tools:

GE-LAV® platform (liquidityillusion.com): native LA-IRR and LA-PME functions
Python / R: 50–100 lines of code

Speaker notes: Students will do this in PS1 (Track 1) for their term project.



15.4.15 Slide 7.15 — Summary and Bridge to Session 8

Title: “Measurement summary”
Key takeaways:

IRR has two structural biases (Session 6)
Standard PMEs share IRR’s constant-rate weakness
LA-IRR and LA-PME correct for time-varying liquidity premium
Magnitudes: 2–4 pp adjustment for LA-IRR; 5–15% adjustment for LA-PME

Tomorrow (Session 8): “What does a correct theory of private market valuation require? Five requirements that GE-LAV satisfies.”
Speaker notes: Set up Session 8 as the synthesis of Unit 2.

title: 15  Session 7: PME · LA-IRR · LA-PME
text:
15.5 Discussion Questions

A 2009-vintage PE fund reports IRR of 22% and KS PME of 1.45. LA-IRR is 16% and LA-PME is 1.18. How should an LP allocating capital today interpret these numbers?
The carry computation in a typical LPA uses reported IRR with an 8% preferred return. If LA-IRR becomes standard, does the 8% hurdle remain at 8%? What should it be?
Some recent academic work argues that PE has earned zero alpha after correct adjustments (Phalippou, Stafford). Other work argues it earns positive alpha (Harris-Jenkinson-Kaplan). Where do you sit, and what’s the role of methodology choice in your answer?

title: 15  Session 7: PME · LA-IRR · LA-PME
section: 15.6 Worked Numerical Example: Full LA-PME Calculation
text:
15.6 Worked Numerical Example: Full LA-PME Calculation
Setup: A 2010-vintage US buyout fund.

Step 1: Standard KS PME
Numerator: \(30/1.55 + 50/1.65 + 80/2.00 + 25/1.95 + 45/2.30 + 60/2.70 = 19.4 + 30.3 + 40.0 + 12.8 + 19.6 + 22.2 = 144.3\)
Denominator: \(50/1.00 + 30/1.02 + 20/1.20 = 50 + 29.4 + 16.7 = 96.1\)
\(KS PME = 144.3 / 96.1 = 1.50\) → 50% outperformance
Step 2: Compute liquidity-adjusted benchmark \(B_t^{LA}\)
Using calibrated \(\pi(L) = 0.045 - 0.025 L + 0.021 L^2\):
At each year, compute \(\pi(L_t)\) and integrate over time. The cumulative liquidity premium drag on the benchmark over 14 years (assuming average \(\pi\) ≈ 4.5%) is approximately \(\exp(0.045 \cdot 14) = 1.88\).
Adjusted benchmark levels: \(B_t^{LA} = B_t \cdot e^{\int_0^t \pi(L_s) ds}\)
For simplicity, applying a uniform 4.5%/year drag:



Year
Adjusted Benchmark




2010
1.00


2011
1.067 (1.02 × 1.046)


2014
1.79 (1.55 × 1.156)


2018
2.66 (2.00 × 1.33)


2022
3.45 (2.30 × 1.50)


2024
4.21 (2.70 × 1.56)



Step 3: Recompute PME with adjusted benchmark
Numerator: \(30/1.79 + 50/[B_{2016}^{LA}] + 80/2.66 + 25/[B_{2020}^{LA}] + 45/3.45 + 60/4.21\) ≈ \(16.8 + 24.5 + 30.1 + 9.5 + 13.0 + 14.3 = 108.2\)
Denominator: \(50/1.00 + 30/1.067 + 20/1.135 = 50 + 28.1 + 17.6 = 95.7\)
\(LA PME = 108.2 / 95.7 = 1.13\) → 13% outperformance
Interpretation: - Reported KS PME: 1.50 (50% outperformance) - LA-PME: 1.13 (13% outperformance) - 37 percentage points of reported outperformance was compensation for the liquidity premium drag the benchmark didn’t reflect

title: 15  Session 7: PME · LA-IRR · LA-PME
text:
15.7 Common Student Confusions (Instructor Notes)

“If LA-PME is lower, doesn’t that mean PE is worse than we thought?” Not exactly. It means reported outperformance was partly fair compensation for liquidity risk, not true alpha. The fund may still be a good investment, just for different reasons.
“How do I get an L_t time series?” From secondary market discount data. PS1 Track 1 walks you through this.
“Doesn’t this assume the public benchmark is the right risk-free reference?” It assumes the risk-adjusted benchmark is the right reference. Beta adjustments are typically applied separately. LA-PME handles the illiquidity risk specifically.
“What if the secondary market is itself wrong?” GE-LAV assumes secondary market prices reflect the marginal investor’s willingness to pay. If you reject that assumption, you reject the entire framework. Reasonable critique; addressed in book Ch. 2.

title: 15  Session 7: PME · LA-IRR · LA-PME
text:
15.8 What to Expect Next Session
Session 8 is the synthesis of Unit 2: what would a correct theory of private market valuation actually require? We’ll cover the five requirements from book Chapter 5:

Stochastic illiquidity premia
Investor heterogeneity
Endogenous market-clearing
Empirical implementability
Welfare and regulatory analysis support

GE-LAV satisfies all five. No other current framework does. Session 8 sets up the midterm and the conceptual transition to Unit 3 (decision and application).
Reading: Book Chapter 5, all sections (~10 pages — short chapter).
Reminder: PS1 is due Session 8. Allow yourself the weekend to work through the OU calibration questions.

16.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, students will be able to:

title: 16  Session 8: What a Correct Theory Must Deliver
text:
State the five requirements a correct private market valuation theory must satisfy.
Evaluate alternative frameworks (DCF, DLOM, factor models, GE-LAV) against these five requirements.
Articulate why no framework prior to GE-LAV satisfies all five simultaneously.
Connect Unit 1 (why DCF fails) and Unit 2 (measurement problems) to Unit 3 (the GE-LAV alternative).
Identify the five main results of GE-LAV by name and recognize them when encountered.

title: 16  Session 8: What a Correct Theory Must Deliver
text:
16.4 Slide Content Briefs (New Slides Needed)

16.4.1 Slide 8.1 — Title

Title: “What a Correct Theory Must Deliver”
Tag line: “Five requirements · The standard GE-LAV satisfies and no other framework meets”
Speaker notes: Session 8 is the conceptual synthesis. Students should leave knowing the criteria against which any valuation theory should be judged.



16.4.2 Slide 8.2 — Why a Theory, Not Just Better Numbers

Title: “Why we need a theory”
The problem with ad hoc fixes:

DLOM with regime-dependent percentages: which regime are we in?
“Improved” IRR with reinvestment assumptions: which rate?
Factor models: which factors?

What a theory provides:

Falsifiable predictions that data can confirm or reject
Internal consistency — pieces fit together
Generalization to new asset classes without recalibration of every parameter
Welfare implications — can we say what the socially optimal outcome is?

Speaker notes: Be philosophical here. Students from MBA backgrounds often resist theory in favor of “just give me the number.” Make the case for theoretical rigor.



16.4.3 Slide 8.3 — Requirement 1: Stochastic Premia

Title: “Requirement 1: The illiquidity premium must be stochastic”
Why: “Secondary market evidence shows the premium varies 10× the magnitude of its assumed constant” (Session 3 result)
What this rules out:

Standard DCF (constant premium)
DLOM (constant percentage)
Standard CAPM (no liquidity term)

What this requires:

A stochastic process model: OU is the minimal choice
Calibration to time-series data

Speaker notes: This is the empirical requirement. The data demands it.



16.4.4 Slide 8.4 — Requirement 2: Investor Heterogeneity

Title: “Requirement 2: The theory must accommodate investor heterogeneity”
Why: “Different LPs have different liquidity preferences (insurers with long liabilities, endowments with intergenerational concerns, sovereign wealth funds with no horizon)”
What this rules out:

Representative agent models (assumes one LP type)
Most factor models (single discount factor)

What this requires:

Heterogeneous beta in the GE-LAV setting
Distributional dynamics of investor liquidity states

Speaker notes: Heterogeneity is what makes the McKean-Vlasov machinery necessary. One representative LP wouldn’t need it.



16.4.5 Slide 8.5 — Requirement 3: Endogenous Market Clearing

Title: “Requirement 3: Premium must be determined in equilibrium, not assumed”
Why: “The illiquidity premium is what the marginal investor requires to clear the market. Taking it as a fixed parameter ignores the price-setting mechanism.”
What this rules out:

DCF with fixed premium (no market clearing)
Partial equilibrium asset pricing

What this requires:

Fixed-point structure: premium depends on behavior, behavior depends on premium
General equilibrium: simultaneous solution for premium and behavior
McKean-Vlasov mechanism: mathematical formalization

Speaker notes: This is the structural requirement. GE-LAV’s main theoretical contribution.



16.4.6 Slide 8.6 — Requirement 4: Empirical Implementability

Title: “Requirement 4: The theory must be implementable on real data”
Why: “A theory that only works on synthetic data is not a theory of finance”
What this requires:

Calibration procedures with confidence intervals
Robustness checks
Software / platform for practitioners
Reproducibility

GE-LAV implementation:

OU calibration via MLE on Preqin / Setter data
Closed-form Jensen bias formula
Numerical HJB solution for exit boundary
GE-LAV® platform at liquidityillusion.com

Speaker notes: Distinguish GE-LAV from purely academic theories that lack empirical implementation. The platform is a deliberate part of the framework.



16.4.7 Slide 8.7 — Requirement 5: Welfare and Regulatory Support

Title: “Requirement 5: The theory must support welfare and regulatory analysis”
Why: “Private markets are systemically important ($13T AUM); valuation methodology has financial-stability implications”
What this requires:

Ability to compute constrained social optimum (welfare-maximizing allocation given liquidity friction)
Identification of externalities
Corrective instruments (taxes, subsidies, capital charges)

GE-LAV outputs:

Welfare gap: Quantified cost of the McKean-Vlasov externality (~2.3%/year)
Pigouvian exit tax: Optimal regulatory correction
Capital adequacy adjustments: Solvency II / Basel III implications

Speaker notes: This is what elevates GE-LAV from “better valuation method” to “framework with policy implications.” Critical for regulators.



16.4.8 Slide 8.8 — Summary: The Five Requirements

Title: “Five requirements, summarized”
Numbered list:

Stochastic premia (data demands it)
Investor heterogeneity (institutional reality)
Endogenous market clearing (theoretical necessity)
Empirical implementability (engineering requirement)
Welfare and regulatory support (social responsibility)

Speaker notes: This slide is the poster for the rest of the course. Have students photograph it.



16.4.9 Slide 8.9 — Evaluating Alternatives: The Comparison Table

Title: “How alternatives stack up”
Table:












Framework
Stochastic?
Heterogeneous?
Endogenous?
Implementable?
Welfare?




DCF (constant premium)
✗
✗
✗
✓
✗


DLOM
✗
✗
✗
✓
✗


CAPM / LA-CAPM
Partial
✗
✗
✓
✗


Factor models (Pastor-Stambaugh)
✓
✗
✗
✓
✗

✗

Bottom-line: “GE-LAV is the first framework to satisfy all five requirements simultaneously.”
Speaker notes: Don’t oversell. Be respectful of prior frameworks. They were the best available given their time and tools. GE-LAV is the first to have both the conceptual structure and the modern math tools (Itô calculus, MFG theory, computational power).



16.4.10 Slide 8.10 — The GE-LAV Model at a Glance

Title: “GE-LAV in one slide”
Layout: Six components, one box each
Components:

Liquidity process: \(dL_t = \kappa(\bar{L} - L_t)\,dt + \sigma\,dW_t\)
Premium function: \(\pi(L) = \pi_0 - \pi_1 L + \pi_2 L^2\)
Effective discount rate: \(r(L) = r_f + \beta(\text{ERP}) + \pi(L)\)
Valuation operator (LAV): \(V_0 = E^Q[\int_0^T C_t e^{-\int_0^t r(L_s) ds} dt]\)
General equilibrium correction: \(r_{GE-LAV}(L) = r(L) + \text{collective externality term}\)
Welfare instrument: Pigouvian exit tax \(\tau(L)\)

Speaker notes: This is the architecture. Each component gets its own treatment in Unit 3 and onward. Track 2 sees the derivation of each in Sessions 25–29.



16.4.11 Slide 8.11 — The Five Main Results of GE-LAV

Title: “The five results (Ch. 1 preview, restated)”
Numbered:

Jensen Convexity Bias: Closed-form, calibrated 0.8–5.4% across asset classes (Ch. 18)
Optimal Exit Boundary \(L^*(t)\): Smooth-pasting solution to HJB (Ch. 6, 11)
Welfare Gap: Quantified externality cost ~2.3%/year (Ch. 19)
Pigouvian Exit Tax: Welfare-maximizing corrective \(\tau(L)\) (Ch. 19)
Strict Valuation Hierarchy: DCF ⊊ LAV ⊊ GE-LAV proven (Ch. 20)

Speaker notes: Each of these has been mentioned before. Now they’re named. Students should know what each is by Session 24.



16.4.12 Slide 8.12 — Bridge to Unit 3 and the Midterm

Title: “Where we go next”
Session 9: Midterm review (synthesis of Sessions 1–8)
Session 10: Midterm exam
Unit 3 (Sessions 11–18): Building the GE-LAV alternative

Sessions 11–13: Exit timing (Ch. 6)
Sessions 14–15: Portfolio construction (Ch. 7)
Session 16: Regulation and crisis dynamics (Ch. 8)
Sessions 17–18: GE-LAV platform implementation (Ch. 9)

Speaker notes: Unit 1 diagnosed the problem. Unit 2 examined the measurement consequences. Unit 3 builds the solution. This is the conceptual arc.

title: 16  Session 8: What a Correct Theory Must Deliver
text:
16.5 Discussion Questions

Of the five requirements, which do you think is most controversial within the practitioner community? Which is most controversial within the academic community? Why might they differ?
The Pastor-Stambaugh liquidity factor satisfies “stochastic premia” but fails “endogenous market clearing.” Does that matter for using it as a factor in a portfolio? When?
If GE-LAV becomes the regulatory standard for private market valuation, what new operational burdens does it impose on a typical PE fund GP? How would you mitigate those?
crumbs: 16  Session 8: What a Correct Theory Must Deliver

title: 16  Session 8: What a Correct Theory Must Deliver
section: 16.6 Worked Conceptual Example: Evaluating a New Framework
text:
16.6 Worked Conceptual Example: Evaluating a New Framework
Setup: Suppose a new valuation method is proposed called “Adaptive DCF” — it uses a different discount rate depending on which of four manually-defined regimes the analyst declares the market to be in.
Evaluation against the five requirements:

Stochastic? Partial. Discount rate varies across regimes, but not within. No process model.
Heterogeneous? No. Same regime classification for all investors.
Endogenous? No. Regime is exogenously declared, not equilibrium-determined.
Implementable? Yes. Easy to implement; analyst judgment-based.
Welfare? No. No mechanism for welfare analysis or regulatory correction.

Conclusion: Adaptive DCF is an improvement over constant-premium DCF but doesn’t satisfy requirements 2, 3, or 5. It would be acceptable as a transition methodology but not as a long-term framework.
Teaching note: Use this exercise to show students how to evaluate frameworks they encounter in their careers. The five-requirement template is the rubric.
crumbs: 16  Session 8: What a Correct Theory Must Deliver

title: 16  Session 8: What a Correct Theory Must Deliver
text:
16.7 Common Student Confusions (Instructor Notes)

“Aren’t these requirements just GE-LAV-specific?” No — they’re general criteria any theory should meet. GE-LAV happens to satisfy them; that’s the test, not the definition.
“What if a new framework satisfied 4 of 5 but missed welfare? Could that be acceptable?” Depends on use case. For internal LP valuation: probably fine. For regulatory adoption: no, welfare matters.
“Why is welfare requirement 5 and not 1?” Logical ordering — without stochastic premia (1), you can’t even ask welfare questions. The five build on each other.
“Is there a sixth requirement we’re missing?” Plausibly: jump dynamics / tail risk. GE-LAV addresses these in Ch. 13 (Lévy extension) but not as a base requirement. Reasonable critique.

Suggested study sequence: 1. Re-read book Ch. 1 (the conceptual core) 2. Work all PS1 questions one more time 3. Make your formula sheet before the review session — bring it to Session 9 to check 4. Sample exam questions from midterm blueprint

title: 17  Session 9: Midterm Review
text:
17.4 Slide Content Briefs (New Slides Needed)

17.4.1 Slide 9.1 — Title and Midterm Logistics

Title: “Midterm Review”
Logistics box:

Date/Time: Session 10 (next class), 75 minutes
Location: [TBD]
Format: Closed-book, 1 handwritten 8.5×11” double-sided formula sheet
Materials: pen, pencil, eraser, non-programmable calculator, ID
Coverage: Sessions 1–9 (Book Ch. 1–5)

Speaker notes: Get logistics out of the way first. Address questions about format up front.



17.4.2 Slide 9.2 — The Conceptual Arc

Title: “Where we’ve been”
Visual: Linear flow diagram
Flow:

Unit 1 (Sessions 1–5): “DCF is broken”

1: Why valuation matters → 2: Three failures → 3: Secondary market evidence → 4: OU process intuition → 5: Term structure

Unit 2 (Sessions 6–8): “Measurement is also broken”

6: IRR’s biases → 7: PME and LA-IRR/LA-PME → 8: Five requirements


Where we’re going: Unit 3 (Sessions 11–18) → “Build the solution”
Speaker notes: This diagram should already be familiar from the syllabus. The point is to anchor where the midterm sits in the bigger arc.



17.4.3 Slide 9.3 — High-Probability Exam Topics

Title: “Topics most likely on the exam”
Tier 1 (definitely on the exam):

Three structural failures of DCF
Jensen convexity bias — intuition and magnitude
OU process parameters (κ, \(\bar{L}\), σ) and stationary distribution
IRR’s two biases

Tier 2 (very likely):

Valuation hierarchy (DCF ⊊ LAV ⊊ GE-LAV)
Secondary market regimes
PME variants (Kaplan-Schoar, Long-Nickels, Direct Alpha)
Five requirements

Tier 3 (possible):

Term structure of Jensen bias
DLOM vs. GE-LAV
LA-CAPM
Vintage-year effects

Speaker notes: This is your study triage. Master Tier 1 first. Get to Tier 2 if time. Tier 3 is bonus.



17.4.4 Slide 9.4 — Worked Example #1: Short Answer Style

Title: “Type 1 — Short Answer”
Question: “State the GE-LAV stationary distribution of \(L_t\) at calibrated parameters κ = 0.45, σ = 0.32, \(\bar{L} = 1.0\). Show how you compute the variance.”
Answer (worked live):

Stationary distribution: \(L_\infty \sim \mathcal{N}(\bar{L}, \sigma^2/(2\kappa))\)
Mean: 1.0
Variance: \(0.32^2 / (2 \times 0.45) = 0.1024 / 0.9 = 0.1138\)
Std: \(\sqrt{0.1138} = 0.337\)
Distribution: \(L_\infty \sim \mathcal{N}(1.0, 0.1138)\)

Common errors to avoid:

Reporting \(\sigma\) instead of \(\sigma^2/(2\kappa)\)
Forgetting the factor of 2
Mixing up variance and standard deviation

Speaker notes: Walk through this on the board. Have students write it down.



17.4.5 Slide 9.5 — Worked Example #2: Quantitative Problem

Title: “Type 2 — Quantitative Problem”
Question: “Compute the Jensen bias for a 15-year infrastructure asset at \(A = 0.18\%\)/yr and \(C = 0\). Express in dollars for a \(\$200M\) DCF mark.”
Answer:

\(B(T) = A \cdot T + C = 0.18\% \times 15 + 0 = 2.7\%\)
Dollar impact: \(\$200M \times 2.7\% = \$5.4M\)
LAV mark: \(\$200M + \$5.4M = \$205.4M\)
Direction: LAV > DCF (always for Jensen channel)

Speaker notes: Make sure students remember Jensen bias is always positive. This catches them.



17.4.6 Slide 9.6 — Worked Example #3: Conceptual Essay

Title: “Type 3 — Conceptual Essay (200 words)”
Question: “Explain why DCF’s constant-premium assumption fails for private market assets, citing both empirical and theoretical evidence.”
Outline of a strong answer:

Sentence 1: State the failure precisely (the premium varies stochastically)
Sentence 2–3: Empirical evidence (secondary market data 2003–2024; range 0–50%)
Sentence 4–5: Theoretical reason (Jensen’s inequality + McKean-Vlasov endogeneity)
Sentence 6: Magnitude implication (Jensen bias 1–4%; equilibrium correction up to 70%)
Sentence 7: Bottom-line (DCF systematically misvalues; magnitude regime-dependent)

Common errors:

Listing all three failures without depth on any
Forgetting to cite empirical magnitude
Confusing Jensen bias with the equilibrium correction

Speaker notes: Quality > quantity. A focused 150-word answer beats a sprawling 250-word answer.



17.4.7 Slide 9.7 — Worked Example #4: Integrative Case

Title: “Type 4 — Integrative Case”
Quick walkthrough of the sample case from the midterm blueprint (the 2007-vintage trapped LP)
Key steps:


DCF computation: hold-to-liquidation PV


LAV adjustment using \(r_\text{LAV}(L)\)


Decomposition (level vs. convexity)


Qualitative discussion of additional GE-LAV considerations


Time budget on the exam: 30 minutes for this question alone
Speaker notes: Have a printed copy of the sample case for students. Work through (a) and (b) live; assign (c) and (d) for self-study.



17.4.8 Slide 9.8 — Formula Sheet: What to Include

Title: “Building your formula sheet”
Must include:

OU SDE: \(dL_t = \kappa(\bar{L} - L_t) dt + \sigma dW_t\)
OU stationary: \(L_\infty \sim \mathcal{N}(\bar{L}, \sigma^2/(2\kappa))\)
Half-life: \(\ln(2)/\kappa\)
Premium function: \(\pi(L) = \pi_0 - \pi_1 L + \pi_2 L^2\)
DCF rate decomposition: \(r_f + \beta \cdot \text{ERP} + \text{size} + \pi\)
Jensen bias affine form: \(B(T) = AT + C\)
Asset class calibration: A values (0.10–0.18%/yr)
KS PME: \(\sum D_t/B_t / \sum C_t/B_t\)
Five requirements (one-line each)

Speaker notes: This is the essential list. Don’t waste your formula sheet on derivations students need to memorize anyway.



17.4.9 Slide 9.9 — Formula Sheet: What to Omit

Title: “What NOT to put on your formula sheet”
Omit:

General CAPM formulas (presumed prerequisite)
Detailed derivation steps (not on the midterm)
GE-LAV math (Part 2 content; not on midterm)
Calibration numbers you should know cold (κ = 0.45, σ = 0.32)
Worked examples (use the space for formulas instead)

Speaker notes: Density matters. A focused 30-formula sheet beats a chaotic 100-formula sheet.



17.4.10 Slide 9.10 — Study Plan for the Next 48 Hours

Title: “Your 48-hour study plan”
Day 1 (24 hours before exam):

Re-read book Ch. 1 carefully (the conceptual core)
Work all PS1 questions a second time
Finalize formula sheet

Day 2 (12 hours before):

Re-read book Ch. 4–5 (measurement + theory)
Practice all four sample question types
Light review of OU process intuition

Last 6 hours:

One pass through your formula sheet
Light review only — no new content
Sleep

Speaker notes: Stress the sleep point. Tired students perform worse than students who study less but sleep more.

title: 17  Session 9: Midterm Review
text:
17.5 Discussion Questions
For Q&A in the last 5 minutes:

What’s the one concept from Sessions 1–8 you’re least confident about?
Which question type do you find hardest — short answer, quantitative, conceptual, or integrative?
What would you do differently if you could re-take Sessions 1–8?

title: 17  Session 9: Midterm Review
text:
17.6 Common Student Concerns (Instructor Notes)

“How much partial credit is given?” Generous on quantitative problems if work is shown. Strict on short answers (right or wrong). Conceptual essays graded against rubric.
“What if I freeze on the integrative case?” Start with the easiest sub-part (usually (a)). Work outward. Even partial answers on (b), (c), (d) earn credit.
“Can I bring a typed formula sheet?” No — must be handwritten. The act of writing it is part of the study process.
“Is GE-LAV math on the midterm?” No. Only intuition and applications. The math is reserved for Track 2 in Sessions 25–31.

title: 17  Session 9: Midterm Review
text:
17.7 What to Expect Next Session
Session 10 is the midterm exam. Arrive 10 minutes early. Bring all required materials. Power off phones.
After the midterm, Unit 3 begins with exit timing, portfolio construction, regulation, and platform implementation. The shift from “what’s wrong with DCF” to “what does GE-LAV deliver” begins.

title: 18  Session 10: Midterm Exam
text:
18.2 During the Exam

18.2.1 Strategy advice

Triage first. Read all questions before starting. Identify the easiest and start there.
Show your work. On quantitative problems, partial credit requires visible reasoning. Final answer alone earns ~40% if correct.
Time discipline. If you’ve spent 8 minutes on a 9-point quantitative problem, move on. Return if time permits.
Conceptual essays: Outline before writing. Quality > quantity. A focused 150-word answer beats a sprawling 250-word answer.
Integrative case: Start with (a). Even partial answers on (b)–(d) earn credit. Don’t lose all 20 points by getting stuck on (a).



18.2.2 If you finish early

Re-read your answers
Double-check arithmetic on quantitative problems
Verify essay structure addresses all parts of the question
Do not leave early — there is no benefit, and you may catch errors



18.2.3 If you run out of time

Outline answers you couldn’t complete
Even bullet points for an essay earn partial credit
Show setup on quantitative problems you didn’t finish — the setup is worth points

title: 18  Session 10: Midterm Exam
text:
18.3 Academic Integrity
Standard university academic integrity rules apply. Specifically:

No looking at other students’ papers
No communication with other students during the exam
No use of phones, smart watches, or any electronic device beyond the calculator
No leaving the room with the exam (bathroom breaks permitted with proctor escort)

Violations result in F for the course and referral to the academic conduct office.

title: 18  Session 10: Midterm Exam
text:
18.4 After the Exam

Solutions: Posted to course site 24 hours after the exam window closes
Grades: Returned within 7 days
Re-grade requests: Submit in writing within 7 days of grade posting, citing specific question(s) and the basis for re-grade
Office hours: Standard schedule resumes Session 11; instructor available for exam debriefs

title: 18  Session 10: Midterm Exam

Session 11 begins Unit 3: Decision and Application. The pivot from “what’s wrong with DCF” to “what does GE-LAV deliver.” Session 11 introduces the exit timing problem — the optimal stopping decision for an LP holding an illiquid asset.
Reading for Session 11: Book Chapter 6, sections 6.1–6.4 (~10 pages).

title: 19  Session 11: Exit Timing · The Trapped Investor Problem
text:
19.4 Slide Content Briefs (New Slides Needed)

19.4.1 Slide 11.1 — Title and Unit 3 Setup

Title: “Exit Timing · The Trapped Investor”
Tag line: “Unit 3 begins: from diagnosis to decision”
Unit 3 preview: “Sessions 11–18 build the GE-LAV alternative: exit timing, portfolio, regulation, implementation”
Speaker notes: Pivot moment. Less critique, more building.



19.4.2 Slide 11.2 — The Exit Decision in Practice

Title: “What an LP actually faces”
Setup: “An LP holds a 7-year PE position currently 4 years in. NAV reported by GP: $50M. Recent secondary market bid: $42M (16% discount). What should they do?”
Standard practice:

Most LPs hold to natural liquidation (no decision)
Some LPs sell to secondaries when forced (denominator effect, liquidity needs)
Very few LPs systematically optimize the exit decision

GE-LAV question: “What’s the optimal policy as a function of \(L_t\)?”
Speaker notes: Frame the gap between practice and theory. Most LPs don’t optimize because they don’t have the framework.



19.4.3 Slide 11.3 — The Optimal Stopping Formulation

Title: “Exit as an optimal stopping problem”
Setup:

At any time \(t\), the LP can:

Hold: receive future distributions per the GP plan
Exit: sell into the secondary market at price \(P_\text{secondary}(L_t)\)

The LP wants to maximize expected discounted value

Value function: \(V(L, t) = \max\{ P_\text{secondary}(L), E[V_\text{hold}(L_t, t)] \}\)
Intuition: “Hold if expected hold value exceeds secondary price; otherwise exit”
Speaker notes: This is a textbook American option style problem. Students with derivatives background will recognize the structure.



19.4.4 Slide 11.4 — The Exit Boundary \(L^*(t)\) (Preview)

Title: “There is an optimal \(L^*\) — and we can compute it”
Setup:

For each time \(t\) remaining, there’s a critical liquidity level \(L^*(t)\)
Above \(L^*\): Hold (better expected value from waiting)
Below \(L^*\): Exit (secondary price exceeds expected hold value)

Boundary shape: \(L^*(t)\) rises as \(t \to T\) (near maturity, only marginally stressed liquidity should trigger exit)
Calibrated benchmark: “For typical mid-life PE position, \(L^* \approx -1.0\) — moderate stress”
Speaker notes: Numerical computation in Session 12. Today is conceptual.



19.4.5 Slide 11.5 — Smooth Pasting (Intuition Only)

Title: “Why \(L^*\) is special: smooth pasting”
Concept:

At \(L^*\), the value of holding equals the value of exiting
At \(L^*\), the slope of the hold value with respect to \(L\) also equals the slope of the exit value
This “smooth pasting” condition determines \(L^*\) uniquely

Visual: Two curves (hold value, exit value) meeting tangentially at \(L^*\)
Track 2 note: “Full derivation in Session 25 — for now, accept it as the optimality condition”
Speaker notes: Don’t derive. Just establish that smooth pasting is how we compute \(L^*\). Track 2 students will see this for real with HJB and variational inequalities.



19.4.6 Slide 11.6 — The Four Trapped Investor Scenarios

Title: “When an LP is ‘trapped’”
Definition: “Trapped = unable to exit at fair value despite wanting to exit”
Four scenarios:

Liquidity stress: \(L_t \ll L^*\), secondary market discounts widen beyond LAV value → exit destroys value
Denominator effect: Public market decline forces PE % allocation above policy limit → LP must sell at bad time
Regulatory constraint: Solvency II / Basel capital charge rises with stress → insurer must reduce private exposure
Funding obligation: LP needs cash to meet pension payouts or other liabilities → must sell regardless of price

Speaker notes: Scenarios 2–4 are forced exits; scenario 1 is value-destroying voluntary exit. Both create trapped behavior.

title: 19  Session 11: Exit Timing · The Trapped Investor Problem
text:
19.6 Discussion Questions

CalPERS publicly sold PE secondaries at significant discounts in 2009. Were they “trapped” by scenario 1, 2, 3, or 4 from today’s slide? Could they have avoided the discount through better planning?
The IPEV valuation guidelines suggest GPs should “hold to liquidation when feasible.” Does this guideline implicitly assume scenario 1 doesn’t exist? What would the guideline say if it acknowledged GE-LAV?
A pension fund’s actuarial assumption is 7% expected return on private markets. If GE-LAV-optimal exit timing reduces realized returns by ~50 bps relative to “hold to liquidation,” is the actuarial assumption violated? Or is the realized return improvement on a risk-adjusted basis still positive?
crumbs: 19  Session 11: Exit Timing · The Trapped Investor Problem

title: 19  Session 11: Exit Timing · The Trapped Investor Problem
section: 19.7 Worked Numerical Example: Hold vs. Exit Decision
text:
19.7 Worked Numerical Example: Hold vs. Exit Decision
Setup: An LP holds a 5-year remaining PE position. Reported NAV: $100M. Current liquidity state: \(L_t = -0.8\) (moderately stressed). OU parameters: κ = 0.45, σ = 0.32, \(\bar{L} = 1.0\).
Step 1: Expected hold value
Under OU dynamics, \(E[L_{t+5}] = \bar{L} + (L_t - \bar{L}) e^{-5\kappa} = 1.0 + (-0.8 - 1.0) \times e^{-2.25} = 1.0 + (-1.8)(0.105) = 0.81\)
The expected liquidity state recovers from −0.8 toward \(\bar{L} = 1.0\) over 5 years, ending at +0.81.
Using \(\pi(L) = 0.045 - 0.025L + 0.021L^2\): - Average premium over 5-year horizon ≈ \(\pi(0.0) = 0.045 - 0 + 0 = 4.5\%\) (rough average of current and future) - More carefully: integrate path; result ~ 4.0% effective
Hold value (Jensen-adjusted LAV): \(V_\text{hold} \approx \$100M \times (1 + \text{Jensen bias at 5 years})\) With \(A = 0.16\%\)/yr (buyout): \(B(5) = 0.8\%\) \(V_\text{hold} \approx \$100.8M\)
Step 2: Current secondary price
At \(L_t = -0.8\), secondary discount calibration suggests a 20% discount to NAV: \(P_\text{secondary} = \$100M \times (1 - 0.20) = \$80M\)
Step 3: Compare
\(V_\text{hold} = \$100.8M\) vs. \(P_\text{secondary} = \$80M\)
Decision: Hold. The mean-reversion benefit (≈$20M expected over 5 years) exceeds any liquidity insurance value at this stress level.
Step 4: Where would exit make sense?
If \(L_t\) dropped to \(-1.5\) (GFC depth) and secondary discount widened to 40%: - \(P_\text{secondary} = \$60M\) - But \(V_\text{hold}\) also drops (higher expected premium during recovery period) — maybe to ~\(\$70M\) - At \(L_t = -1.5\), hold still slightly dominates exit for a 5-year position - For shorter remaining horizons (e.g., 2 years), the trade flips: exit becomes optimal because there’s less time for mean reversion
Teaching note: Walk through this on the board. Students should leave with a quantitative feel for when exit makes sense.
crumbs: 19  Session 11: Exit Timing · The Trapped Investor Problem

title: 19  Session 11: Exit Timing · The Trapped Investor Problem
text:
19.8 Common Student Confusions (Instructor Notes)

“Why is mean reversion the dominant force here?” Because \(\kappa = 0.45\)/yr is moderately fast — over 5 years, \(L_t\) reverts substantially toward \(\bar{L}\). Short positions don’t have this luxury.
“What if I think the secondary market is underpricing my position?” That’s exactly what the hold-vs-exit calculation tests. If \(V_\text{hold} > P_\text{secondary}\), you agree the secondary is underpricing, and you hold.
“How is this different from American option exercise?” Mathematically very similar. Both use optimal stopping. Difference: PE has more complex cash flow structure (J-curve, distributions over time, not a single payoff).
“Does the GP have a role in the LP’s exit decision?” No directly — secondary market transactions don’t require GP consent under most LPAs. But GPs often have right of first refusal, and GP attitude affects valuation. Practical complication beyond pure theory.
crumbs: 19  Session 11: Exit Timing · The Trapped Investor Problem

text:

text:
20.4 Slide Content Briefs (New Slides Needed)

20.4.1 Slide 12.1 — Title and Today’s Goal

Title: “The Exit Boundary \(L^*(t)\) in Practice”
Tag line: “Yesterday: the structure. Today: the numbers.”
Speaker notes: Today is operational. Students should leave able to look at a position and identify the exit recommendation.



20.4.2 Slide 12.2 — The Exit Boundary Chart (Anchor Visual)

Title: “The GE-LAV exit boundary”
Visual: Master chart showing \(L^*(t)\) as a function of remaining time-to-maturity \(T - t\)
Key features to annotate:

X-axis: years remaining (0 to 15)
Y-axis: critical liquidity state \(L^*\)
Boundary curve: rises from approximately \(L^* = -2.0\) at \(T - t = 10\)+ years, up to \(L^* \approx -0.5\) at \(T - t = 1\) year, to \(L^* \approx 0\) near maturity
Hold region: Above the curve
Exit region: Below the curve

Calibrated benchmark numbers (table):




Remaining T
\(L^*\)




10 yr
−1.8


7 yr
−1.5


5 yr

3 yr

1 yr
−0.5


0.5 yr
−0.2




Speaker notes: This chart is the operational tool. Spend 10+ minutes on it.



20.4.3 Slide 12.3 — Why \(L^*(t)\) Rises as \(t \to T\)

Title: “The shape of \(L^*(t)\), intuitively”
Three reasons:

Less time for mean reversion: Near maturity, even small stress can’t be recovered before forced exit at terminal date
Smaller Jensen benefit: Jensen bias scales with \(T\) — less to gain by holding short-term
Asymmetric payoff: Distress risk dominates upside as horizon shrinks

Visual: Three small annotations on the master chart
Speaker notes: Connect to Black-Scholes intuition — option time value decays as expiry approaches. Same principle.



20.4.4 Slide 12.4 — Asset Class Differences

Title: “Exit boundaries vary by asset class”
Table or overlaid chart:




Asset Class
\(L^*\) at 5 years remaining




PE Buyout
−1.2


Growth Equity
−1.1


Real Estate
−1.4


Infrastructure
−1.6


Private Credit
−0.9




Explanation of pattern:

Infrastructure has the most negative \(L^*\) → most willing to hold through stress (long mean-reversion runway, stable cash flows)
Private credit has the least negative \(L^*\) → quickest to exit (short duration, less Jensen benefit to hold)

Speaker notes: Asset class matters for the boundary. This is why portfolio-level exit policy needs asset-class differentiation.



20.4.5 Slide 12.5 — Calibrated Exit Boundary Performance (Class 13 Reuse)

Title: “Historical performance of the GE-LAV exit rule”
Setup: “If LPs had applied the GE-LAV exit boundary 1990–2024, what would have happened?”
