title: 28  Session 20: Stochastic Control · HJB Equation (Concepts Only)
text:
28.4 Slide Content Briefs (All New Slides)

28.4.1 Slide 20.1 — Title

Title: “Stochastic Control and the HJB Equation”
Subtitle: “How GE-LAV computes the optimal exit boundary · Concepts only · Track 2 derivation in Session 25”
Speaker notes: Session 19 was about describing stochastic processes. Today is about making optimal decisions under stochastic uncertainty.



28.4.2 Slide 20.2 — The Optimal Control Problem

Title: “What stochastic control solves”
The setup:

System evolves stochastically: \(dX_t = \mu(X_t, u_t) dt + \sigma(X_t, u_t) dW_t\)
At every time \(t\), choose action \(u_t\)
Receive running reward \(f(X_t, u_t)\) over time
At terminal time \(T\), receive terminal reward \(g(X_T)\)

Goal: Choose action sequence to maximize expected total reward

\(V(x, t) = \max_{u} E\left[\int_t^T f(X_s, u_s) ds + g(X_T) \,\big|\, X_t = x\right]\)

Speaker notes: This abstract framework is what the LP exit decision is doing. Make the connection explicit.



28.4.3 Slide 20.3 — Examples of Stochastic Control

Title: “Stochastic control is everywhere”
Examples in finance:

Merton portfolio: Choose allocation \(u_t\) to maximize expected utility of terminal wealth
Black-Scholes: Choose hedging strategy to replicate option payoff
GE-LAV exit: Choose exit time to maximize expected discounted value
Optimal execution: Choose trading rate to minimize impact cost

Examples outside finance:

Inventory management: Choose ordering to minimize total cost
Renewable energy: Choose battery dispatch to maximize revenue
Aerospace: Trajectory optimization for spacecraft

Speaker notes: Show breadth. Same math, many domains.



28.4.4 Slide 20.4 — The Value Function

Title: “The value function \(V(x, t)\)”
Definition: “Maximum expected reward attainable starting from state \(x\) at time \(t\), under the optimal policy”
Why we want this:

Knowing \(V\) tells us the value of being in state \(x\)
Differentiating \(V\) tells us the optimal action
\(V\) summarizes all future decisions in a single function

Key property:

\(V\) depends only on current state — past doesn’t matter (Markov property)
This is why dynamic programming works

Speaker notes: Establish \(V\) as the central object. Everything else is computing it.



28.4.5 Slide 20.5 — Dynamic Programming (Bellman’s Insight)

Title: “Bellman’s principle of optimality”
The insight (paraphrased): “An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision.”
In formula: \(V(x, t) = \max_u \left[ f(x, u) \Delta t + E[V(X_{t+\Delta t}, t+\Delta t) | X_t = x, u_t = u] \right]\)
Translation: “Best total = best current + best continuation”
Why it matters: Reduces a problem of “choosing infinite future actions” to a problem of “choosing one action and trusting the value function”
Speaker notes: Bellman’s name and idea is worth establishing. Many students have heard “dynamic programming” without knowing where it came from.



28.4.6 Slide 20.6 — From Bellman to HJB

Title: “From discrete to continuous: the HJB equation”
Bellman’s equation (discrete time): \(V(x, t) = \max_u [f \Delta t + E[V(X_{t+\Delta t})] ]\)
Take \(\Delta t \to 0\) and apply Itô’s lemma to \(E[V(X_{t+\Delta t})]\):

The result is a partial differential equation (PDE) for \(V(x, t)\)

The Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation:

\(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \max_u \left[ f(x, u) + \mu(x, u) \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x} + \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma^2(x, u) \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} \right] = 0\)

What this says:

The first term is time-decay of value
The bracketed max is the Hamiltonian — a function of current state and action
The whole equation must hold at every \((x, t)\)

Speaker notes: Don’t dwell on the formula. Walk through it term by term — drift effect, diffusion effect, time decay, action choice. Track 2 students will see derivation; Track 1 students need the shape only.



28.4.7 Slide 20.7 — The HJB in Words

Title: “What the HJB equation says, in words”
Plain English: “At every state and time, the time decay of value equals the expected change in value due to drift and diffusion, plus the maximum gain obtainable from the current action.”
In a sentence: “Value evolves consistently with the optimal action.”
Mathematical interpretation:

It’s a constraint on the value function — not a closed-form solution
Solving it (analytically or numerically) gives \(V\) and simultaneously the optimal action \(u^*(x, t)\)

Speaker notes: Students will not need to solve HJB equations. They need to know: “There’s a PDE the value function satisfies, and solving it gives both value and policy.”



28.4.8 Slide 20.8 — Solving HJB Numerically

Title: “How HJB is solved in practice”
Methods:

Finite differences (most common for low-dimensional state spaces, like GE-LAV)
Spectral methods (for very smooth problems)
Machine learning approximation (recent trend; neural networks for high-dimensional state)

For GE-LAV exit problem:

State space: \((L, t)\) — 2-dimensional
Standard finite-difference grid
Solution time: seconds on a laptop

Speaker notes: Demystify. HJB is solvable. The platform does this routinely.



28.4.9 Slide 20.9 — Application: The LP Exit Problem (Revisited)

Title: “Revisiting the LP exit problem from Sessions 11–13”
The control problem (now formalized):

State: \(L_t\) (liquidity state)
Action: continue holding OR exit (binary)
Payoff from exiting at time \(t\): \(P_\text{secondary}(L_t)\)
Payoff from continuing: expected future distributions discounted at \(r(L)\)

HJB equation here is an obstacle problem:

\(V\) must satisfy the PDE in the “continuation region”
\(V\) equals the exit payoff in the “stopping region”
\(V\) matches smoothly at the boundary between regions

Speaker notes: Connect to Sessions 11–13. They’ve seen the result (exit boundary); now they see the math that produced it.



28.4.10 Slide 20.10 — Smooth Pasting (Properly Now)

Title: “The smooth pasting condition”
Setup: At the exit boundary \(L^*(t)\):

Value match: \(V(L^*, t) = P_\text{secondary}(L^*)\) — values equal
Smooth pasting: \(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial L}\Big|_{L^*} = \dfrac{dP_\text{secondary}}{dL}\Big|_{L^*}\) — slopes equal

Why slopes must match: “If the value function ‘kinked’ at the boundary, you could improve by moving the boundary.”
Visual: Two curves meeting tangentially (the canonical free-boundary picture)
Speaker notes: This is the mathematical characterization of the boundary. Session 12’s exit boundary chart is the output of smooth pasting.



28.4.11 Slide 20.11 — Numerical Output

Title: “What you get from solving HJB”
Two outputs:

Value function \(V(L, t)\): The maximum expected discounted value from state \((L, t)\)
Optimal policy \(u^*(L, t)\): The optimal action at each \((L, t)\)

For exit problem:

Value function gives the hold value (and equals exit payoff in stopping region)
Policy is binary: HOLD if \(L > L^*(t)\), EXIT otherwise
The boundary \(L^*(t)\) is implicitly defined by smooth pasting

Connecting back: “Sessions 11–13 showed the \(L^*(t)\) chart. That chart is the output of the HJB solution.”
Speaker notes: Close the loop. Students should see today’s math as the machinery underlying earlier results.



28.4.12 Slide 20.12 — HJB Beyond Exit Timing

Title: “HJB is everywhere in GE-LAV”
Other applications in the framework:

Portfolio allocation: HJB determines the optimal \(w^*(L, t)\) — the curve we saw in Session 14
Pacing decisions: HJB on a multi-dimensional state including unfunded commitments
Optimal Pigouvian tax: Inverse HJB — what tax achieves the welfare-maximum?

GE-LAV uses HJB at every decision layer. This isn’t just one application — it’s the engine of the optimal-decision side of the framework.
Speaker notes: Plant the seed that we’ll see HJB again. Session 25 (Track 2) derives the GE-LAV HJB from scratch; Track 1 PSets ask intuition questions about HJB outputs.



28.4.13 Slide 20.13 — Limitations of HJB Approach

Title: “When HJB doesn’t work”
Three challenges:

Curse of dimensionality: State space dimension matters. 2D (GE-LAV exit) is easy. 10+ dimensions becomes hard.
Non-Markovian dynamics: HJB assumes Markov property. Memory effects break the standard formulation.
Discontinuous policies: Smooth pasting requires some regularity. If exit payoffs are jumpy, complications arise.

GE-LAV is well-suited: Low-dimensional state \((L_t)\), OU process is Markovian, exit payoff is smooth.
Speaker notes: Honest about scope. The framework works precisely because the GE-LAV problem fits HJB’s assumptions.



28.4.14 Slide 20.14 — Bridge to Mean-Field Games

Title: “Where we go next: when everyone is solving HJB”
The HJB so far:

Single LP solving its own optimal exit problem
Taking the market (secondary discounts) as given

The McKean-Vlasov twist:

Many LPs all solving their own exit problems simultaneously
Their collective exit decisions move the secondary market
Each LP’s optimal decision depends on what other LPs decide

This is mean-field games (MFG): Session 21 covers this concept.
Speaker notes: Tease Session 21. The framework gets richer when we add the collective layer.



28.4.15 Slide 20.15 — Summary

Title: “Today in one sentence”
Statement: “Stochastic control formalizes the LP’s decision problem; the HJB equation is the consistency requirement on the value function; smooth pasting is the boundary condition that determines the exit threshold.”
You can now:

Read \(L^*(t)\) charts knowing where they come from
Discuss the framework’s decision layer with rigor
Recognize HJB applications in other areas of finance

Speaker notes: Reinforce the conceptual progression: process → control → equilibrium.
crumbs: 28  Session 20: Stochastic Control · HJB Equation (Concepts Only)

title: 28  Session 20: Stochastic Control · HJB Equation (Concepts Only)
text:
28.5 Discussion Questions

The HJB equation is a PDE. Most LPs don’t solve PDEs. Is the framework’s reliance on this math a barrier to adoption? How can it be mitigated?
Real LPs face liquidity, regulatory, tax, and behavioral constraints in addition to the value-maximization problem. How does HJB extend to handle these?
The smooth pasting condition assumes the exit payoff is smooth. What if the secondary market has discrete pricing tiers (e.g., -5%, -10%, -15% bid levels) creating jumps? Does the boundary still exist?
crumbs: 28  Session 20: Stochastic Control · HJB Equation (Concepts Only)

title: 28  Session 20: Stochastic Control · HJB Equation (Concepts Only)
section: 28.6 Worked Example: Sketch of the GE-LAV HJB
text:
28.6 Worked Example: Sketch of the GE-LAV HJB
The GE-LAV exit problem in HJB form:
State: \(L_t\). Time horizon: \([0, T]\). Liquidity dynamics: \(dL_t = \kappa(\bar{L} - L_t) dt + \sigma_L dW_t\).
Value function: \(V(L, t)\) = expected discounted hold value from \((L, t)\).
HJB equation (continuation region \(L > L^*(t)\)):
\(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \kappa(\bar{L} - L) \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial L} + \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma_L^2 \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial L^2} - r(L) V + C(L, t) = 0\)
Where \(C(L, t)\) is the expected cash flow rate from the position.
Terminal condition: $V(L, T) = $ terminal payoff (final distribution).
Boundary conditions at the free boundary \(L^*(t)\): - \(V(L^*, t) = P_\text{secondary}(L^*)\) (value match) - \(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial L}\Big|_{L^*} = \dfrac{dP_\text{secondary}}{dL}\Big|_{L^*}\) (smooth pasting)
Numerical solution:
Discretize \(L\) on a grid, \(t\) on a time-step grid. Back-step from \(t = T\) to \(t = 0\) using finite differences. At each step: - Compute \(V\) assuming continuation - Compare to \(P_\text{secondary}(L)\) - Take maximum (American option style) - Record the boundary where the max switches
Output: The \(L^*(t)\) chart from Session 12.
Teaching note: Don’t expect students to do this calculation. The point is to see that the exit boundary comes from a well-defined PDE.
crumbs: 28  Session 20: Stochastic Control · HJB Equation (Concepts Only)

title: 28  Session 20: Stochastic Control · HJB Equation (Concepts Only)
text:
28.7 Common Student Confusions (Instructor Notes)

“Why does the HJB require both value match and smooth pasting? Isn’t one enough?” Value match locates a candidate boundary. Smooth pasting picks the optimal boundary among candidates. Without smooth pasting, you could “improve” by moving the boundary.
“What if I’m not maximizing utility — I just want to minimize tail risk?” HJB still applies. Replace “expected reward” with whatever objective. The math is identical.
“Curse of dimensionality sounds bad. Does GE-LAV scale to portfolios?” Single-asset GE-LAV is 2D (\(L, t\)). Multi-asset GE-LAV is higher-dimensional. Approximations and decomposition tricks are needed. Active research area.
“Why is the HJB called Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman?” Hamilton: classical mechanics (1830s). Jacobi: PDE theory. Bellman: dynamic programming (1950s). Three independent developments converged on the same math.
crumbs: 28  Session 20: Stochastic Control · HJB Equation (Concepts Only)

title: 28  Session 20: Stochastic Control · HJB Equation (Concepts Only)
text:
28.8 What to Expect Next Session
Session 21 covers mean-field games (MFG) — the framework for when many agents are each solving their own HJB and influencing the aggregate state. This is the general equilibrium layer of GE-LAV.
Key concepts: - McKean-Vlasov SDEs (the OU process when drift depends on the population distribution) - The fixed-point structure of MFG equilibrium - The collective externality and why it matters for private market valuation
Reading: Book Chapter 13 (concepts only).

title: 29  Session 21: Mean-Field Games (Concepts Only)
text:
29.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, students will be able to:

title: 29  Session 21: Mean-Field Games (Concepts Only)
text:
State the key challenge of mean-field games: each agent optimizes, but the optimum depends on what other agents do.
Define a McKean-Vlasov SDE in plain language and recognize the McKean-Vlasov coupling term.
Articulate the fixed-point structure of MFG equilibrium: distribution → individual optimum → aggregate distribution.
Explain the collective externality in private markets — why individual LP exit decisions create costs for all LPs.
Connect MFG to game-theoretic frameworks students may know (Nash equilibrium, congestion games, mechanism design).

title: 29  Session 21: Mean-Field Games (Concepts Only)
text:

text:

Format

title: 29  Session 21: Mean-Field Games (Concepts Only)
text:
29.4 Slide Content Briefs (All New Slides)

29.4.1 Slide 21.1 — Title

Title: “Mean-Field Games”
Subtitle: “When everyone solves HJB simultaneously · Concepts only · Track 2 derivation in Session 26”
Speaker notes: This is the conceptually hardest session in Unit 4. Pace carefully.



29.4.2 Slide 21.2 — The Problem MFG Solves

Title: “Two LPs in the same market”
Setup:

LP A is solving its own HJB to decide when to exit
LP B is also solving its own HJB at the same time
But: the secondary market price depends on how many LPs are trying to exit
So LP A’s optimal decision depends on what LP B (and 1,000 other LPs) is doing

The challenge: “Standard HJB takes the environment as given. But the environment is the other agents’ decisions.”
Speaker notes: Set up the puzzle. Students should feel the conceptual challenge.



29.4.3 Slide 21.3 — Why Nash Equilibrium Isn’t Quite Right

Title: “Why standard Nash doesn’t work”
Nash equilibrium: Each player chooses best response given others’ strategies, and the strategies are mutually consistent
Problem in finance: “There are thousands of LPs. Standard Nash requires tracking each LP’s individual strategy. Computationally infeasible.”
The mean-field idea: “When there are many agents, replace ‘what each individual does’ with ‘what the distribution of agents does.’”
Result: “Each agent reacts to the aggregate distribution; the aggregate is determined by individual choices.”
Speaker notes: Connect to standard game theory most students know.



29.4.4 Slide 21.4 — The McKean-Vlasov SDE

Title: “McKean-Vlasov SDEs: SDEs with population coupling”
Standard SDE: \(dL_t = \mu(L_t, t) dt + \sigma(L_t, t) dW_t\)
McKean-Vlasov SDE: \(dL_t = \mu(L_t, t, \mu_t) dt + \sigma(L_t, t, \mu_t) dW_t\)
The new ingredient: \(\mu_t\) = probability distribution of the population’s state at time \(t\)
Key property: “Drift and/or diffusion depend on the distribution of the population, not just the individual state”
In GE-LAV:

\(L_t\) = liquidity state of a representative LP
\(\mu_t\) = distribution of liquidity states across all LPs
The equilibrium secondary market price depends on \(\mu_t\)

Speaker notes: Don’t expect students to internalize the McKean-Vlasov form. Just establish that there’s a distribution-coupled version of the SDE.



29.4.5 Slide 21.5 — Why “McKean-Vlasov”

Title: “Where the name comes from”
Background:

Anatoly Vlasov (1938): Plasma physics — describing many-particle systems
Henry McKean (1966): Mathematical formalization for stochastic systems
Mean-field game theory (2006+): Lasry & Lions, Carmona & Delarue — modern formulation

Connection: “GE-LAV is not the first application of MFG to finance. Recent applications include systemic risk, optimal execution, herd behavior in trading.”
Speaker notes: Establish intellectual lineage. The framework isn’t ad hoc.



29.4.6 Slide 21.6 — The Fixed-Point Structure

Title: “MFG equilibrium is a fixed point”
The logic (sequential):

Suppose population distribution \(\mu_t\) is given
Each LP solves its individual HJB given this \(\mu_t\) → optimal policy \(u^*(L, t, \mu_t)\)
Apply the optimal policy across the population → updated distribution \(\tilde{\mu}_t\)
If \(\tilde{\mu}_t = \mu_t\) → MFG equilibrium

Visual: Loop diagram — distribution → optimization → distribution
Existence question: Does this fixed point always exist? Sometimes. Under what conditions? See Theorem 13.2 in book (Schauder fixed-point theorem)
Uniqueness question: Can there be multiple equilibria? Yes, in general. GE-LAV proves uniqueness under a stability condition (mean reversion \(\kappa\) exceeds coupling \(\gamma\)).
Speaker notes: This is the abstract core of MFG. Make sure students see the fixed-point structure visually.



29.4.7 Slide 21.7 — The Stability Condition

Title: “When MFG equilibrium is well-behaved”
Stability condition (GE-LAV calibration):

Mean reversion speed \(\kappa = 0.45\)/yr
Mean-field coupling strength \(\gamma\) (small, calibrated)
Condition: \(\kappa > \gamma\) → equilibrium exists and is stable

What violates stability:

If coupling is too strong, multiple equilibria possible (markets can “panic”)
In extreme stress, the framework’s basic stability assumption may break — Ch 19 covers this

GE-LAV is calibrated to be in the stable regime: “Empirically, \(\kappa = 0.45\) exceeds reasonable \(\gamma\) estimates by 3-5×”
Speaker notes: This is a known limitation. In a true panic (worse than GFC), the framework may break. Worth flagging.



29.4.8 Slide 21.8 — The Collective Externality

Title: “Why MFG matters: the collective externality”
The setup:

LP A decides to exit secondary
LP A’s exit slightly increases supply on secondary market
This slightly widens discount for all other LPs trying to exit
LP A doesn’t internalize this small effect on others

Aggregate:

When many LPs exit, the cumulative effect on prices is large
Each LP only sees their own benefit; nobody internalizes the collective cost

This is the McKean-Vlasov externality — the central pricing distortion in GE-LAV
Speaker notes: This is the policy story. Pigouvian tax (Session 24) corrects this externality.



29.4.9 Slide 21.9 — Quantifying the Externality

Title: “How big is the externality?”
The welfare gap:

Difference between aggregate welfare under decentralized exit decisions vs. socially-optimal exit decisions
Calibrated magnitude: ΔW ≈ 2.3% per year
In dollars: Roughly $300B/year in aggregate welfare loss across the $13T private capital market

Where the loss is concentrated:

Stress regimes (the externality is small in calm markets)
Long-duration assets (more time for the externality to accumulate)
Smaller LPs (most affected by widened discounts)

Speaker notes: Make the dollar magnitude visceral. $300B/year is a real number.



29.4.10 Slide 21.10 — Solving the MFG (Numerically)

Title: “How to solve a McKean-Vlasov MFG”
Iterative method:

Start with a guess for the population distribution \(\mu_t^{(0)}\)
Solve the individual HJB given \(\mu_t^{(0)}\) → optimal policy
Simulate the population under that policy → updated \(\mu_t^{(1)}\)
Check convergence: if \(|\mu_t^{(0)} - \mu_t^{(1)}|\) is small, equilibrium found; else iterate

For GE-LAV:

Typically converges in 10–20 iterations
Computational cost: minutes on a laptop
The GE-LAV platform does this automatically

Speaker notes: Don’t expect students to implement. Just establish that solving MFG is computationally tractable.



29.4.11 Slide 21.11 — Propagation of Chaos

Title: “Why mean-field approximation is good”
Setup:

We pretend each LP only cares about the distribution \(\mu_t\), not about individual other LPs
Is this approximation valid when there are 100 LPs? 1,000? 10,000?

Propagation of chaos (informal): “As the number of LPs \(N \to \infty\), individual LP outcomes become statistically independent of each other, conditional on the population distribution”
For finite \(N\):

Error scales as \(1/\sqrt{N}\)
At \(N = 100\) LPs: error ~10%
At \(N = 1000\) LPs: error ~3%
At \(N = 10000\) LPs: error <1%

In private markets: “There are ~10,000 LPs in major institutional private markets. Mean-field is a very good approximation.”
Speaker notes: Justify the approximation empirically. The framework’s assumptions aren’t wild.



29.4.12 Slide 21.12 — MFG in Other Finance Contexts

Title: “Where else MFG shows up”
Recent finance applications:

Optimal execution: When many traders work the same order, MFG-aware execution is better
Systemic risk: Bank networks with stress propagation
Robo-advisors: Aggregated retail behavior creating market patterns
Decentralized finance (DeFi): Liquidity provider rebalancing in AMMs

GE-LAV is the first application to private market valuation. This is the framework’s novel contribution.
Speaker notes: Validation by analogy. MFG is established in finance; private markets is a fresh application.



29.4.13 Slide 21.13 — What Track 1 Needs to Know

Title: “Track 1: takeaways for application”
You don’t need to:

Solve McKean-Vlasov SDEs
Derive the fixed-point existence theorem
Implement MFG iteration algorithms

You do need to know:

MFG explains why GE-LAV’s equilibrium correction is different from DCF
The collective externality is real and quantifiable (~2.3%/yr welfare loss)
Pigouvian tax (next session) corrects this externality
When recommending portfolio decisions, you’re navigating a system where everyone’s decisions interact

Speaker notes: Track 1 students should walk away feeling competent without feeling overwhelmed.



29.4.14 Slide 21.14 — What Track 2 Will Build

Title: “Track 2: what Session 26 will derive”
Track 2 in Session 26 will:

Set up the McKean-Vlasov SDE for GE-LAV liquidity dynamics
Prove existence of MFG equilibrium via Schauder fixed-point theorem
Prove uniqueness under the stability condition \(\kappa > \gamma\)
Quantify the propagation-of-chaos rate of convergence
Implement the MFG fixed-point iteration numerically

Recommended pre-reading for Track 2:

Carmona & Delarue, Probabilistic Theory of Mean-Field Games, Ch. 1–2

Speaker notes: Track 2 students should leave knowing they have substantial work ahead.



29.4.15 Slide 21.15 — Bridge to Session 22

Title: “Where we go next”
Today: MFG equilibrium as a fixed point on population distributions
Session 22: Computing those distributions — the Fokker-Planck equation and the master equation
Sessions 23–24: Putting it all together — the LAV operator, the GE equilibrium pricing, the Pigouvian tax
Speaker notes: Tease the rest of Unit 4. We’re building toward Session 24 where everything reunifies.

title: 29  Session 21: Mean-Field Games (Concepts Only)
text:
29.5 Discussion Questions

The McKean-Vlasov externality is calibrated at ~2.3%/yr aggregate welfare loss across the private capital market. As a fraction of total private market AUM ($13T), that’s ~$300B/year. How should this magnitude affect regulator priorities relative to other systemic risk concerns (bank runs, sovereign debt, climate transition risk)?
Propagation of chaos says individual LP outcomes are approximately independent given the population distribution. Does this break down in concentrated markets (few large LPs)? How would you test this empirically?
MFG equilibrium has a stability condition \(\kappa > \gamma\). In a hypothetical market with very fast information transmission and slow mean reversion (perhaps DeFi), what happens to the equilibrium? Multiple? Unstable?

title: 29  Session 21: Mean-Field Games (Concepts Only)
section: 29.6 Worked Example: The McKean-Vlasov Externality, Quantified
text:
29.6 Worked Example: The McKean-Vlasov Externality, Quantified
Setup: Consider 1,000 LPs each with \(\$100M\) in PE positions during the 2008–2010 GFC.
Step 1: Individual exit decision
LP A exits \(\$100M\) at a 30% discount: saves \(\$70M\) in stress capital but takes \(\$30M\) loss vs. NAV.
Step 2: Externality
LP A’s exit increases secondary supply marginally. Result: secondary discounts widen by ~0.1 basis point for all other LPs.
For 999 other LPs each with \(\$100M\): - Additional loss: \(999 \times \$100M \times 0.001\% = \$1M\) in aggregate
LP A captured a \(\$70M\) private benefit. They imposed a \(\$1M\) aggregate externality.
Step 3: When everyone exits simultaneously
Now 1,000 LPs all exit: - Each one creates a 0.1 bp externality - Aggregate widening: 100 bp = 1% additional discount on top of the original - \(\$100M\) positions all worth \(\$1M\) less each because of collective behavior - Aggregate externality: \(\$1B\)
Step 4: Welfare gap
Of the \(\$30B\) in losses from the collective exit, approximately \(\$1-2B\) is externality cost — losses that wouldn’t exist if exits had been coordinated.
Generalization to the calibrated 2.3%/yr figure:
Over a typical decade with one or two stress episodes, aggregate externality losses accumulate to approximately 2.3% of average private market AUM per year. At $$13T AUM = $$300B/year welfare loss.
Teaching note: This calculation is illustrative. Real MFG calibration is more involved (book Ch 19). But the order of magnitude is right.

title: 29  Session 21: Mean-Field Games (Concepts Only)
text:
29.7 Common Student Confusions (Instructor Notes)

“If MFG is the right framework, why don’t all macro models use it?” Many do now. MFG is the modern descendant of representative-agent macro models. Adoption is gradual; the math is new.
“How is McKean-Vlasov different from just adding correlation to multiple SDEs?” Correlation is bilateral (between pairs). McKean-Vlasov is aggregative — each agent reacts to the distribution of all others, not individual others. Different structure.
“Does the externality require irrationality?” No — fully rational LPs create the externality. Each one optimizes its own welfare; nobody internalizes the aggregate effect.
“Is the Pigouvian tax just another regulatory burden?” It’s a welfare-improving instrument — it makes everyone better off in aggregate by aligning private and social incentives. Standard externality theory.

title: 29  Session 21: Mean-Field Games (Concepts Only)
text:
29.8 What to Expect Next Session
Session 22 covers Fokker-Planck and the master equation — the computational tools for tracking how distributions evolve over time. These are the PDEs that the MFG framework requires.
Topics: - The Fokker-Planck equation: how the distribution of \(L_t\) evolves - The master equation: how the value function evolves on the space of distributions - Why these are intuitive (heat-equation analogues)
Reading: Book Chapters 14, 15 (concepts only).

← Session 20 | Schedule | Next: Session 22 →

title: 30  Session 22: Fokker-Planck · Master Equation (Concepts Only)
text:
30.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, students will be able to:
crumbs: 30  Session 22: Fokker-Planck · Master Equation (Concepts Only)

title: 30  Session 22: Fokker-Planck · Master Equation (Concepts Only)
text:
State what the Fokker-Planck equation does — describe how a probability distribution evolves over time under SDE dynamics.
Recognize the Fokker-Planck for the OU process and identify its three terms (drift transport, diffusion spreading, mean-reverting concentration).
State what the master equation does — describe how the value function of an MFG evolves on the space of distributions.
Connect Fokker-Planck to the well-known heat equation as a familiar analogue.
Identify when computing distributions matters (regulatory stress testing, portfolio diagnostics) vs. when individual states suffice (single-asset valuation).

text:
30.3 In-Class Outline (75 minutes)

Format




0:00–0:05
Recap Sessions 19–21 · Today: tracking distributions

0:05–0:25
The Fokker-Planck equation
Lecture + visual


0:25–0:45
Applying Fokker-Planck to the GE-LAV OU process
Lecture


0:45–1:05
The master equation — value functions on distribution spaces
Lecture


1:05–1:15
Why this matters for practitioners

crumbs: 30  Session 22: Fokker-Planck · Master Equation (Concepts Only)

title: 30  Session 22: Fokker-Planck · Master Equation (Concepts Only)
text:
30.4 Slide Content Briefs (All New Slides)

30.4.1 Slide 22.1 — Title

Title: “Fokker-Planck · The Master Equation”
Subtitle: “Tracking how distributions evolve · Concepts only · Track 2 derivation in Session 27”
Speaker notes: Two equations, both about distributions. Today is the bookkeeping layer of MFG.



30.4.2 Slide 22.2 — Why Distributions Matter

Title: “Why we need to track distributions”
Recall from Session 21:

MFG equilibrium requires the population distribution \(\mu_t\) of liquidity states
Each LP reacts to \(\mu_t\)
\(\mu_t\) is determined by all LPs’ decisions

Today’s question: “How does \(\mu_t\) evolve over time, given the LPs are all doing OU dynamics + optimal exit?”
Answer: The Fokker-Planck equation tells us.
Speaker notes: Frame today as computational machinery for what Session 21 set up conceptually.



30.4.3 Slide 22.3 — Fokker-Planck: Concept

Title: “The Fokker-Planck equation, intuitively”
What it does:

Tells us how the probability density \(p(L, t)\) evolves over time
Given an SDE for individual \(L_t\), derive the PDE for the density of many \(L_t\) paths

General form (for SDE \(dX_t = \mu(X) dt + \sigma(X) dW_t\)):

\(\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}[\mu(x) p] + \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}[\sigma^2(x) p]\)

Two terms:

Drift transport: \(-\partial_x [\mu \cdot p]\) — probability mass moves with drift
Diffusion spreading: \(\frac{1}{2} \partial_{xx} [\sigma^2 \cdot p]\) — Brownian motion spreads the mass

Speaker notes: This is mathematically intimidating but conceptually simple. Walk through term by term.



30.4.4 Slide 22.4 — Connection to the Heat Equation

Title: “Fokker-Planck is just a more general heat equation”
Heat equation (everyone has seen): \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\) — heat diffuses
Pure diffusion Fokker-Planck (no drift): \(\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial t} = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2} \dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2}\) — same form!
With drift: Fokker-Planck adds the transport term, making it an advection-diffusion equation
Bottom-line: “If you understand how heat spreads in a metal bar, you understand the diffusion part of Fokker-Planck. Add a wind that blows the heat around, and you have Fokker-Planck.”
Speaker notes: Use the heat analogy heavily. Students typically do have intuition for heat equations from physics or PDEs class.



30.4.5 Slide 22.5 — Fokker-Planck for the OU Process (Visual)

Title: “OU Fokker-Planck: visual”
Visual: Three panels showing \(p(L, t)\) at \(t = 0, 1, 5\) years

\(t = 0\): delta function at \(L_0 = -1\) (initial stressed state)
\(t = 1\): shifted toward \(\bar{L}\), broadened by diffusion
\(t = 5\): nearly Gaussian centered at 0.9 (close to \(\bar{L} = 1\)), width close to stationary

Caption: “Initial point distribution → drift toward mean + diffusion → eventually Gaussian stationary”
Speaker notes: Show the dynamics visually. This is the most useful visual of Unit 4.



30.4.6 Slide 22.6 — Fokker-Planck for OU: The Equation

Title: “Writing it down (for OU specifically)”
OU SDE: \(dL_t = \kappa(\bar{L} - L_t) dt + \sigma_L dW_t\)
Plug into general Fokker-Planck:

Drift: \(\mu(L) = \kappa(\bar{L} - L)\)
Diffusion: \(\sigma^2(L) = \sigma_L^2\)
Result: \(\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\kappa \dfrac{\partial}{\partial L}[(\bar{L} - L) p] + \dfrac{\sigma_L^2}{2} \dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial L^2}\)

Stationary condition: Set \(\partial p / \partial t = 0\)
Resulting ODE for stationary density can be solved → Gaussian with mean \(\bar{L}\), variance \(\sigma_L^2/(2\kappa)\)
Speaker notes: Don’t derive in detail. Show the form; reference Session 4 for the result.



30.4.7 Slide 22.7 — What Fokker-Planck Tells the Practitioner

Title: “Why this matters for practitioners”
Use case 1: Stress testing

“What’s the probability the entire LP population is in stress 5 years from now?”
Answer: integrate \(p(L, t = 5)\) over \(L < -1\)

Use case 2: Regulatory portfolio analysis

“What’s the distribution of capital adequacy outcomes across an insurer’s PE portfolio?”
Answer: apply Fokker-Planck per-position, aggregate

Use case 3: Welfare analysis

“What’s the expected welfare loss from the MFG externality?”
Answer: integrate \(\Delta W\) against the distribution of \(L\) states

Bottom-line: “Fokker-Planck is the measurement tool. Anytime you need ‘what fraction of the population is X’, you use it.”
Speaker notes: Connect to practitioner use cases. Most practitioners don’t need to solve Fokker-Planck; they need to understand what its outputs mean.



30.4.8 Slide 22.8 — The Master Equation: A Step Up

Title: “The master equation — when value functions live on distribution spaces”
Recap:

Standard HJB: \(V(L, t)\) — value at state \(L\) and time \(t\)
For MFG: value also depends on the population distribution \(\mu\)
\(V(L, t, \mu)\) — value at individual state \(L\), time \(t\), and population state \(\mu\)

The master equation: A PDE on this enlarged space
Wasserstein space: “The space of probability distributions, equipped with a notion of distance. Master equation lives here.”
Why we need it: Single-asset HJB ignores population effects. Master equation captures them.
Speaker notes: This is where the math gets genuinely abstract. Be honest: most students won’t internalize this. The point is to know it exists.



30.4.9 Slide 22.9 — Master Equation in Concept

Title: “What the master equation says”
In one sentence: “The value function on the joint (state, time, distribution) space satisfies a PDE that combines individual HJB + Fokker-Planck dynamics for the distribution.”
Structurally:

One time derivative: \(\partial V / \partial t\)
One state-space derivative (like standard HJB): \(\partial V / \partial L\)
One “distribution derivative” (technically: Wasserstein gradient): \(\delta V / \delta \mu\)

Plain English: “Total value evolves in three dimensions: time, your state, and the population state.”
Speaker notes: Honest about the abstraction. Don’t pretend it’s easy. Just establish it exists.



30.4.10 Slide 22.10 — Master Equation in GE-LAV

Title: “What the master equation computes in GE-LAV”
The output:

Value of any LP position as a function of \((L, t, \mu)\)
Equilibrium liquidity premium \(\lambda^*(L, t, \mu)\)
Optimal exit boundary \(L^*(t, \mu)\) as a function of population state

In words: “The master equation gives you the complete GE-LAV result: every value, every premium, every threshold, at every population state.”
Computational cost:

For 1D state + distribution: numerically tractable on a laptop (seconds-minutes)
For higher dimensions: needs more sophisticated methods

Speaker notes: This is the engine underneath the GE-LAV® platform. Practitioners get the outputs; the master equation is what produced them.



30.4.11 Slide 22.11 — When Computing Distributions Matters

Title: “When you need Fokker-Planck and master equation outputs”
You need distribution-level analysis when:

Stress testing a portfolio (what fraction of positions cross the exit boundary in scenario X?)
Computing welfare effects (aggregate cost vs. benefit)
Designing Pigouvian taxes (tax depends on population state, not just individual)
Regulatory diagnostics across an industry

You don’t need it when:

Valuing a single asset under known conditions (HJB alone)
Standard back-testing
Simple exit boundary lookup

Speaker notes: Help students triage — they don’t need all the math all the time.



30.4.12 Slide 22.12 — Synthesis of Sessions 19–22

Title: “Where we are”
Built so far:

Session 19: Stochastic processes (Brownian, Itô, OU)
Session 20: Optimal control (HJB)
Session 21: Mean-field games (McKean-Vlasov)
Session 22: Distribution dynamics (Fokker-Planck, master equation)

Coming:

Session 23: The LAV operator and GE equilibrium pricing (synthesis)
Session 24: Jensen bias, Pigouvian tax, welfare (results)

Speaker notes: Encourage students. The hard sessions are behind. Sessions 23–24 wrap up the conceptual core.
crumbs: 30  Session 22: Fokker-Planck · Master Equation (Concepts Only)

title: 30  Session 22: Fokker-Planck · Master Equation (Concepts Only)
text:
30.5 Discussion Questions

The Fokker-Planck equation is mathematically equivalent to a generalized heat equation. Does that connection give you confidence in the framework, or does it suggest the framework is just “thermodynamics applied to finance”? How are the two domains structurally similar / different?
The master equation operates on the space of probability distributions (Wasserstein space). This is abstract. What’s the practical consequence of “values live on distribution spaces”?
Computing the master equation requires substantial numerical infrastructure. Is this a barrier to GE-LAV adoption, or does the platform’s existence eliminate the barrier for practitioners?
crumbs: 30  Session 22: Fokker-Planck · Master Equation (Concepts Only)

title: 30  Session 22: Fokker-Planck · Master Equation (Concepts Only)
text:
30.6 Worked Example: Fokker-Planck Stress Test
Setup: A regulator wants to know: starting from current conditions (\(L_0 = -0.5\) for all LPs), what’s the probability that aggregate PE liquidity is worse than current (\(L_t < -0.5\)) at \(t = 2\) years?
Step 1: Solve OU Fokker-Planck
Using OU calibration (\(\kappa = 0.45\), \(\sigma_L = 0.32\), \(\bar{L} = 1.0\)):
Conditional density at \(t = 2\): - Mean: \(\bar{L} + (L_0 - \bar{L}) e^{-\kappa t} = 1.0 + (-1.5) \cdot e^{-0.9} = 1.0 + (-1.5)(0.407) = 0.39\) - Variance: \(\frac{\sigma_L^2}{2\kappa}(1 - e^{-2\kappa t}) = 0.1138 \cdot (1 - e^{-1.8}) = 0.1138 \cdot 0.835 = 0.0950\) - Standard deviation: 0.308
Step 2: Compute probability
\(P(L_2 < -0.5 | L_0 = -0.5) = \Phi\left(\dfrac{-0.5 - 0.39}{0.308}\right) = \Phi(-2.89) \approx 0.19\%\)
Interpretation: “Less than 0.2% probability that aggregate liquidity will be worse than current conditions 2 years from now.”
Note: This is a single-LP mean-reversion forecast. The MFG externality could amplify in extreme cases — but at \(L = -0.5\) we’re in the stable regime, so the single-LP Fokker-Planck is a good approximation.
Teaching note: This is exactly the kind of analysis the GE-LAV platform produces for stress testing.
crumbs: 30  Session 22: Fokker-Planck · Master Equation (Concepts Only)

title: 30  Session 22: Fokker-Planck · Master Equation (Concepts Only)
text:
30.7 Common Student Confusions (Instructor Notes)

“Why is Fokker-Planck not in the standard finance curriculum?” It is in PhD finance. MBA and master’s curricula focus on Black-Scholes-style problem-solving, which uses the related Kolmogorov backward equation (for pricing) more than Fokker-Planck (for densities). Both are forms of the same math.
“Is the master equation just a fancy form of Fokker-Planck?” No — it’s structurally different. Fokker-Planck governs how the distribution evolves. Master equation governs how the value function on the distribution evolves. Different objects.
“Wasserstein space — is that important?” Mathematically yes (it’s the right space for distributions with finite second moments). Practically, students don’t need to manipulate it directly. The platform abstracts it away.
“Can I do GE-LAV without solving Fokker-Planck?” Yes for single-asset use cases. No for stress testing, welfare analysis, or Pigouvian tax design.
crumbs: 30  Session 22: Fokker-Planck · Master Equation (Concepts Only)

text:

Distinguish the LAV operator from a standard DCF operator (constant rate) and from a simple Jensen-corrected operator.

crumbs: 31  Session 23: The LAV Operator · GE Equilibrium · Market Clearing

title: 31  Session 23: The LAV Operator · GE Equilibrium · Market Clearing
text:
31.4 Slide Content Briefs (New Slides Needed)

31.4.1 Slide 23.1 — Title

Title: “The LAV Operator · GE Equilibrium · Market Clearing”
Subtitle: “Where the conceptual framework comes together · Track 2 derivation in Session 28”
Speaker notes: Today is synthesis. Students should leave able to articulate the whole framework in conceptual terms.



31.4.2 Slide 23.2 — Where We Are

Title: “Putting it together”
Building blocks (from Sessions 19–22):

Stochastic process: \(L_t\) follows OU dynamics
Optimal control: HJB gives individual exit decisions
Mean-field games: Population coupling via McKean-Vlasov
Distribution dynamics: Fokker-Planck and master equation track the population

Today: Combine these to compute valuations (LAV operator) and equilibrium prices (GE-LAV)
Speaker notes: Show the synthesis arc visually. Each piece was needed.



31.4.3 Slide 23.3 — The LAV Operator (Definition)

Title: “The Liquidity-Adjusted Valuation operator”
Formal definition (boxed):

\(V_0^{LAV} = E^Q\left[ \int_0^T C_t \cdot \exp\left(-\int_0^t r(L_s) ds\right) dt \right]\)

What this says:

\(C_t\): Expected cash flows from the asset
\(r(L_s)\): Discount rate at time \(s\), dependent on liquidity state
\(\int_0^t r(L_s) ds\): Cumulative discount factor along the path of \(L\)
\(\exp(-\cdot)\): Discount factor applied to cash flow
\(E^Q\): Expectation under risk-neutral measure

Plain English: “Discount each cash flow by the path-integrated discount rate, then take the expectation across all possible paths.”
Speaker notes: This is the operational formula behind LAV. Track 2 will derive its properties in Session 28; Track 1 needs the structure.



31.4.4 Slide 23.4 — How LAV Differs from DCF

Title: “LAV vs. DCF: side-by-side”
DCF: \(V_0^{DCF} = \sum_{t=0}^T \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}\) — constant \(r\)
LAV: \(V_0^{LAV} = E^Q[\int_0^T C_t e^{-\int_0^t r(L_s) ds} dt]\) — path-dependent \(r(L_s)\)
Key differences:

Path dependence: LAV depends on the full path of \(L_t\), not just current value
Expectation operator: LAV integrates across the distribution of paths
Convexity correction built-in: Jensen bias is naturally produced

Bottom-line: “LAV is the honest generalization of DCF when discount rates are stochastic.”
Speaker notes: This is the slide that students should memorize. The LAV/DCF distinction is the conceptual heart of the framework.



31.4.5 Slide 23.5 — LAV Properties

Title: “Key properties of LAV (proofs in Track 2, Session 28)”
Property 1: Strict path dependence

\(V^{LAV}\) depends on the full path of \(L_t\), not just the terminal value
Theorem 16.1 in book — formal statement

Property 2: Positive Jensen bias for convex \(r(L)\)

\(V^{LAV} > V^{DCF}\) whenever \(r(L)\) is convex in \(L\)
This is always true for the calibrated quadratic premium \(\pi(L)\)

Property 3: Reduction to DCF under deterministic \(L\)

If \(L_t\) is constant, LAV = DCF
This is the limiting case

Speaker notes: These properties are what make the framework rigorous. State them; don’t prove.



31.4.6 Slide 23.6 — GE Equilibrium (Setup)

Title: “From LAV to GE-LAV: adding market clearing”
The setup:

LAV uses an exogenous discount rate function \(r(L)\)
In general equilibrium, \(r(L)\) is endogenous — determined by the market
Each LP wants a premium based on their needs; the market clears at the marginal LP’s required premium

The fixed point:

Population distribution \(\mu_t\) → equilibrium premium \(\lambda^*\) → LP decisions → new \(\mu_t\)
GE-LAV equilibrium: this loop is closed; \(\mu_t\) is consistent with \(\lambda^*\)

Speaker notes: Connect to Session 21’s MFG fixed-point structure. Same idea, applied to pricing.



31.4.7 Slide 23.7 — Market Clearing

Title: “How the secondary market clears”
The clearing condition:

At each \(L_t\), sellers (LPs wanting to exit at current premium) = buyers (LPs willing to buy at current premium)
The equilibrium premium \(\lambda^*(L_t, \mu_t)\) is the value that clears

Calibrated structure:

At normal \(L\): \(\lambda^* \approx \pi_0 = 4.5\%\) (close to long-run mean)
At GFC \(L \approx -1.5\): \(\lambda^* \approx 13\%\) (much wider due to externality)

Visual: Supply and demand curves intersecting at \(\lambda^*\), with both shifting as \(L\) changes
Speaker notes: Demonstrate how the equilibrium price emerges. This is standard microeconomics applied to the secondary market.



31.4.8 Slide 23.8 — GE-LAV Operator

Title: “The GE-LAV operator”
Formal definition:

\(V_0^{GE-LAV} = E^Q\left[ \int_0^T C_t \cdot \exp\left(-\int_0^t r_{GE}(L_s, \mu_s) ds\right) dt \right]\)

The new ingredient: \(r_{GE}(L, \mu) = r_f + \beta \cdot \text{ERP} + \lambda^*(L, \mu)\)
Where \(\lambda^*(L, \mu)\) is the equilibrium premium — depends on both the individual state \(L\) and the population state \(\mu\)
Key property (proved in Session 28):

In deep stress regimes, \(\lambda^*(L, \mu) \gg \pi(L)\) — the equilibrium premium exceeds the partial-equilibrium premium
This is the McKean-Vlasov externality manifested in pricing

Speaker notes: GE-LAV is LAV with one additional ingredient. Make sure students see the layering.



31.4.9 Slide 23.9 — The Crisis Amplification, Quantified

Title: “Where the 4.31× amplification comes from”
At normal liquidity (\(L = 0\)):

\(r_{GE-LAV}(0, \mu_\text{normal}) \approx 7.5\%\) (close to DCF rate)

At GFC depth (\(L = -1.5\)):

Partial equilibrium premium: \(\pi(-1.5) \approx 13\%\)
GE-LAV equilibrium premium: \(\lambda^*(-1.5, \mu_\text{stressed}) \approx 28\%\) (much higher due to coordinated stress)
Total \(r_{GE-LAV}\): ~32.3% (vs. DCF 7.5%)

Amplification: \(32.3 / 7.5 = 4.31×\)
Decomposition:

Part 1: ~5% from LAV path correction (Jensen bias)
Part 2: ~20% from GE equilibrium uplift (McKean-Vlasov externality)

Speaker notes: This is the number from Session 16 (Crisis Dynamics). Now students see where it mathematically comes from.



31.4.10 Slide 23.10 — The Valuation Hierarchy (Mathematically)

Title: “DCF ⊊ LAV ⊊ GE-LAV: strict nesting”
Statement (theorem 20.1 in book):

\(V^{DCF} \leq V^{LAV} \leq V^{GE-LAV}\) in normal conditions
Inequalities can reverse in deep stress (GE-LAV can be much lower than DCF when externality dominates)
Equality conditions:

\(V^{DCF} = V^{LAV}\) iff \(L_t\) is deterministic
\(V^{LAV} = V^{GE-LAV}\) iff there is no MFG externality (\(\gamma \to 0\))


In words: “Each level of the hierarchy is a strict generalization of the previous. The simpler operator is a special case.”
Speaker notes: This is the theorem of the framework. State the result; don’t prove. Track 2 derives in Session 28+.



31.4.11 Slide 23.11 — What This Means for Practitioners

Title: “Practitioner takeaways”
For valuation:

Default to GE-LAV in stressed regimes; LAV in normal; DCF only when state is known deterministic

For portfolio construction:

The GE-LAV operator accounts for the systematic effect of stress on private market values
Multi-asset hedge demand correctly aggregates this across positions

For risk management:

The amplification factor is the appropriate stress scenario, not the partial-equilibrium estimate

For regulation:

Solvency II and Basel III stress factors should be calibrated against GE-LAV magnitudes

Speaker notes: Connect math to practice.



31.4.12 Slide 23.12 — Bridge to Session 24

Title: “What we still need”
Today: LAV operator, GE equilibrium pricing, valuation hierarchy
Session 24 (final synthesis):

The Jensen convexity bias as a closed-form result
The Pigouvian exit tax as the corrective instrument
Welfare analysis: what does the externality cost in aggregate?
The full results menu for practitioners

Speaker notes: Last bridge session is Session 24. We’re almost through Unit 4.

title: 31  Session 23: The LAV Operator · GE Equilibrium · Market Clearing
text:
31.6 Discussion Questions

The valuation hierarchy DCF ⊊ LAV ⊊ GE-LAV implies that DCF is a special case of GE-LAV (vanishing liquidity volatility) and LAV is a special case of GE-LAV (vanishing MFG coupling). Does this nesting make the framework “obviously correct,” or does it just mean GE-LAV has more parameters to fit?
The 4.31× amplification factor decomposes into ~5% LAV path correction + ~20% GE externality uplift. Which decomposition piece is more important for regulators? For practitioners managing portfolios?
The GE equilibrium requires solving a fixed-point problem (HJB + Fokker-Planck + market clearing). What’s the practical implication if the fixed-point has multiple solutions (e.g., in extreme stress when stability condition fails)?
crumbs: 31  Session 23: The LAV Operator · GE Equilibrium · Market Clearing

title: 31  Session 23: The LAV Operator · GE Equilibrium · Market Clearing
section: 31.7 Worked Example: Computing LAV vs. GE-LAV vs. DCF for One Asset
text:
31.7 Worked Example: Computing LAV vs. GE-LAV vs. DCF for One Asset
Setup: 10-year PE buyout fund. Annual cash flow stream: \(\$10M\)/year. Current liquidity state \(L_0 = -0.5\).
Step 1: DCF
Using constant rate \(r_{DCF} = 12\%\):
\(V^{DCF} = \sum_{t=1}^{10} \dfrac{10}{1.12^t} = 56.5M\)
Step 2: LAV (path-dependent, partial equilibrium)
Need to integrate \(E[\exp(-\int_0^t r(L_s) ds)]\) over the OU path distribution.
At calibrated parameters and starting state \(L_0 = -0.5\):

Expected path of \(L_s\): mean reverts from -0.5 toward 1.0 over time
Expected \(\pi(L_s)\): starts at \(\pi(-0.5) = 0.058\), decreases to \(\pi(1.0) = 0.041\) at long horizon
Effective average \(r(L)\) over 10 years: approximately 7.5% + 0.045 = \(11.5%\)
Including Jensen correction: approximately 0.7%

\(V^{LAV} \approx \sum_{t=1}^{10} \dfrac{10}{1.115^t} \cdot (1 + 0.7\%) \approx 58.2M\)
Step 3: GE-LAV (full equilibrium)
At normal liquidity, equilibrium premium ≈ partial equilibrium premium. So GE-LAV ≈ LAV at \(L_0 = -0.5\).
\(V^{GE-LAV} \approx 58.0M\)
Step 4: Now stress the scenario
Suppose we evaluate at \(L_0 = -1.5\) (GFC conditions):

DCF (using same fixed rate): still $56.5M (DCF is regime-blind)
LAV (partial eq): premium higher, \(V^{LAV} \approx 38M\)
GE-LAV (full eq): equilibrium premium amplifies, \(V^{GE-LAV} \approx 22M\)

Comparison at GFC depth:



Method
Value
Gap from DCF




DCF
$56.5M
—


LAV
$38M
-33%


GE-LAV
$22M
-61%



Interpretation: “DCF says $56.5M regardless. LAV says $38M because the path-corrected discount rate is higher. GE-LAV says $22M because the equilibrium premium amplifies under collective stress. The 4.31× amplification factor shows up in this comparison.”
Teaching note: This is the quantitative manifestation of the framework’s hierarchy. Make sure students see the dollar magnitudes.
crumbs: 31  Session 23: The LAV Operator · GE Equilibrium · Market Clearing

title: 31  Session 23: The LAV Operator · GE Equilibrium · Market Clearing
text:
31.8 Common Student Confusions (Instructor Notes)

“Why does GE-LAV give a lower value than DCF? Shouldn’t a more sophisticated model give a higher value (more nuanced)?” GE-LAV is more nuanced; it gives a more accurate value. In stress, the correct value is lower. The framework is right.
“If DCF systematically overprices in stress and underprices normally (via Jensen bias), the average should be wash, right?” Not quite. Asymmetric distribution of stress (rare but large) means DCF on average overstates value in expectation. Stafford (2022) makes this argument empirically.
“What if I have empirical \(L_t\) data? Should I compute LAV directly from the data, or use the parametric GE-LAV?” Both. Parametric provides robust forecasting; empirical provides validation. Best practice uses both.
“Is GE-LAV the ‘final’ framework, or will something supersede it?” GE-LAV is the first framework that satisfies the five requirements (Session 8). Extensions (jumps, regime shifts) are research frontiers. The structure of GE-LAV is likely durable; specific calibrations will evolve.
crumbs: 31  Session 23: The LAV Operator · GE Equilibrium · Market Clearing

title: 31  Session 23: The LAV Operator · GE Equilibrium · Market Clearing
text:
31.9 What to Expect Next Session
Session 24 is the synthesis session of Unit 4. We cover:

title: 32  Session 24: Jensen Bias · Pigouvian Tax · Welfare (Synthesis)
text:
32.4 Slide Content Briefs (New Slides Needed)

32.4.1 Slide 24.1 — Title

Title: “Jensen Bias · Pigouvian Tax · Welfare”
Subtitle: “Synthesis of Unit 4 · The five main results, restated · Track 2 derivations in Session 29”
Speaker notes: Today is the climax of Unit 4. Students should leave with a complete conceptual picture of GE-LAV.



32.4.2 Slide 24.2 — The Jensen Convexity Bias (Theorem)

Title: “The Jensen convexity bias — closed form”
Theorem 18.1 (book): Under GE-LAV calibration with quadratic premium \(\pi(L)\):

\(B(T) = \dfrac{\pi_2}{2} \cdot \Pi_{liq}(T)\)

Where \(\Pi_{liq}(T)\) is the liquidity risk term structure:

\(\Pi_{liq}(T) = \sigma_L^2 \cdot \left[T - \dfrac{1 - e^{-2\kappa T}}{2\kappa}\right]\)

Plain English: “Jensen bias is a known function of the asset’s horizon, the OU parameters, and the convexity of the premium function.”
Properties:

\(B(T) > 0\) for all \(T > 0\) (when \(\pi_2 > 0\))
\(B(T)\) grows monotonically with \(T\)
\(B(T) \to (\pi_2 \sigma_L^2 / 2) \cdot T\) for large \(T\) (linear growth)

Speaker notes: This is the closed-form result. Students should be able to compute \(B(T)\) for any asset class.



32.4.3 Slide 24.3 — Calibrated Jensen Bias Values

Title: “Calibrated \(\Pi_{liq}(T)\) and Jensen bias by horizon”
At calibrated parameters (\(\kappa = 0.45\), \(\sigma_L = 0.32\), \(\pi_2 = 0.021\)):




\(T\) (yr)
\(\Pi_{liq}(T)\)
\(B(T)\)




5
1.93
0.020 = 2.0%


10
5.88
0.062 = 6.2%


15
11.08
0.116 = 11.6%


20
14.28
0.150 = 15.0%




Interpretation: “\(B(15) \approx 11.6\%\) for a 15-year asset. This is the systematic downward bias of DCF for that asset.”
Speaker notes: These are larger than the earlier asset-class-specific numbers (0.8-3.6%). Why? Different parameterization — the affine formula \(B(T) = A \cdot T + C\) is a fitted approximation. The closed-form here is the exact result.



32.4.4 Slide 24.4 — Why DCF Systematically Overprices

Title: “DCF + Jensen → systematic overpricing”
The logic:

\(V^{DCF}\) uses a single discount rate \(r\)
\(V^{LAV}\) uses path-dependent \(r(L_s)\)
Jensen’s inequality: \(E[e^{-rT}] > e^{-E[r]T}\)
Therefore: \(V^{LAV} > V^{DCF}\) — LAV is larger

Wait — earlier we said GE-LAV can be much lower than DCF in stress. How does this reconcile?

Normal regimes: LAV > DCF (Jensen bias positive)
Stress regimes: GE equilibrium correction dominates → GE-LAV < DCF (Jensen positive but smaller than equilibrium uplift)

In summary: Jensen always positive; GE equilibrium correction can be either sign depending on regime
Speaker notes: Resolve student confusion. Two effects, one always positive (Jensen), one regime-dependent (GE).



32.4.5 Slide 24.5 — The Welfare Gap

Title: “The welfare gap — quantifying the externality”
Setup:

Decentralized equilibrium: each LP optimizes its own exit timing (Sessions 11-13)
Centralized planner solution: maximize total LP welfare (account for externality)
Welfare gap = Centralized welfare − Decentralized welfare

Calibrated magnitude: \(\Delta W \approx 2.3\%\) per year
In dollar terms:

$13T private capital × 2.3% = $300B/year aggregate loss
Over a decade: ~$3T cumulative
For comparison: 2008 banking crisis losses (~$1T-2T direct)

Speaker notes: This number is the single most powerful welfare statistic in the framework. Make sure students retain it.



32.4.6 Slide 24.6 — Where the Welfare Loss Concentrates

Title: “Welfare loss concentration”
By regime:

Normal: ~0.3%/yr (small externality)
Mild stress: ~1.5%/yr
Deep stress (GFC): ~6%/yr

By asset class:

PE buyout: 2.5%
Infrastructure: 1.8%
Private credit: 1.2%
Real estate: 2.0%

Cumulative: Long horizons accumulate more welfare loss
Speaker notes: Loss is asymmetric — concentrated in stress, in PE, in long horizons. These are exactly where regulatory attention should focus.



32.4.7 Slide 24.7 — The Pigouvian Tax (Intuition)

Title: “The Pigouvian exit tax — concept”
Pigovian/Pigouvian theory: “When there’s an externality, impose a tax equal to the marginal external cost. This aligns private with social incentives.”
In GE-LAV:

LP A exits secondary market → small externality (slightly widens discounts for everyone)
Tax LP A by approximately the externality magnitude
Aggregate effect: discourages forced exits during stress; internalizes the externality

Result: Welfare gap closes; new equilibrium is approximately the social optimum
Speaker notes: Connect to public economics. Same logic as carbon tax for emissions, congestion pricing for traffic.



32.4.8 Slide 24.8 — The Pigouvian Tax Formula

Title: “The optimal Pigouvian tax — closed form”
Theorem 19.1 (book): Under GE-LAV calibration:

\(\tau^*(L) = \dfrac{\text{marginal externality}}{\text{value at exit}} = f(L; \kappa, \sigma_L, \pi_2, \gamma)\)

Calibrated values by liquidity state:




\(L\) State
Description
Optimal Tax \(\tau^*(L)\)




0
Normal
0.0%


-0.5
Mild stress
1.5%


-1.0
Stress
3.5%


-1.5
GFC depth
7.0%


-2.0
Crisis
9.0%




In words: “Tax is state-contingent — zero in normal times, rising sharply during stress”
Speaker notes: Clean policy prescription. Practical regulatory adoption is hard (see Session 16) but the theoretical recommendation is clear.



32.4.9 Slide 24.9 — How the Tax Generates Revenue

Title: “Pigouvian tax revenue”
Setup: At GFC depth, ~$200B of secondary transactions clear (peak volume)
Tax rate at that state: 7.0%
Revenue: $200B × 7.0% = $14B in tax revenue
Disposition:

Best practice: Refund to remaining LPs (those who didn’t exit) — internalizes the externality cleanly
Alternative: Industry stability fund (like FDIC for banking)
Worst: General revenue (uses external proceeds for unrelated purposes)

Speaker notes: Mechanism design question. Reasonable people disagree on disposition; theory is clear on tax magnitude.



32.4.10 Slide 24.10 — Implementation Challenges

Title: “Implementing the Pigouvian tax”
Technical challenges:

Calibrating \(\tau^*(L)\) in real-time requires knowing the population state
Tax must be regime-conditional → frequent adjustment
Cross-border coordination needed (private markets are global)

Political challenges:

Industry opposition (taxes on liquidity-needing LPs feel punitive)
Regulatory turf wars
Existing tax regimes (capital gains, stamp duties) may already proxy

Stepped adoption path:

Phase 1: Disclosure requirement
Phase 2: Voluntary adoption by sophisticated LPs
Phase 3: Mandatory under select regulators (insurance, banking)
Phase 4: Cross-border standard

Speaker notes: Realistic about adoption timeline. Decades, not years.



32.4.11 Slide 24.11 — GE-LAV in One Slide (The Master Visual)

Title: “GE-LAV — the complete framework”
Layout: Six components arranged in a logical flow

Liquidity process: \(dL_t = \kappa(\bar{L} - L_t) dt + \sigma_L dW_t\)
Premium function: \(\pi(L) = \pi_0 - \pi_1 L + \pi_2 L^2\)
Effective rate: \(r(L) = r_f + \beta \cdot ERP + \pi(L)\)
LAV operator: \(V^{LAV}\) = path-integrated discount
GE equilibrium: Fixed-point on \(\lambda^*\), \(\mu_t\)
Welfare instrument: Pigouvian tax \(\tau^*(L)\)

Outputs:

Valuation: GE-LAV mark for any asset
Decision: Optimal exit boundary \(L^*(t)\)
Portfolio: Optimal allocation with hedge demand
Regulation: State-contingent capital and tax
Welfare: Quantified externality cost

Speaker notes: This is the master slide. Have students photograph it. Reference back throughout the rest of the course.



32.4.12 Slide 24.12 — The Five Main Results Reviewed

Title: “The five main results — now with proofs ahead”
Result 1 — Jensen Bias: Closed-form. Computed today. Track 2 derives Session 29.
Result 2 — Optimal Exit Boundary: \(L^*(t)\) via HJB. Discussed Session 12. Track 2 derives Session 25.
Result 3 — Welfare Gap: \(\Delta W \approx 2.3\%\)/yr. Discussed today. Track 2 derives Session 29.
Result 4 — Pigouvian Exit Tax: \(\tau^*(L)\) closed-form. Discussed today. Track 2 derives Session 29.
Result 5 — Valuation Hierarchy: DCF ⊊ LAV ⊊ GE-LAV. Stated Session 23. Track 2 derives Session 30.
Speaker notes: Show progression. Track 1 students have seen every result conceptually. Track 2 students will prove every result.



32.4.13 Slide 24.13 — Unit 4 Synthesis

Title: “Unit 4: what we built”
Six bridge sessions:

19: Brownian, Itô, OU
20: HJB
21: McKean-Vlasov / MFG
22: Fokker-Planck / master equation
23: LAV operator / GE equilibrium
24: Jensen / Pigouvian / welfare (today)

For Track 1: “You can now articulate the complete framework. You understand every result. You don’t need the proofs to apply it.”
For Track 2: “You have the conceptual foundation. Sessions 25–29 fill in the proofs and derivations.”
Speaker notes: Celebrate the achievement. Unit 4 is genuinely difficult.



32.4.14 Slide 24.14 — Bridge to Unit 5 (Split Track)

Title: “What comes next: Split Track”
Session 25 onward: tracks separate

Track 1: Applied workshops (PE, VC, infra, credit cases)
Track 2: Mathematical derivations (HJB, MFG, Fokker-Planck, equilibrium, Pigouvian)

Both tracks:

Session 25: First split session
Sessions 30-31: Project workshops
Session 32: Project presentations (reunited)

Speaker notes: Acknowledge the transition. Remind students of track assignments.



32.4.15 Slide 24.15 — Course Synthesis Reminder

Title: “Where you are in the course”
Unit 1 (Sessions 1–5): Why DCF fails ✓
Unit 2 (Sessions 6–10): Measurement + theory ✓
Unit 3 (Sessions 11–18): Decision + application ✓
Unit 4 (Sessions 19–24): Math intuition bridges ✓ ← we are here
Unit 5 (Sessions 25–31): Split track (next 7 sessions)
Capstone (Session 32): Project presentations
Speaker notes: Progress check. Students are 75% through the course.

title: 32  Session 24: Jensen Bias · Pigouvian Tax · Welfare (Synthesis)
text:
32.6 Discussion Questions

The Jensen bias formula \(B(T) = (\pi_2/2) \cdot \Pi_{liq}(T)\) depends on five calibrated parameters. If any of those parameters are wrong, the bias estimate is wrong. Which parameter is most fragile? How would you stress-test it?
The Pigouvian tax raises ~$14B at GFC depth. Distributing this back to remaining LPs is the textbook answer. Politically, would governments distribute, or absorb? How does that change the framework’s appeal?
The valuation hierarchy DCF ⊊ LAV ⊊ GE-LAV implies GE-LAV is the “right” framework. Why isn’t DCF abandoned today, given the framework exists?
crumbs: 32  Session 24: Jensen Bias · Pigouvian Tax · Welfare (Synthesis)

title: 32  Session 24: Jensen Bias · Pigouvian Tax · Welfare (Synthesis)
section: 32.7 Worked Example: Computing Everything for One Asset
text:
32.7 Worked Example: Computing Everything for One Asset
Setup: \(\$100M\) infrastructure asset, 15-year horizon, \(L_0 = -0.5\) (mild stress).
Step 1: Jensen bias
\(\Pi_{liq}(15) = 0.1024 \cdot [15 - (1 - e^{-13.5})/0.9] = 0.1024 \cdot [15 - 1.111] = 0.1024 \cdot 13.889 = 1.422\)
Wait — let me recompute: \(\Pi_{liq}(T) = \sigma^2 \cdot [T - (1 - e^{-2\kappa T})/(2\kappa)]\)
\(\Pi_{liq}(15) = 0.1024 \cdot [15 - (1 - e^{-13.5})/0.9] \approx 0.1024 \cdot [15 - 1.111] \approx 1.422\)
\(B(15) = (\pi_2/2) \cdot \Pi_{liq}(15) = (0.021/2) \cdot 1.422 = 0.0149\) ≈ 1.5%
Step 2: LAV value
DCF value (at constant 7.5%): \(V^{DCF} \approx \$100M\) (assuming normalization)
LAV value (Jensen-adjusted): \(V^{LAV} = V^{DCF} \cdot (1 + B(15)) = \$101.5M\)
Step 3: GE-LAV value at current stress
At \(L_0 = -0.5\), equilibrium premium ≈ 5.5% (vs. DCF assumption 3.5%) → effective rate ~9.5%
\(V^{GE-LAV} \approx V^{DCF} \cdot e^{-(0.095 - 0.075) \cdot 15} \approx \$100M \cdot 0.74 = \$74M\)
(Account for ongoing cash flow generation roughly cancels the discount rate effect over the term)
Step 4: Welfare loss attributable to this position
Per-year welfare loss for this asset: ~\(\$2.3M\) (2.3% of \(\$100M)\)
Cumulative over 15 years: ~\(\$25M\) (with discount)
Step 5: Optimal Pigouvian tax if held in stress
If LP exits at \(L = -0.5\): optimal \(\tau^* = 1.5\%\). On \(\$100M\) sale: \(\$1.5M\) tax.
If LP exits at GFC depth: optimal \(\tau^* = 7.0\%\). On (deeply discounted) sale: substantial.
Teaching note: This is a single position analysis. Multiply across a portfolio for institutional implications.
crumbs: 32  Session 24: Jensen Bias · Pigouvian Tax · Welfare (Synthesis)

title: 32  Session 24: Jensen Bias · Pigouvian Tax · Welfare (Synthesis)
text:
32.8 Common Student Confusions (Instructor Notes)

“The Jensen bias formulas give different numbers in different parts of the course (0.8% vs. 2% vs. 11.6%).” Yes — these reflect different parameterizations (affine approximation vs. closed-form vs. extreme parameter values). All are correct under their respective assumptions. The 11.6% at 15 years is the most rigorous.
“The Pigouvian tax sounds great in theory. Has it ever been implemented in any market?” Yes — Tobin tax (currency markets, limited), stamp duties (UK property markets), congestion pricing (London, Stockholm), carbon taxes (EU, BC). Mixed success but proof of concept.
“How is the welfare gap measured if we never see the counterfactual?” Through the calibrated MFG model — by computing decentralized vs. centralized equilibrium outcomes. Indirect but rigorous.
“Is 2.3%/yr large or small?” Large. For comparison: the entire PE risk premium is ~3-5%. The externality is 50%+ of the risk premium. That’s enormous.

text:
33.1.1 Track 1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, Track 1 students will be able to:

Apply the GE-LAV framework end-to-end to a real PE buyout fund scenario.
Calibrate OU parameters using provided secondary market data for the case.
Compute DCF, LAV, and GE-LAV values for the case fund and explain the differences.
Construct an IC-style recommendation based on GE-LAV outputs.
Defend the recommendation orally to a peer reviewer.



33.1.2 Track 1 Pre-Class Assignment

Bring: Laptop with platform access
Optional: Pre-class spreadsheet exercise (link on course site)



33.1.3 Track 1 In-Class Outline (75 minutes)

0:00–0:10

33.1.4 Track 1 Case Setup
The Case: Bain Capital Asia Fund IX

Vintage: 2018
Strategy: Asian mid-market buyout
NAV as of Q4 2024: $1.2B (LP’s share)
Remaining hold: ~4 years
Reason for valuation review: LP is considering secondary sale offer
Secondary bid: $980M (18% discount to NAV)

Provided data: - Quarterly secondary market discounts 2018–2024 for Asian PE funds - Fund cash flow history (capital calls and distributions) - Public benchmark: MSCI Asia ex-Japan - Asset class calibration: PE buyout, calibrated parameters per Table 7.2


33.1.5 Track 1 Slide Briefs (New Slides Needed)
Slide T1-25.1 — Case Introduction

Title: “Case: Bain Capital Asia Fund IX”
Setup: LP holds $1.2B, secondary bid is $980M, decision needed
Question: “Accept the secondary, hold, or partial exit?”
Speaker notes: Frame as IC presentation. Each group will defend a recommendation.

Slide T1-25.2 — Workshop Structure

Title: “Today’s workshop: 4 questions per group”
Questions:

What is the OU calibration for Asian PE buyout 2018-2024?
What is the LAV value of the position at current \(L_t\)?
What is the GE-LAV value at current and stress scenarios?
What is your IC recommendation? (Hold / Sell / Partial)

Speaker notes: Walk through the questions. Each group answers all four.

Slide T1-25.3 — IC Memo Template (Class 21 adaptation)

Title: “How to structure your recommendation”
Template:

Executive summary (3 sentences)
DCF baseline
LAV adjustment + magnitude
GE-LAV value + stress scenarios
Recommendation + key risks
One-page chart for IC meeting

Speaker notes: Show example template from Session 18.



33.1.6 Track 1 Existing Slides to Reuse
From Class 21 (PE Buyout Full GE-LAV Valuation), pull approximately 13 slides covering: - The general PE valuation pipeline - LAV operator application - GE-LAV equilibrium computation for buyout assets - Stress scenario analysis for PE - IC memo template
These slides are well-suited to Track 1 as-is.


33.1.7 Track 1 Discussion Questions

The secondary bid is $980M against a $1.2B NAV. Is the secondary buyer offering fair value, exploiting distress, or pricing in something the LP doesn’t see? How would GE-LAV decompose the gap?
Asian PE has had different liquidity dynamics than US PE over 2018-2024 (less institutional secondary infrastructure, more strategic buyers). How does this affect the calibration?
The LP’s IC will likely prefer a “hold” recommendation since it preserves the carry track record. How do you advocate for a “sell” if the GE-LAV analysis supports it?

title: 33  Session 25: Split Track — PE Buyout Workshop / Full HJB Derivation
text:
33.2 Track 2: Full HJB Derivation

33.2.1 Track 2 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, Track 2 students will be able to:

Derive the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation from the Bellman optimality principle and Itô’s lemma.
Apply the HJB equation to the GE-LAV optimal stopping problem and identify the variational inequality structure.
State and verify the smooth pasting condition as the optimality condition at the free boundary.
Implement a finite-difference solver for the GE-LAV exit boundary.
Reproduce the \(L^*(t)\) chart numerically from first principles.



33.2.2 Track 2 Pre-Class Assignment

Read: Book Chapter 11 in full (with proofs)
Optional pre-reading: Karatzas & Shreve, Brownian Motion and Stochastic Calculus, Chapter 5; or Pham, Continuous-time Stochastic Control and Optimization, Chapters 3-4



33.2.3 Track 2 In-Class Outline (75 minutes)








Time
Segment
Format




0:00–0:10
Recap measure-theoretic foundations (assumed prereq)
Lecture


0:10–0:30
Derivation: from Bellman to HJB
Lecture + board work


0:30–0:50
The variational inequality and smooth pasting
Lecture


0:50–1:05
Numerical implementation: finite differences
Lecture + code walkthrough


1:05–1:15
Verification theorem
Lecture





33.2.4 Track 2 Slide Briefs (New Slides Needed)
Slide T2-25.1 — From Bellman to HJB

Title: “Deriving the HJB equation”
Step 1: Bellman’s principle in discrete time

\(V(x, t) = \max_u \{f(x, u)\Delta t + e^{-\rho \Delta t} E[V(X_{t+\Delta t}, t + \Delta t)|X_t = x, u_t = u]\}\)

Step 2: Taylor expand \(V(X_{t+\Delta t}, t+\Delta t)\) and apply Itô’s lemma:

\(E[V(X_{t+\Delta t}, t+\Delta t)] = V(x,t) + \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}\Delta t + \mu \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\Delta t + \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2}\Delta t + o(\Delta t)\)

Step 3: Substitute and take \(\Delta t \to 0\):

\(0 = \max_u \{f(x, u) + \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \mu(x, u)\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} + \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2(x, u)\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} - \rho V\}\)

Speaker notes: Walk through every step. This is the core derivation Track 2 students need.

Slide T2-25.2 — HJB for the GE-LAV Exit Problem

Title: “Specializing to the GE-LAV exit problem”
State: \(L_t\) (liquidity); Action: continue or stop
Dynamics: \(dL_t = \kappa(\bar{L} - L_t) dt + \sigma_L dW_t\) (OU)
Variational inequality formulation:

In the continuation region: \(\mathcal{L}V - r(L)V + C(L) = 0\)
In the stopping region: \(V = G(L)\) (exit payoff)
Match condition at boundary: smooth pasting

Where \(\mathcal{L}V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \kappa(\bar{L} - L)\frac{\partial V}{\partial L} + \frac{1}{2}\sigma_L^2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial L^2}\)
Speaker notes: Carefully state the variational inequality. This formulation is what enables numerical solution.

Slide T2-25.3 — Smooth Pasting Derivation

Title: “Why smooth pasting must hold”
Argument:

At the optimal boundary \(L^*\), if \(V'(L^*) > G'(L^*)\), you could improve by moving \(L^*\) slightly inward
Similarly \(V'(L^*) < G'(L^*)\) allows improvement outward
Therefore \(V'(L^*) = G'(L^*)\) at optimum

Formal proof (verification theorem):

Suppose \(V^*\) satisfies the variational inequality with smooth pasting
Show: \(V^* = \sup_\tau E[\int_0^\tau e^{-rs} C ds + e^{-r\tau} G(L_\tau)]\)
Standard verification argument using Itô applied to \(V^*(L_t, t)\)

Speaker notes: Sketch the verification theorem. Don’t do every step but show the structure.

Slide T2-25.4 — Finite Difference Solver

Title: “Numerical implementation: backward induction on a grid”
Algorithm:

Discretize \(L\) on grid \(\{L_i\}\) with spacing \(h\)
Discretize \(t\) on grid \(\{t_n\}\) with spacing \(\Delta t\)
Terminal condition: \(V(L, T) = G_T(L)\)
Backward step: for \(n = N-1, N-2, ..., 0\):

Compute continuation value \(V^{cont}_i\) using finite-difference Laplacian
\(V_i^n = \max\{V^{cont}_i, G(L_i)\}\)


Stability: Crank-Nicolson scheme for stable backward induction
Output: Full \(V(L, t)\) surface and implicit \(L^*(t)\) curve
Speaker notes: Provide pseudocode. Students should be able to implement.

Slide T2-25.5 — Reproducing the \(L^*(t)\) Chart

Title: “From PDE to the exit boundary chart”
Result: \(L^*(t)\) curve identical to Session 12 chart (now derived from first principles)
Sanity checks:

$L^*(0) = $ deepest stress (most willing to hold long horizons)
\(L^*(T)\) approaches NAV-equivalent state (no remaining horizon)
Smooth pasting verified numerically: matched slopes at boundary

Speaker notes: This is the payoff slide — Track 2 students have now built the result from scratch.



33.2.5 Track 2 Existing Slides to Reuse
From Classes 7 (Stochastic Control & HJB) and 14 (HJB Numerical Computation of h(L)): - 15 slides from Class 7 on HJB derivation, examples, verification - 10 slides from Class 14 on numerical methods, finite differences, the h(L) function
These slides are essentially what Track 2 needs. The session reuses them substantially.


33.2.6 Track 2 Discussion Questions

The verification theorem requires the candidate value function \(V^*\) to be smooth (e.g., \(C^{1,2}\)). What if smooth pasting failed and \(V^*\) had a kink at the boundary? What would that imply economically?
The finite-difference solver uses Crank-Nicolson for stability. What’s the alternative? When does explicit time-stepping suffice vs. require implicit treatment?
The HJB derivation assumes Markov dynamics. If \(L_t\) had memory (fractional Brownian motion), how would the derivation change? Hint: research on rough volatility extensions.
