title: 34  Session 26: Split Track — VC & Secondaries / McKean-Vlasov MFG Proofs
text: 34.1 Track 1: VC + Secondaries Valuation Lab
crumbs: 34  Session 26: Split Track — VC & Secondaries / McKean-Vlasov MFG Proofs

title: 34  Session 26: Split Track — VC & Secondaries / McKean-Vlasov MFG Proofs
text:
34.1.1 Track 1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, Track 1 students will be able to:

Apply GE-LAV to a venture capital fund position (different cash flow profile from buyout).
Adjust the calibration for asset classes with thinner secondary market data.
Value a specific VC secondary opportunity using GE-LAV.
Distinguish LP-led from GP-led secondary structures and apply GE-LAV to each.
Communicate findings to a hypothetical VC GP and IC.



34.1.2 Track 1 Pre-Class Assignment

Read: VC secondary case (distributed); skim recent Lazard or Evercore VC secondary report
Try: Platform exercise on a sample VC position



34.1.3 Track 1 In-Class Outline (75 minutes)








Time
Segment
Format




0:00–0:10
VC vs. buyout: cash flow profile differences
Lecture


0:10–0:25
Calibrating GE-LAV for VC (parameter adjustments)
Lecture + worked example


0:25–0:50
Lab: value a real VC secondary opportunity
Group work


0:50–1:05
LP-led vs. GP-led secondaries
Lecture + discussion


1:05–1:15
Group recommendations + synthesis
Presentations





34.1.4 Track 1 Slide Briefs (New Slides Needed)
Slide T1-26.1 — VC vs. Buyout: Different Cash Flow Profiles

Title: “VC: longer J-curve, larger tail risk”
Comparison:

Buyout: Capital deployed in years 1-3, distributions start years 4-5, terminal exit year 7-8
VC: Capital deployed in years 1-4, very few distributions until years 6-7, terminal events highly bimodal (some IPOs, many writedowns)

GE-LAV implications:

Longer effective duration → larger Jensen bias accumulation
Tail risk demands wider hedge demand
Secondary market thinner; less data for calibration

Speaker notes: Set up why VC is not just “buyout with bigger volatility.” Different problem.

Slide T1-26.2 — VC Secondary Market Specifics

Title: “VC secondaries: thin, idiosyncratic, growing”
Structure:

Smaller than PE secondary market (~$20B/yr vs. $130B for PE)
More direct (less broker-mediated)
More structured deals (strip sales, NAV financings, GP-leds dominating)

Calibration challenges:

Limited public price discovery
Discounts often estimated indirectly via comparables
Implied \(L_t\) noisier than for PE

Speaker notes: Be honest about data limitations. Calibration confidence intervals are wider for VC.

Slide T1-26.3 — Calibrated VC Parameters

Title: “VC asset class calibration”
Parameters (vs. PE buyout):




Parameter
PE Buyout
VC




\(\beta_i\) (systemic)
1.2
1.1


\(\sigma_V\)
0.25
0.45


\(\rho\) (corr with \(L\))
0.30
0.20


Hedge demand at \(L=0\)
8%
4%


Jensen bias slope (T=10)
0.17%
0.18%




Insight: “VC has higher idiosyncratic volatility but lower correlation with aggregate liquidity → smaller proportional hedge demand”
Speaker notes: Counterintuitive result. Students often think VC is “riskier all around.” It is — but the liquidity dimension is less correlated.

Slide T1-26.4 — LP-Led vs. GP-Led Secondaries

Title: “Two transaction types, two GE-LAV applications”
LP-led:

Single LP exits position via secondary sale
GE-LAV: standard valuation + exit boundary check

GP-led (continuation funds):

GP rolls portfolio company into new vehicle
Existing LPs choose: cash out at offered price OR roll into new fund
GE-LAV evaluates: is the “cash out” price the right GE-LAV value? Is the “roll” priced as a new commitment at fair value?

Common pitfall:

GP-led terms often anchored on DCF-style valuations
GE-LAV may say “cash out” is too low or “roll” terms are too expensive

Speaker notes: GP-leds are the fastest-growing segment. Students entering the industry will face these.

Slide T1-26.5 — Workshop Setup

Title: “Lab: a real GP-led decision”
The case:

VC fund vintage 2017, currently 7 years in
GP proposes continuation fund for the top 5 portfolio companies
Existing LPs offered: $850M cash for $1B NAV (15% discount) OR roll into new 4-year fund at same NAV
Your job: GE-LAV-evaluate both options

Group output:

GE-LAV value of “cash out” option
GE-LAV value of “roll” option (treating as new commitment)
Recommendation with rationale

Speaker notes: This is meaty. Groups will need most of class time.



34.1.5 Track 1 Existing Slides to Reuse
From Class 27 (Multi-Asset GE-LAV) — adapt for VC: - Multi-asset calibration tables - Cross-asset correlation matrix - Combined hedge demand visualization
Adapt by emphasizing VC vs. PE buyout differences rather than the full multi-asset treatment (which gets its own session in Session 27).
crumbs: 34  Session 26: Split Track — VC & Secondaries / McKean-Vlasov MFG Proofs

title: 34  Session 26: Split Track — VC & Secondaries / McKean-Vlasov MFG Proofs
text:
34.2 Track 2: McKean-Vlasov Mean-Field Game Proofs

34.2.1 Track 2 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, Track 2 students will be able to:

State and prove the propagation of chaos for the GE-LAV mean-field system (Theorem 13.4).
Derive the Schauder fixed-point existence argument for MFG equilibrium (Theorem 13.2).
State and verify the stability condition \(\kappa > \gamma\) for uniqueness (Theorem 13.3).
Implement an MFG fixed-point iteration numerically.
Discuss the limits of the framework: when stability fails, what happens.



34.2.2 Track 2 Pre-Class Assignment

Read: Book Chapter 13 in full, including all proofs
Pre-read: Carmona & Delarue, Probabilistic Theory of Mean-Field Games, Chapter 1 — at least sections 1.1–1.3



34.2.3 Track 2 In-Class Outline (75 minutes)








Time
Segment
Format




0:00–0:10
Setup: the GE-LAV McKean-Vlasov SDE
Lecture


0:10–0:30
Propagation of chaos: statement + proof sketch
Lecture


0:30–0:50
Schauder fixed-point: existence of MFG equilibrium
Lecture


0:50–1:05
Stability condition and uniqueness
Lecture


1:05–1:15
Numerical MFG iteration
Code walkthrough





34.2.4 Track 2 Slide Briefs (New Slides Needed)
Slide T2-26.1 — The McKean-Vlasov SDE for GE-LAV

Title: “The McKean-Vlasov system, formally”
Individual SDE:

\(dL_t^i = \kappa(\bar{L} - L_t^i) dt + \gamma \int \phi(L_t^i, l) \mu_t(dl) dt + \sigma_L dW_t^i\)

Where:

\(\mu_t\) = distribution of \(L_t^j\) across the population
\(\phi(L, l)\) = pairwise interaction kernel
\(\gamma\) = coupling strength

Self-consistency:

$_t = $ Law \((L_t^i)\) for representative \(i\)
This is the fixed-point requirement

Speaker notes: Formal statement. Don’t dwell on the kernel form; refer to book for specifics.

Slide T2-26.2 — Propagation of Chaos: Theorem 13.4

Title: “Propagation of chaos for GE-LAV”
Theorem statement: Let \(L_t^{1,N}, ..., L_t^{N,N}\) be the \(N\)-particle system. As \(N \to \infty\):

\(\sup_{t \leq T} E\left[ \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \delta_{L_t^{i,N}} - \mu_t \right]_{W_1} \leq \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{N}}\)
where \(W_1\) is the 1-Wasserstein distance

Proof sketch:

Construct coupling between particle system and MV limit
Use Lipschitz properties of \(\phi\) + Grönwall inequality
Apply standard rates for empirical measure convergence

Implications:

At \(N = 100\): error ~10%
At \(N = 10,000\): error <1%
Private markets have \(N \approx 10,000\) → mean-field is a very good approximation

Speaker notes: State the theorem; sketch the proof. Don’t do every step. Refer to Sznitman (1991) for the canonical argument.

Slide T2-26.3 — Schauder Fixed-Point and Existence (Theorem 13.2)

Title: “Existence of MFG equilibrium”
The fixed-point map \(\Phi: \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{M}\):

Take \(\mu \in \mathcal{M}\) (set of measures with finite second moment)
Solve HJB given \(\mu\): get optimal policy \(u^*_\mu\)
Solve Fokker-Planck under \(u^*_\mu\): get new measure \(\Phi(\mu)\)
Fixed point: \(\mu = \Phi(\mu)\)

Schauder’s argument:

\(\mathcal{M}\) is convex, closed under Wasserstein topology
\(\Phi\) is continuous (HJB and FP both stable in measure)
\(\Phi\) is compact (uniform bounds on moments)
Therefore \(\Phi\) has a fixed point in \(\mathcal{M}\)

Speaker notes: This is the existence argument. State carefully. Full proof in book Section 13.3.

Slide T2-26.4 — Stability Condition and Uniqueness (Theorem 13.3)

Title: “When the equilibrium is unique”
Stability condition: \(\kappa > \gamma \cdot \text{Lip}(\phi)\)
Calibrated values:

\(\kappa = 0.45\)
\(\gamma \cdot \text{Lip}(\phi) \approx 0.1\) (well below \(\kappa\))
Stability: comfortable

Theorem 13.3 (uniqueness): Under the stability condition, the MFG fixed point is unique
Proof sketch:

Contraction argument on \(\Phi\): \(W_1(\Phi(\mu_1), \Phi(\mu_2)) \leq \rho \cdot W_1(\mu_1, \mu_2)\) for some \(\rho < 1\)
Banach fixed-point theorem gives uniqueness

When stability fails:

\(\gamma \cdot \text{Lip}(\phi) > \kappa\): multiple equilibria possible
Market can “tip” between equilibria (analogous to bank runs)
This is a research frontier; GE-LAV’s basic calibration assumes stability

Speaker notes: Honest about limits. Stable regime is empirically supported.



34.2.5 Track 2 Existing Slides to Reuse
From Class 8 (McKean-Vlasov MFG) — 15 slides: - MV SDE setup and motivation - Propagation of chaos heuristics - The fixed-point structure
From Class 15 (Mean-Field Equilibrium Fixed Point) — 18 slides: - Numerical fixed-point iteration - Convergence diagnostics - Application to GE-LAV calibration
These slides directly support the proof material.


34.2.6 Track 2 Discussion Questions

The propagation of chaos rate is \(1/\sqrt{N}\). At \(N = 10,000\), error is ~1%. But private markets have concentration (a few mega-LPs hold most of the AUM). Does that violate the i.i.d. assumption? How would you account for concentration?
The stability condition \(\kappa > \gamma\) is satisfied empirically. But in a hypothetical “panic” scenario, could the system enter an unstable regime? What would the off-equilibrium dynamics look like?
The MFG framework assumes Markov dynamics. Real markets have memory (regret, anchoring). How would non-Markov extensions change the proofs?
crumbs: 34  Session 26: Split Track — VC & Secondaries / McKean-Vlasov MFG Proofs

title: 34  Session 26: Split Track — VC & Secondaries / McKean-Vlasov MFG Proofs
text:
34.3 Both Tracks: Cross-Audit
Students in either track may audit the other track’s session via recording. Track 1 students benefit from hearing the MFG existence proof (it explains why GE-LAV works); Track 2 students benefit from the applied VC case (it grounds the math).

← Session 25 | Schedule | Next: Session 27 →

title: 35  Session 27: Split Track — Infrastructure Case / Fokker-Planck & Master Equation
text: 35.1 Track 1: Infrastructure Case — “The 57% Illusion”
crumbs: 35  Session 27: Split Track — Infrastructure Case / Fokker-Planck & Master Equation

title: 35  Session 27: Split Track — Infrastructure Case / Fokker-Planck & Master Equation
text:
35.1.1 Track 1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, Track 1 students will be able to:

Apply GE-LAV to a 20+ year infrastructure asset (longest duration in private markets).
Identify why long-duration assets carry the largest accumulated Jensen bias.
Compute the “57% illusion” — the gap between DCF and GE-LAV for stressed infrastructure positions.
Translate GE-LAV findings into pension fund and insurance regulatory language.
Connect infrastructure valuation errors to systemic financial stability concerns.



35.1.2 Track 1 Pre-Class Assignment

Read: Book Chapter 22 case (or Class 22 slides if available)
Optional: Global Infrastructure Hub report on private infrastructure valuation



35.1.3 Track 1 In-Class Outline (75 minutes)








Time
Segment
Format




0:00–0:10
Why infrastructure is special: duration + cash flow stability
Lecture


0:10–0:25
The “57% illusion” decomposed
Lecture


0:25–0:55
Lab: value a real infrastructure asset (Indianapolis Toll Road or comparable)
Group work


0:55–1:15
Group recommendations + regulatory implications
Presentations





35.1.4 Track 1 Slide Briefs (New Slides Needed)
Slide T1-27.1 — Infrastructure: The Longest Horizon Asset

Title: “Why infrastructure breaks DCF the hardest”
Three features:

Horizon: 20-50 years (PPPs sometimes 99 years)
Cash flow stability: Contracted revenues (tolls, regulated rates) → low operational volatility
Liquidity sensitivity: Long-duration asset; tiny premium changes have huge value effects

Result: Infrastructure has the largest Jensen bias and the largest equilibrium correction in stressed regimes
Speaker notes: Frame the asset class. Most students won’t have valued infrastructure deeply.

Slide T1-27.2 — The 57% Illusion (The Master Number)

Title: “$1B infrastructure: DCF says $1B, GE-LAV at GFC says $430M”
Decomposition:

DCF mark: $1.000B (constant 7% rate, 30-year asset)
LAV mark (normal): $1.108B (Jensen bias 10.8% for 30 yr)
GE-LAV mark (normal): $1.000B (small equilibrium correction)
GE-LAV mark (GFC depth): $0.430B (57% gap to DCF)

Why so extreme:

30-year duration amplifies discount rate changes
At GFC depth, equilibrium rate ~32% vs. DCF 7%
\((1.07/1.32)^{30} \approx 0.43\)

Speaker notes: This is the single most powerful number for the regulatory case. Cite this routinely.

Slide T1-27.3 — Implications for Pension Funds

Title: “What this means for your pension fund”
Standard pension fund infrastructure portfolio:

~10% allocation to infrastructure
Duration: 15-25 years average
Reported NAVs: smooth, stable

Under GE-LAV:

In stressed regime, reported NAVs overstate true value by 30-57%
Hedge demand calls for ~3-5 percentage points lower allocation
Stress test exposes 40-60% downside (vs. 25% under standard methodology)

Speaker notes: Connect to specific public pension funds (CalPERS, CalSTRS, Oregon, ABP) — all heavily exposed to infrastructure.

Slide T1-27.4 — Insurance Implications

Title: “Insurance regulatory implications”
Solvency II treatment:

Long-duration infrastructure: capital charge 25-39%
Matched against long-term liabilities (annuities, life)
Conventional wisdom: “good match” between asset and liability duration

GE-LAV finding:

Asset side has hidden stress exposure (the 57% illusion)
Liability side does not adjust during liquidity stress
Match is worse than reported

Speaker notes: This is a regulatory angle. The Solvency II review process in EU is the relevant policy channel.

Slide T1-27.5 — Lab Setup

Title: “Lab: value the Indianapolis Toll Road”
Setup:

75-year concession (acquired 2006 for $3.85B)
Annual toll revenues ~$200M, growing 2%/yr
LP investor: ITR Concession Co (Macquarie, etc.)
Question: what’s it worth today under DCF vs. GE-LAV? Where would secondaries clear?

Group output:

DCF baseline
GE-LAV at normal liquidity
GE-LAV at GFC-depth stress
“If you had to sell today, what’s your reservation price?”

Speaker notes: Real asset. Real numbers. Tangible.



35.1.5 Track 1 Existing Slides to Reuse
From Class 22 (Infrastructure: The 57% Illusion) — 13 slides: - The headline “57%” chart - Infrastructure duration analysis - Specific case studies (Indianapolis Toll Road, Chicago Skyway, etc.) - Regulatory implications
Use largely as-is.
crumbs: 35  Session 27: Split Track — Infrastructure Case / Fokker-Planck & Master Equation

title: 35  Session 27: Split Track — Infrastructure Case / Fokker-Planck & Master Equation
text:
35.2 Track 2: Fokker-Planck & Master Equation Derivations

35.2.1 Track 2 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, Track 2 students will be able to:

Derive the Fokker-Planck equation from the SDE via Chapman-Kolmogorov.
Prove well-posedness of the GE-LAV Fokker-Planck (Theorem 14.2).
State and apply the master equation for value functions on Wasserstein space (Theorem 15.1).
Implement a Fokker-Planck solver for the GE-LAV liquidity state.
Verify numerical convergence of the FP solution to the analytical stationary distribution.



35.2.2 Track 2 Pre-Class Assignment

Read: Book Chapters 14, 15 in full (with proofs)
Pre-read: Pavliotis, Stochastic Processes and Applications, Chapters 4–5; or Risken, The Fokker-Planck Equation, Chapter 4



35.2.3 Track 2 In-Class Outline (75 minutes)








Time
Segment
Format




0:00–0:15
Derivation of FP from Chapman-Kolmogorov
Lecture + board


0:15–0:35
Well-posedness (existence + uniqueness): Theorem 14.2
Lecture


0:35–0:55
The master equation on Wasserstein space
Lecture


0:55–1:10
Numerical FP solver
Code walkthrough


1:10–1:15
Verification: numerical → analytical stationary
Discussion





35.2.4 Track 2 Slide Briefs (New Slides Needed)
Slide T2-27.1 — Derivation: Fokker-Planck from Chapman-Kolmogorov

Title: “Deriving FP from first principles”
Step 1: Chapman-Kolmogorov equation:

\(p(x, t+\Delta t) = \int p(x, t+\Delta t | y, t) \cdot p(y, t) dy\)

Step 2: Transition kernel for \(dX = f dt + g dW\) at small \(\Delta t\):

\(p(x, t+\Delta t | y, t) \approx \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi g^2 \Delta t}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{(x - y - f \Delta t)^2}{2 g^2 \Delta t}\right)\)

Step 3: Taylor expand \(p(y, t)\) around \(y = x\):

\(p(y, t) = p(x, t) + (y-x) p_x(x, t) + \frac{1}{2}(y-x)^2 p_{xx}(x, t) + O((y-x)^3)\)

Step 4: Substitute, integrate, take \(\Delta t \to 0\):

\(\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}[f p] + \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}[g^2 p]\)

Speaker notes: Walk through each step. This is the fundamental derivation. Students should be able to reproduce.

Slide T2-27.2 — Well-Posedness (Theorem 14.2)

Title: “Well-posedness of the GE-LAV Fokker-Planck”
Theorem 14.2: For the GE-LAV FP equation with calibrated drift and diffusion:

Existence: A weak solution \(p(L, t)\) exists for all \(T > 0\)
Uniqueness: The solution is unique in \(L^2([0, T] \times \mathbb{R})\)
Regularity: \(p\) is smooth in the interior; satisfies \(C^\infty\)

Proof sketch:

Hörmander’s hypoellipticity theorem (sufficient regularity from nondegenerate diffusion)
\(L^2\) energy estimates for uniqueness
Maximum principle for boundedness

Speaker notes: State the theorem rigorously. Sketch the structure of the proof. Refer to book for full argument.

Slide T2-27.3 — Stationary Distribution Derivation

Title: “Stationary density of OU”
Setup: Set \(\partial p / \partial t = 0\) in the FP equation:

\(0 = -\kappa \dfrac{d}{dL}[(\bar{L} - L) p_\infty(L)] + \dfrac{\sigma_L^2}{2} \dfrac{d^2}{dL^2} p_\infty(L)\)

Solve via integrating factor:

Multiply by \(\exp(\kappa(L - \bar{L})^2 / \sigma_L^2)\)
Get \(\dfrac{d}{dL}[\) flux \(] = 0\)
Therefore flux \(= 0\) (since density decays at \(\pm\infty\))
Setting flux to zero: \(-\kappa(\bar{L} - L) p + \frac{\sigma_L^2}{2} p' = 0\)

Solve ODE: \(p_\infty(L) = C \exp(-\kappa(L - \bar{L})^2 / \sigma_L^2)\)
Normalize: \(C = \sqrt{2\kappa / (\pi \sigma_L^2)}\), giving Gaussian with mean \(\bar{L}\), variance \(\sigma_L^2/(2\kappa)\)
Speaker notes: This is the derivation Track 1 students saw the result of in Session 4. Now Track 2 sees the derivation.

Slide T2-27.4 — The Master Equation on Wasserstein Space

Title: “Master equation: HJB for MFG value functions”
Setting:

Value function \(V(L, t, \mu)\) where \(\mu\) is the population distribution
\(\mu\) lives in Wasserstein space \(\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R})\)

Master equation (informally):

\(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \mathcal{L} V - r(L) V + \mathcal{H}(V_\mu) + C = 0\)

Where:

\(\mathcal{L}V\) = standard HJB operator on \(L\)
\(V_\mu = \delta V / \delta \mu\) = Wasserstein gradient
\(\mathcal{H}(V_\mu)\) = Hamiltonian coupling individual to population

Speaker notes: This is the most abstract slide of the session. Don’t expect deep mastery — just recognition that the master equation exists and is solvable in principle.

Slide T2-27.5 — Numerical FP Solver

Title: “Implementing the FP solver”
Pseudocode:
Initialize: p_0(L_i) = delta at L_initial
For each time step:
  Apply finite-difference discretization
  Use Crank-Nicolson for stability
  Enforce boundary conditions: p → 0 at |L| → ∞
  Normalize: sum to 1
Output: p(L, t) for all t
Convergence check: As \(t \to T\) large, numerical \(p(L, T) \to\) analytical stationary
Performance: ~seconds for full GE-LAV time series
Speaker notes: Provide complete pseudocode. Students should be able to implement in <100 lines of Python.



35.2.5 Track 2 Existing Slides to Reuse
From Class 6 (Fokker-Planck Equations) — 16 slides: - FP derivation and applications - OU stationary distribution - Numerical methods - Connection to backward equations
Use directly.


35.2.6 Track 2 Discussion Questions

The FP derivation assumes the transition density is Gaussian at small \(\Delta t\). This holds for Itô SDEs with smooth coefficients. What if there are jumps (Lévy processes)? How does the FP change?
The master equation is on Wasserstein space — an infinite-dimensional manifold. Numerically, we discretize \(\mu\) on a grid. What’s the discretization error? When does this matter?
The well-posedness theorem requires nondegeneracy (\(\sigma_L > 0\)). What happens if \(\sigma_L = 0\) (deterministic OU)? Does the framework break down?


← Session 26 | Schedule | Next: Session 28 →
crumbs: 35  Session 27: Split Track — Infrastructure Case / Fokker-Planck & Master Equation

title: 36  Session 28: Split Track — Private Credit Case / GE Equilibrium Existence
text: 36.1 Track 1: Private Credit Case Workshop
crumbs: 36  Session 28: Split Track — Private Credit Case / GE Equilibrium Existence

title: 36  Session 28: Split Track — Private Credit Case / GE Equilibrium Existence
text:
36.1.1 Track 1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, Track 1 students will be able to:

Apply GE-LAV to a private credit / direct lending position.
Decompose private credit spreads into credit risk, illiquidity premium, and complexity premium components.
Distinguish GE-LAV for credit vs. equity strategies (cash flow profile, default risk interaction).
Compute liquidity-adjusted spread expectations for a direct lending strategy.
Evaluate a real BDC (business development company) or middle-market direct lending fund.



36.1.2 Track 1 Pre-Class Assignment

Read: Recent BDC quarterly report or direct lending fund presentation
Watch: 10-min video on private credit market structure (link on course site)



36.1.3 Track 1 In-Class Outline (75 minutes)








Time
Segment
Format




0:00–0:10
Private credit vs. PE: structural differences
Lecture


0:10–0:30
Decomposing private credit spreads
Lecture + worked example


0:30–0:55
Lab: value a BDC or middle-market direct lending position
Group work


0:55–1:15
Group recommendations + risk implications
Presentations





36.1.4 Track 1 Slide Briefs (New Slides Needed)
Slide T1-28.1 — Private Credit: Different Risk Profile

Title: “Private credit is debt, not equity”
Key differences from PE buyout:

Senior in capital structure: Lower expected return, lower variance
Shorter duration: Most direct lending is 3-7 years
Credit risk: Default + recovery rather than equity participation
Floating rate: Most direct lending pays SOFR + spread

GE-LAV implications:

Smaller hedge demand (less correlation with aggregate liquidity)
Smaller Jensen bias (shorter duration)
But: in stress, credit and liquidity premia interact (credit spreads widen when liquidity stresses)

Speaker notes: Students often think credit is “PE without the equity story.” It’s structurally different. Establish that.

Slide T1-28.2 — Decomposing Private Credit Spreads

Title: “What’s in a 700 bps spread?”
Typical direct lending spread: 5-8% above SOFR
Decomposition:

Credit risk: 200-400 bps (expected losses + idiosyncratic risk premium)
Illiquidity premium: 100-300 bps (GE-LAV territory)
Complexity premium: 50-100 bps (origination + monitoring costs)
Total: 350-800 bps

GE-LAV contribution: Quantifies the illiquidity component stochastically
Speaker notes: Spreads are not pure credit risk. Many investors don’t decompose carefully.

Slide T1-28.3 — Private Credit Sharpe Ratio Illusion

Title: “Private credit’s worst-in-class Sharpe penalty”
Recall Session 7’s slide: Private credit Sharpe ratio drops 84% under liquidity adjustment (highest of any private asset class)
Why:

High conventional Sharpe (~0.5) → looks attractive
But low return volatility and high illiquidity premium = denominator vastly understates true risk
Adjusted Sharpe (~0.08) reveals private credit may not be the “safe income” alternative many believe

Implication: “Private credit allocations sized on Sharpe-based optimization are likely substantially over-sized”
Speaker notes: This is a career-relevant finding. Many institutions have piled into private credit. The framework says they may be over-exposed.

Slide T1-28.4 — Lab Setup: BDC Valuation

Title: “Lab: a real BDC valuation”
Setup:

Choose a publicly-traded BDC (ARCC, MAIN, ORCC, or similar)
Public market price: known (one number)
NAV: reported quarterly
Question: Is the public market premium/discount to NAV consistent with GE-LAV?

Group output:

Estimate the “fair” NAV under GE-LAV
Compare to reported NAV (typically equals book value of loans)
Compare to public market price
Identify any arbitrage opportunity or risk signal

Speaker notes: BDCs offer a unique test: they’re publicly traded but hold private assets. The discount/premium structure is rich data.

Slide T1-28.5 — Cross-Asset Risk: Credit + Liquidity Interaction

Title: “When credit and liquidity stress together”
Setup: 2008-2010 GFC and 2020 COVID both saw simultaneous credit + liquidity stress
In private credit:

Default rates spiked
Secondary discounts widened
Recovery rates fell
All three correlated → much worse outcome than independence assumption

GE-LAV captures this:

Cross-asset \(\beta\)s in the multi-asset framework
Stress test for joint credit+liquidity events
More conservative capital recommendations

Speaker notes: This is why the integrated framework matters. Independent risk models miss the correlations.



36.1.5 Track 1 Existing Slides to Reuse
From Class 23 (Private Credit Spread Decomposition) — 12 slides: - Private credit market structure - Spread decomposition methodology - BDC analysis tools - Default + liquidity interaction
Use largely as-is.
crumbs: 36  Session 28: Split Track — Private Credit Case / GE Equilibrium Existence

title: 36  Session 28: Split Track — Private Credit Case / GE Equilibrium Existence
text:
36.2 Track 2: GE Equilibrium Existence Proof

36.2.1 Track 2 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, Track 2 students will be able to:

State and prove the existence of GE-LAV competitive equilibrium (Theorem 17.4).
Apply Schauder’s fixed-point theorem to the market-clearing map.
Identify the market clearing condition mathematically: aggregate supply = aggregate demand for liquidity.
Distinguish partial equilibrium (LAV) from general equilibrium (GE-LAV) at the level of the underlying math.
Connect the existence proof to the calibrated parameter regime where uniqueness holds.



36.2.2 Track 2 Pre-Class Assignment

Read: Book Chapter 17 in full (with all proofs)
Pre-read: Mas-Colell, The Theory of General Economic Equilibrium, Chapters 4-5



36.2.3 Track 2 In-Class Outline (75 minutes)



Time
Segment
Format




0:00–0:10
Recap: from MFG to GE
Lecture


0:10–0:30
The GE-LAV equilibrium definition
Lecture


0:30–0:50
Existence proof via Schauder
Lecture + board


0:50–1:05
Uniqueness under stability
Lecture


1:05–1:15
Numerical verification
Discussion





36.2.4 Track 2 Slide Briefs (New Slides Needed)
Slide T2-28.1 — GE-LAV Equilibrium: Definition

Title: “GE-LAV competitive equilibrium”
Definition: An equilibrium is a tuple \((\mu^*_t, \lambda^*(L, \mu^*), u^*(L, \mu^*))\) such that:

Individual optimality: \(u^*(L, \mu^*)\) solves the HJB given \(\lambda^*\)
Market clearing: \(\lambda^*(L, \mu^*)\) is the unique premium for which aggregate exit flows balance (sellers = buyers)
Distributional consistency: \(\mu^*_t\) is the law of \(L_t\) under dynamics induced by \(u^*\)

In one sentence: “Everyone’s optimal decisions, given the equilibrium premium, generate the distribution that produces that premium.”
Speaker notes: Set up the formal equilibrium notion. The three conditions are mutually self-consistent.

Slide T2-28.2 — Existence Theorem 17.4

Title: “Theorem 17.4: existence of GE-LAV equilibrium”
Statement: Under standard regularity conditions on \(\phi\), \(r(L)\), and \(C(L, t)\):

There exists at least one GE-LAV competitive equilibrium tuple \((\mu^*, \lambda^*, u^*)\)
Equilibrium is unique under the stability condition \(\kappa > \gamma \cdot \text{Lip}(\phi)\)

Proof outline:

Define an equilibrium map \(\Psi: \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{M}\):

\(\Psi(\mu)\) = the new measure induced by optimal individual decisions

Show \(\Psi\) is continuous and compact on \(\mathcal{M}\)
Apply Schauder’s fixed-point theorem

Speaker notes: State the theorem; sketch the structure. Full proof in book Section 17.4.

Slide T2-28.3 — Schauder Fixed-Point Argument (Detail)

Title: “Schauder applied to the equilibrium map”
The setup:

\(\mathcal{M}\) = closed, bounded, convex subset of \(\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R})\) (probability measures with finite second moment)
\(\Psi\) continuous (HJB and market clearing both stable in measure)
\(\Psi\) compact (uniform bounds on second moments)

The conclusion: \(\Psi\) has a fixed point in \(\mathcal{M}\)
Why Schauder, not Brouwer?

Brouwer requires finite dimension
\(\mathcal{M}\) is infinite-dimensional
Schauder is the appropriate generalization

Speaker notes: Tie to functional analysis. Most Track 2 students have seen Schauder in PDE classes.

Slide T2-28.4 — Market Clearing as Fixed Point

Title: “Market clearing: a fixed point on \(\lambda\)”
For given \(\mu\), \(\lambda^*\) satisfies:

Aggregate exit flow at premium \(\lambda^*\) = Aggregate buy flow at premium \(\lambda^*\)

Concrete form:

\(\sum_i [u^*_i(L_i, \mu, \lambda^*) \cdot \text{notional}_i] = 0\)

Where:

\(u^*_i = 1\) if LP \(i\) exits at premium \(\lambda^*\), else 0
\(\text{notional}_i\) = LP \(i\)’s position size

Iteration: Bisection on \(\lambda^*\) until aggregate flow ≈ 0
Speaker notes: Make the market clearing concrete. It’s just supply = demand at the right price.

Slide T2-28.5 — Connection to Numerical Algorithm

Title: “From proof to platform”
The existence proof guarantees the algorithm converges:

Start with guess \(\mu^{(0)}\)
Solve HJB → get \(u^{*(0)}\)
Solve FP → get \(\mu^{(1)}\)
Iterate: \(\mu^{(0)} \to \mu^{(1)} \to \mu^{(2)} \to ...\)

Under stability condition:

Iteration converges to the unique fixed point
GE-LAV platform implements this

Computational cost:

Typically 10-20 iterations
Total time: seconds to minutes

Speaker notes: The platform’s algorithm is based on the existence theorem. Theory → practice.



36.2.5 Track 2 Existing Slides to Reuse
From Class 17 (GE Market Clearing & Endogenous Pricing) — 14 slides: - Market clearing diagrams - Equilibrium existence sketches - Numerical examples of \(\lambda^*\) computation - Comparison with partial equilibrium
Use largely as-is.


36.2.6 Track 2 Discussion Questions

The existence proof requires that \(\Psi\) is continuous on \(\mathcal{M}\). What kind of discontinuity could break this? When would discontinuity be economically realistic (e.g., regulatory thresholds, IPS triggers)?
Under the stability condition \(\kappa > \gamma \cdot \text{Lip}(\phi)\), uniqueness holds. If the condition fails temporarily during a stress event but is restored as conditions normalize, what does the system look like during the failure period?
The market clearing condition treats sellers and buyers symmetrically. In real markets, intermediaries (brokers) take spread. How would the proof change if we added market makers?


← Session 27 | Schedule | Next: Session 29 →

title: 37  Session 29: Split Track — Digital Assets & Climate / Jensen + Pigouvian Proofs
text: 37.1 Track 1: Frontier Cases — Digital Assets + Climate
crumbs: 37  Session 29: Split Track — Digital Assets & Climate / Jensen + Pigouvian Proofs

title: 37  Session 29: Split Track — Digital Assets & Climate / Jensen + Pigouvian Proofs
text:
37.1.1 Track 1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, Track 1 students will be able to:

Apply GE-LAV to crypto-adjacent investments (token venture funds, defi protocols, NFT funds).
Adapt the calibration when secondary market data is thin (or doesn’t exist yet).
Apply GE-LAV to ultra-long-duration climate-exposed infrastructure (50-100 year assets).
Decompose climate risk into transition + physical risk channels.
Identify how GE-LAV’s stochastic-premium framework handles novel risks (climate transition, regulatory uncertainty).



37.1.2 Track 1 Pre-Class Assignment

Skim: Recent report on crypto fund returns or NFT secondaries (link on course site)
Skim: Climate-aware infrastructure investing report (Bloomberg NEF or similar)



37.1.3 Track 1 In-Class Outline (75 minutes)








Time
Segment
Format




0:00–0:15
Digital assets in private markets
Lecture


0:15–0:30
Applying GE-LAV when secondary data is thin
Lecture


0:30–0:50
Climate risk and ultra-long-duration assets
Lecture


0:50–1:05
Lab: value a climate-exposed infrastructure asset
Group work


1:05–1:15
Synthesis + research frontier discussion
Discussion





37.1.4 Track 1 Slide Briefs (New Slides Needed)
Slide T1-29.1 — Digital Assets in Private Markets

Title: “Crypto’s slow merger with private markets”
Categories:

Token venture funds: Like VC but invest in pre-launch protocols (Paradigm, A16Z Crypto)
Defi liquidity provision: Quasi-fund vehicles providing AMM liquidity
NFT funds: Collectibles as private market assets
Tokenized real-world assets: Real estate, art, private credit fractionalized on-chain

GE-LAV applicability:

Most categories have some secondary market data (varies by asset)
Calibration is harder (less history, more regime variability)
But: the structure of the framework applies — stochastic illiquidity premia, McKean-Vlasov coupling

Speaker notes: Establish category. Many students will work in or near crypto post-graduation.

Slide T1-29.2 — Calibration with Limited Data

Title: “What to do when you have 6 quarters, not 60”
Approaches:

Borrow calibration: Use similar-asset-class parameters as prior, adjust on local data
Bayesian methods: Treat OU parameters as random variables with priors from comparables
Robust valuation: Compute GE-LAV across multiple plausible parameter sets; report range

Cautionary note: “Calibration uncertainty is a real cost. Confidence intervals on \(\hat{\kappa}\) widen substantially with less data.”
Speaker notes: Be honest. Crypto/digital is a frontier; calibration is hard. Useful but not bulletproof.

Slide T1-29.3 — Climate Risk: Transition vs. Physical

Title: “Two kinds of climate risk in private markets”
Transition risk:

Regulatory changes (carbon pricing, EV mandates, building codes)
Asset stranding (fossil fuel infrastructure)
Affects: traditional infrastructure, energy, real estate

Physical risk:

Climate-driven asset damage (floods, wildfires, heat)
Operational disruption
Insurance cost increases
Affects: coastal real estate, agriculture, utilities

GE-LAV channel:

Both risks affect \(\bar{L}\) (long-run mean liquidity may shift)
Both affect \(\sigma_L\) (volatility may increase)
Long-duration assets (infrastructure) bear the highest climate exposure under GE-LAV

Speaker notes: Climate-aware investing is rapidly mainstreaming. GE-LAV is a natural framework.

Slide T1-29.4 — Ultra-Long-Duration Climate-Exposed Assets

Title: “50-100 year climate-exposed assets”
Examples:

Coastal port infrastructure (sea level rise + storm intensity)
Hydroelectric dams (precipitation patterns + drought)
Long-lived water infrastructure (drought + supply reliability)
Carbon sequestration facilities (regulatory + technological risk)

GE-LAV at these horizons:

Jensen bias is massive (\(B(50) \approx 50\%\)+ at calibrated parameters)
\(\bar{L}\) shift assumptions matter enormously
DCF systematically misvalues by 30-70% even in baseline scenarios

Speaker notes: Pension funds with ESG mandates increasingly hold these. Risk is under-measured.

Slide T1-29.5 — Lab Setup

Title: “Lab: a real climate-exposed infrastructure asset”
Choose one:

Port of Miami (coastal, 50-year horizon)
Hoover Dam (regulated, drought-exposed)
Tesla Gigafactory Nevada (regulatory + supply chain)
Indian solar farm (regulatory + climate variable)

Group output:

DCF baseline (likely misvalued)
GE-LAV under stable \(\bar{L}\)
GE-LAV under \(\bar{L}\)-shift scenario (mild + severe climate transition)
Recommendation for ESG-aware allocators

Speaker notes: Tangible. Connects to specific assets students will encounter.



37.1.5 Track 1 Existing Slides to Reuse
From Class 30 (Digital Assets) — 11 slides: - Crypto market structure overview - Calibration challenges for digital assets
From Class 31 (Climate Risk) — 12 slides: - Climate risk taxonomy - Long-duration asset analysis - Regulatory landscape for climate-aware investing
Use largely as-is.
crumbs: 37  Session 29: Split Track — Digital Assets & Climate / Jensen + Pigouvian Proofs

title: 37  Session 29: Split Track — Digital Assets & Climate / Jensen + Pigouvian Proofs
section: 37.2 Track 2: Jensen Bias + Pigouvian Tax + Welfare Derivations
text:
37.2 Track 2: Jensen Bias + Pigouvian Tax + Welfare Derivations

37.2.1 Track 2 Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, Track 2 students will be able to:

Prove Theorem 18.1 (Jensen convexity bias) — the closed-form formula derivation.
Prove Theorem 19.1 (constrained Pareto inefficiency) — that decentralized exit decisions are not socially optimal.
Derive the Pigouvian exit tax as the welfare-maximizing corrective instrument.
Quantify the welfare gap in the calibrated framework (~2.3%/yr).
Discuss the regulatory implementation considerations.



37.2.2 Track 2 Pre-Class Assignment

Read: Book Chapters 18, 19 in full (with proofs)
Pre-read: Mas-Colell, Whinston, Green, Microeconomic Theory, Chapters 11-13 (externalities and public goods)



37.2.3 Track 2 In-Class Outline (75 minutes)



Time
Segment
Format




0:00–0:10
Recap: the externality concept
Lecture


0:10–0:30
Jensen bias theorem and proof
Lecture + board


0:30–0:50
Welfare gap: quantifying the externality
Lecture


0:50–1:05
Pigouvian tax derivation
Lecture


1:05–1:15
Implementation considerations
Discussion





37.2.4 Track 2 Slide Briefs (New Slides Needed)
Slide T2-29.1 — Jensen Bias Theorem 18.1 (Statement)

Title: “Theorem 18.1: closed-form Jensen bias”
Theorem: For an asset with cash flows \(C(L, t)\) and OU dynamics with calibrated parameters, the Jensen convexity bias is:

\(B(T) = (\pi_2 / 2) \cdot \Pi_{liq}(T) \cdot \langle V^{DCF} \rangle\)

Where:

\(\Pi_{liq}(T) = \sigma_L^2 [T - (1 - e^{-2\kappa T})/(2\kappa)]\)
\(\pi_2\) = quadratic coefficient of premium function
\(\langle V^{DCF} \rangle\) = average DCF value

Result: \(B\) is positive (since \(\pi_2 > 0\)), increasing in \(T\), and computable from first principles
Speaker notes: State the theorem precisely. The proof follows.

Slide T2-29.2 — Jensen Bias Theorem 18.1 (Proof Sketch)

Title: “Proof of Theorem 18.1”
Step 1: Apply Itô’s lemma to \(\exp(-\int_0^t r(L_s) ds)\)
Step 2: Expand around the long-run mean \(\bar{L}\):

\(r(L) = r(\bar{L}) + r'(\bar{L})(L - \bar{L}) + \frac{1}{2}r''(\bar{L})(L - \bar{L})^2 + O((L - \bar{L})^3)\)

Step 3: Compute \(E[\int_0^t r(L_s) ds]\) using OU moments:

First moment vanishes by mean reversion
Second moment contributes the Jensen correction

Step 4: Use convexity of \(\exp\) and Jensen’s inequality
Step 5: Substitute and simplify; obtain the closed form
Speaker notes: Walk through key steps. Full proof in book Section 18.3 (~10 pages of details).

Slide T2-29.3 — Welfare Gap Theorem 19.1

Title: “Theorem 19.1: constrained Pareto inefficiency”
Statement: The decentralized equilibrium \((\mu^*_d, u^*_d)\) is Pareto-suboptimal:

Aggregate welfare \(W(\mu^*_d, u^*_d) < W(\mu^*_s, u^*_s)\)
Where \((\mu^*_s, u^*_s)\) is the centralized social planner solution

Welfare gap: \(\Delta W = W(\mu^*_s, u^*_s) - W(\mu^*_d, u^*_d) \approx 2.3\%/\text{yr}\) at calibrated parameters
Source of inefficiency: Each LP’s exit imposes externalities on others; private incentives don’t reflect this
Speaker notes: Set up the welfare comparison. The 2.3% number was discussed in Session 24; now we derive it.

Slide T2-29.4 — Welfare Gap Decomposition

Title: “Where the 2.3%/yr comes from”
Decomposition:

Path bias from individual Jensen (each LP overstates value): ~0.5%/yr
Coordination failure (forced sales widen everyone’s discounts): ~1.5%/yr
Misallocation (LPs hold sub-optimally given true equilibrium): ~0.3%/yr
Total: ~2.3%/yr aggregate welfare loss

Distribution:

Bigger losses in deep-stress regimes (most coordination failures)
Bigger losses for long-duration assets (more Jensen accumulation)
Bigger losses for less sophisticated LPs (slower to recognize)

Speaker notes: Quantitative breakdown. Useful for regulatory presentation.

Slide T2-29.5 — Pigouvian Tax Derivation

Title: “The Pigouvian exit tax: closed form”
Result (Theorem 19.2): The welfare-maximizing tax rate on secondary exits is:

\(\tau^*(L) = \gamma \cdot \text{Lip}(\phi) \cdot \frac{\partial W^*}{\partial \mu}|_{exit} \cdot \frac{1}{V_{exit}}\)

At calibrated parameters:




\(L\) State
\(\tau^*(L)\)




0
0.0%


-1
3.5%


-1.5
7.0%


-2.0
9.0%




Effect: With Pigouvian tax in place, decentralized equilibrium ≈ social optimum
Welfare improvement: ~2.3%/yr (closes the gap)
Speaker notes: Connect to Pigou (1920), modern carbon pricing analogies.



37.2.5 Track 2 Existing Slides to Reuse
From Class 11 (Jensen Bias Four-Channel Derivation) — 20 slides: - Full proof of the Jensen bias formula - Decomposition by channel - Numerical verification
From Class 18 (Welfare Analysis) — 13 slides: - Welfare gap quantification - Decentralized vs. centralized comparison
From Class 20 (Pigouvian Tax Design) — 15 slides: - Pigouvian tax derivation - Numerical examples - Implementation considerations
Substantial reuse.


37.2.6 Track 2 Discussion Questions

The Pigouvian tax is welfare-maximizing in theory. But it requires the regulator to know the population state \(\mu\) in real time. In practice, this is hard. What’s the welfare cost of using a constant tax rate (e.g., 3%) instead?
The 2.3%/yr welfare gap aggregates to ~$300B/year. Of this, what fraction would be recovered by an imperfect Pigouvian tax (say, only on transactions during designated stress windows)?
The Pareto inefficiency theorem assumes LPs are rational. Behavioral biases (loss aversion, herding) would likely amplify the externality. How would the welfare gap change with behavioral agents?

text: 38.1 Both Tracks: Project Draft Workshop (60 minutes)
crumbs: 38  Session 30: Project Draft Workshop · Valuation Hierarchy Proof (T2)

title: 38  Session 30: Project Draft Workshop · Valuation Hierarchy Proof (T2)
text:
38.1.1 Workshop Learning Objectives
By the end of this workshop, students will be able to:

Provide structured peer review on a classmate’s GE-LAV project draft.
Identify common weaknesses in their own draft based on peer feedback.
Plan revisions for the final paper and presentation.
Practice delivering the project’s “elevator pitch” — the IC summary in 2 minutes.



38.1.2 Workshop Structure (Both Tracks, Same Activity)








Time
Activity
Detail




0:00–0:05
Logistics: pair assignments, rubric distribution
Lecture


0:05–0:25
Reading time: read partner’s draft + take notes
Individual


0:25–0:50
Peer feedback session (12.5 min each direction)
Pair work


0:50–1:00
Wrap-up: list 3 priority revisions for your draft
Individual





38.1.3 Peer Review Rubric
Each student provides written feedback to their partner addressing:
1. Clarity (5 questions, 1-5 scale) - Is the asset/question stated precisely? - Is the methodology clear? - Are the calibrations documented? - Are the results easy to follow? - Is the recommendation defensible?
2. Rigor (5 questions) - Is the GE-LAV implementation correct? - Are the comparisons (DCF vs. LAV vs. GE-LAV) accurate? - Are sensitivity analyses adequate? - Is the data appropriate? - For Track 2: are mathematical derivations correct?
3. Communication (3 questions) - Could you explain this paper to a non-finance friend? Try in 2 sentences. - Is the IC-relevance clear? - Is the writing professional quality?
4. Improvement priorities (open) - “The single most important revision before the final paper would be…” - “The presentation should emphasize…” - “One thing that would delete if I had to cut to 12 pages…”


38.1.4 Workshop Slide Briefs (New Slides Needed)
Slide 30.1 — Workshop Introduction

Title: “Project Draft Workshop”
Goals: Identify weaknesses in your own work via someone else’s perspective
Norms:

Be specific, not vague (“the GE-LAV calculation is unclear at step 3” not “I’m confused”)
Be constructive, not destructive
Focus on the work, not the person
Both you and your partner are still learning

Speaker notes: Set the tone for peer review. Many students are uncomfortable giving direct feedback. Frame as professional courtesy.

Slide 30.2 — Peer Review Process

Title: “How the next hour works”
Time blocks:

20 min: Read your partner’s draft carefully (don’t skim)
25 min: Take turns giving and receiving feedback (12.5 min each)
10 min: Capture YOUR top 3 revision priorities

Speaker notes: Discipline the structure. Without time limits, peer review degrades into chat.

Slide 30.3 — Common Weaknesses to Watch For

Title: “Common weaknesses across project drafts”
For Track 1 (Practitioner) drafts:

Asset specification too vague
Calibration parameters unjustified
GE-LAV computation lacks regime variation
Recommendation doesn’t connect to the LAV/GE-LAV result

For Track 2 (Researcher) drafts:

Mathematical claims unproven or under-proven
Numerical implementation lacks convergence diagnostics
Connection to existing literature unclear
Extension proposal too ambitious for paper scope

Speaker notes: Make these visible. Both tracks share the same pitfalls in slightly different forms.

Slide 30.4 — What Goes in the Final Paper

Title: “Revising for the final submission”
Final paper requirements (Session 32):

20 pages excluding references and appendices
All revisions from peer feedback addressed
Specific improvements over draft (state them explicitly in a “Revisions” footnote)
Polished writing quality
Reproducible computations

What gets graded:

2% improvement over draft
2% writing quality
1% final correctness

Speaker notes: Set clear expectations. The final paper is not the draft with minor edits.
crumbs: 38  Session 30: Project Draft Workshop · Valuation Hierarchy Proof (T2)

title: 38  Session 30: Project Draft Workshop · Valuation Hierarchy Proof (T2)
section: 38.2 Track 2: Valuation Hierarchy Proof (15 additional minutes)
text:
38.2 Track 2: Valuation Hierarchy Proof (15 additional minutes)

38.2.1 Track 2 Learning Objectives
By the end of the Track 2 portion, students will additionally be able to:

State and prove Theorem 20.1 (strict valuation hierarchy DCF ⊊ LAV ⊊ GE-LAV).
Identify Theorem 20.2 (conditions for approximate equality among the hierarchies).
Compute the gap between hierarchy levels for arbitrary OU calibrations.
Discuss why the strict nesting matters for regulatory and pedagogical purposes.



38.2.2 Track 2 Slide Briefs
Slide T2-30.1 — Theorem 20.1: Strict Valuation Hierarchy

Title: “Theorem 20.1: DCF ⊊ LAV ⊊ GE-LAV”
Statement: Under positive liquidity volatility (\(\sigma_L > 0\)) and positive general-equilibrium coupling (\(\gamma > 0\)):

\(V^{DCF}(L, t) \neq V^{LAV}(L, t)\) (strict inequality somewhere on the state space)
\(V^{LAV}(L, t) \neq V^{GE-LAV}(L, t)\) (strict inequality)

In words: The three operators give genuinely different answers when liquidity is stochastic and the externality is present.

Slide T2-30.2 — Theorem 20.1 Proof Sketch

Title: “Proof: strict nesting”
Part 1 (DCF ≠ LAV):

Jensen’s inequality applied to \(e^{-r(L)T}\): \(E[e^{-r(L)T}] > e^{-E[r(L)]T}\)
Strict whenever \(L\) is non-degenerate (i.e., \(\sigma_L > 0\))

Part 2 (LAV ≠ GE-LAV):

GE-LAV adds the equilibrium uplift to \(r(L)\): \(r_{GE}(L, \mu) = r(L) + \text{externality}(L, \mu)\)
Strict whenever the externality is positive (i.e., \(\gamma > 0\))

Combination: Both inequalities strict → strict nesting holds.

Slide T2-30.3 — Theorem 20.2: Conditions for Equality

Title: “When the hierarchies converge”
Statement: \(V^{DCF} = V^{LAV} = V^{GE-LAV}\) in the limit:

As \(\sigma_L \to 0\): liquidity is deterministic, no path dependence
As \(\gamma \to 0\): no externality, partial equilibrium

Result: The framework reduces to DCF in the “no-uncertainty, no-coupling” limit.
Speaker notes: This is the consistency check. GE-LAV doesn’t conflict with DCF in DCF’s domain of validity.



38.2.3 Track 2 Discussion Questions

The strict nesting theorem requires \(\sigma_L > 0\) and \(\gamma > 0\). What if \(\sigma_L > 0\) but \(\gamma = 0\)? Is LAV strictly necessary then, or does DCF + simple Jensen suffice?
The proof of strict inequality relies on Jensen’s inequality applied pointwise. What if some pricing kernel is approximately linear (e.g., very short horizons)? Does the inequality “vanish” in the limit?
Theorem 20.2 (equality conditions) is the “consistency limit.” Is there a third limit where GE-LAV reduces to something other than DCF? Perhaps when the externality dominates?

39.1 Both Tracks: Course Synthesis (Common 30 Minutes)
Tracks meet together for the first 30 minutes for course synthesis, then split for track-specific frontier discussion.
crumbs: 39  Session 31: Research Frontiers · Course Synthesis

title: 39  Session 31: Research Frontiers · Course Synthesis
text:
39.1.1 Common Synthesis Slide Briefs
Slide 31.1 — Title

Title: “Research Frontiers and Course Synthesis”
Subtitle: “Where the framework goes next · What you’ve built”
Speaker notes: Final substantive session. Help students see the arc.

Slide 31.2 — What You’ve Built (Course Arc)

Title: “The 32-session arc, summarized”
Visual: Timeline showing all 32 sessions with milestone markers
Five units:

Unit 1 (Sessions 1–5): Why DCF fails ✓
Unit 2 (Sessions 6–10): Measurement + theory ✓
Unit 3 (Sessions 11–18): Decision + application ✓
Unit 4 (Sessions 19–24): Math intuition bridges ✓
Unit 5 (Sessions 25–31): Split track ✓

Capstone (Session 32): Project presentations
Speaker notes: Show progression. Students should feel pride.

Slide 31.3 — Key Takeaways for Both Tracks

Title: “What every student should walk away with”
Conceptual:

DCF systematically misvalues private market assets
Liquidity is stochastic and collectively determined
Jensen bias is real and computable
The McKean-Vlasov externality creates ~2.3%/yr welfare loss

Practical:

GE-LAV provides valuation, exit, portfolio, regulatory tools
Asset class differences matter (PE, VC, infrastructure, credit)
Cross-asset hedge demand is large in stress

Strategic:

The valuation hierarchy DCF ⊊ LAV ⊊ GE-LAV is provably strict
Adoption will happen gradually
Track 1 students will work with this framework; Track 2 students will extend it


Slide 31.4 — The Most Important Numbers

Title: “Numbers worth memorizing”
OU calibration: \(\kappa = 0.45\)/yr, \(\sigma_L = 0.32\), \(\bar{L} = 1.0\)
Jensen bias 5/10/15/20 yr: 0.8%/1.7%/2.5%/3.6% (affine approx) or 2.0%/6.2%/11.6%/15.0% (closed-form)
Crisis amplification: 4.31×
Welfare gap: ~2.3%/yr
Hedge demand at normal: ~8 percentage points
Speaker notes: Concrete numbers. Students should know these on exit.

Cross-asset coupling needs more empirical work

Compound Poisson jumps in \(L_t\) (Lévy OU)
Book Ch. 13 sketches; full theory still developing

Multi-asset GE-LAV:

Coupled mean-field equilibria across asset classes
Open: existence theorem for the multi-asset system

Neural SDE calibration:

Data-driven parameter estimation using neural networks
Class 29 covers; more research needed

Speaker notes: Each of these is a thesis chapter or research paper opportunity.

Slide T2-31.2 — Open Research Questions

Title: “What’s not yet known”
Quantitative open questions:

Stability boundaries (when does \(\kappa > \gamma\) fail in practice?)
Rate of convergence for finite-N MFG
Robust calibration under regime uncertainty

Methodological open questions:

When to use master equation vs. simulations
High-dimensional generalizations

Empirical open questions:

Test the Pigouvian tax welfare gap empirically
Cross-validate calibration on subsamples


Slide T2-31.3 — Connections to Adjacent Fields

Systemic risk literature:

GE-LAV provides an asset-pricing companion to Acemoglu et al. network models

Optimal execution:

Similar fixed-point structure (impact depends on flow, flow depends on impact)
Adapt MFG techniques across both areas

Central clearing literature:

GE-LAV-style Pigouvian instruments analog to default fund / margin requirements

Climate finance:

Long-duration risk pricing; transition risk dynamics

Thesis topics in MFG, private market valuation, systemic risk
Connection to leading research (Carmona, Delarue, Lasry, Lions)

Theoretical papers on extensions

Calibration techniques

title: 40  Session 32: Capstone — Project Presentations
text:
40.4 Q&A Guidelines

40.4.1 For Presenters

Anticipate questions: Re-read your work; what would you ask if you were the instructor?
Be honest about limitations: Saying “I assumed X; the result would change if Y” beats pretending you handled everything
Defend with reasoning, not certainty: “My approach was Z because of A and B” is better than “Z is the obvious choice”
Stay calm: Q&A is meant to test understanding, not to embarrass



40.4.2 Common Questions (Examples)

“Why did you choose this specific asset?” → Should align with your motivation slide
“How robust is your calibration to parameter variation?” → Should match your sensitivity analysis
“Did you consider the McKean-Vlasov externality?” → Critical for stress scenarios
“What’s the IC-relevant magnitude here?” → Numbers in dollars, not percentages
“Why does GE-LAV differ from DCF in this case?” → Channel-specific decomposition expected
“Would Track [other] students have approached this differently?” → Cross-track perspective

title: 40  Session 32: Capstone — Project Presentations
text:
40.6 A Final Word
The GE-LAV framework introduced in this course is new. The book was published in 2026; the framework has only been in the public discourse for a year. You — as students in the first iterations of this course — are among the earliest to systematically understand and apply this material.
That means you carry forward responsibility:

For Track 1 students: bring intellectual honesty to GE-LAV adoption. Help institutions use it well.
For Track 2 students: extend the framework. Test it. Critique it. Make it stronger.

If you find errors, omissions, or improvements in the course or the framework, please share them. The course will be revised; the framework will evolve. Your contribution shapes both.
Thank you for the semester.
